questions.question: What is EtherChannel (also called Port Channel or LAG)?
options: A protocol that prevents switching loops
A technology that bundles multiple physical links between switches into one logical link
A method for creating VLANs on a switch
questions.explanation: EtherChannel bundles 2–8 physical Ethernet links into a single logical link, increasing bandwidth and providing redundancy. From STP's perspective, it appears as a single link (preventing STP from blocking redundant physical links). Negotiated via LACP (802.3ad) or PAgP (Cisco proprietary).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 23: EtherChannel; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: In Spanning Tree Protocol, what is a Bridge Protocol Data Unit (BPDU)?
options: A frame used to transport VoIP traffic
A special frame switches use to exchange STP information and elect the Root Bridge
A management packet used by CDP
questions.explanation: BPDUs are special frames that STP-enabled switches send to each other to share topology information. BPDUs are used to elect the Root Bridge (lowest Bridge ID wins), determine port roles (Root Port, Designated Port, Blocked Port), and detect topology changes. Sent every 2 seconds by default.
questions.source_ref: CCNA_Cramsession.htm STP section; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 20: STP

questions.question: In STP, how is the Root Bridge selected?
options: The switch with the highest Bridge ID (highest MAC address) becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the lowest Bridge ID (priority + MAC) becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the most ports becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the highest IP address becomes Root Bridge
questions.explanation: The Root Bridge is the switch with the LOWEST Bridge ID. Bridge ID = Bridge Priority (default 32768, configurable in multiples of 4096) + MAC address. To make a switch the Root Bridge, lower its priority using 'spanning-tree vlan {id} priority {value}' or 'spanning-tree vlan {id} root primary'.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 20: STP; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What is the well-known UDP port number used by TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol)?
questions.explanation: TFTP uses UDP port 69. Other key ports: FTP data=20, FTP control=21, Telnet=23, SMTP=25, DNS=53, BootP server=67, BootP client=68, TFTP=69, HTTP=80, SNMP=161. TFTP/SNMP/DNS/NFS use UDP; Telnet/FTP/HTTP use TCP.

questions.question: What is the standard Cisco encapsulation method for IP packets on Ethernet interfaces?
questions.explanation: ARPA (also called Ethernet_II) is the default encapsulation for IP on Cisco Ethernet interfaces. SNAP is the default for Token Ring. NOVELL-ETHER is for IPX on Ethernet. SAP is Ethernet_802.2 LLC. The 'show interfaces' output shows 'Encapsulation ARPA'.

questions.question: What is the correct IOS command to configure an IP address on a router interface (from the Router(config-if)# prompt)?
questions.explanation: The correct command is 'ip address {IP address} {subnet mask}' under interface configuration mode. The mask is required. Shorthand in CIDR notation (/24) is also accepted in modern IOS. Always use 'no shutdown' after to bring the interface up.

questions.question: Which commands display the ARP cache on a Cisco router? (Choose two that apply)
questions.explanation: Both 'show ip arp' and 'show arp' display the ARP cache, which maps IP addresses to MAC addresses. The ARP table shows: Protocol, Address, Age, Hardware Address, Type, and Interface. To clear it, use 'clear arp-cache'.

questions.question: RARP (Reverse Address Resolution Protocol) is used for which purpose?
options: Resolving an IP address to a MAC address
Resolving a destination IP address by sending a broadcast
Resolving an IP address when only the MAC address is known (used by diskless workstations)
Mapping a hostname to an IP address
questions.explanation: RARP allows a host to discover its own IP address when it only knows its MAC address. A diskless workstation broadcasts its MAC address, and a RARP server responds with an assigned IP. ARP does the opposite: maps IP to MAC. RARP is largely replaced by DHCP today.

questions.question: Which of the following applications use TCP (connection-oriented transport)? Choose two.
questions.explanation: Telnet (port 23) and FTP (ports 20/21) use TCP because they require reliable, ordered delivery. TFTP (port 69) and SNMP (port 161) use UDP because they prioritize speed over reliability. DNS (port 53) uses both TCP and UDP.

questions.question: Which of the following are link-state routing protocols? Choose two.
questions.explanation: OSPF and IS-IS are link-state routing protocols. They advertise the state of links to all routers in the area when changes occur, building a topological database and using the SPF (Dijkstra's) algorithm. RIP is distance vector (sends full routing table periodically). EIGRP is advanced distance vector (hybrid).

questions.question: Which command clears the IP ARP cache on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: The command 'clear arp-cache' clears all dynamic ARP entries from the router's ARP table. This is useful after changing an IP address or NIC card on a host to force the router to re-learn the new MAC-to-IP mapping.

questions.question: When do collisions occur in an Ethernet network?
options: When multiple packets are placed on a serial link
When multiple stations listen for traffic and transmit at the same time
When the network is segmented by bridges
When a router forwards a broadcast
questions.explanation: Collisions occur in Ethernet (CSMA/CD) when two or more stations sense no traffic and begin transmitting simultaneously. The first station to detect the collision sends a jamming signal, then all stations back off for a random timer before retransmitting. Collisions do NOT occur on serial links (point-to-point) or Token Ring (token passing).

questions.question: How many usable subnets does a Class C network have with subnet mask 255.255.255.252?
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.252 borrows 6 bits from the host portion (252 = 11111100 in binary). Subnets = 2^6 - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62. Each subnet has only 2 usable hosts (2 host bits, 2^2 - 2 = 2). This is commonly used for point-to-point serial links.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about IP access control lists are TRUE? Choose all that apply.
options: ACLs should have at least one permit statement
Every ACL has an implicit deny-all at the end
ACLs are processed top-down, first match wins
questions.explanation: All three statements are true: (1) Every ACL needs at least one permit, otherwise the implicit deny blocks all traffic. (2) There is an implicit 'deny any' at the end of every ACL. (3) ACLs are processed top-to-bottom; as soon as a match is found, that action is taken and no further entries are checked.

questions.question: An interface has the 'ip access-group 101 in' command applied, but ACL 101 has not been created. What happens to traffic?
options: An error message is displayed and the command is rejected
All traffic is denied in and out
All traffic is permitted in and out
All traffic is denied inbound only
questions.explanation: If 'ip access-group' is applied but the ACL doesn't exist, all traffic is PERMITTED. Think of it as a security guard that hasn't arrived at the gate yet — no guard means no filtering. Only when an ACL exists AND is applied does the implicit deny take effect.

questions.question: What does the 'established' keyword do in an Extended ACL entry like 'access-list 101 permit tcp any 172.16.0.0 0.0.255.255 established'?
options: Permits all TCP traffic regardless of source
Only permits TCP packets that are RETURN traffic (ACK/RST bit set), blocking new connections from outside
Permits only the first SYN packet of a TCP session
questions.explanation: The 'established' keyword permits TCP packets that have the ACK or RST bit set — meaning they are part of an already-established session (return traffic). New TCP connections (initial SYN only, no ACK) are blocked. This allows internal hosts to initiate outbound connections while preventing unsolicited inbound connections.

questions.question: Where should Standard and Extended IP ACLs be placed for best practice?
options: Standard: close to source; Extended: close to destination
Standard: close to destination; Extended: close to source
Both should be placed close to the source
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs filter on SOURCE only — placing them close to the source could accidentally block traffic to other destinations. Place STANDARD ACLs close to the DESTINATION. Extended ACLs filter on source AND destination — they can be precise, so place EXTENDED ACLs close to the SOURCE to avoid unnecessary traffic traversal.

questions.question: What is the correct IOS command to set a DCE clock rate of 56 Kbps on a serial interface?
questions.explanation: 'clock rate 56000' (two words, in bps) is the correct syntax, applied under interface configuration mode (config-if). This command is only valid on DCE serial interfaces (the end of the cable connected to the CSU/DSU simulated device). DTE interfaces do not set clock rate.

questions.question: What is the correct IOS command to set the bandwidth of a serial interface to 56 Kbps?
questions.explanation: The 'bandwidth' command takes a value in kilobits (kbps). So for 56 Kbps, use 'bandwidth 56'. For T1 (1.544 Mbps), use 'bandwidth 1544'. This is a logical setting used by routing protocols (like OSPF) for metric calculation — it does NOT change the actual interface speed.

questions.question: The 'line console 0' command can be used to:
options: Establish a Telnet session with a remote router
Set a login password on the console terminal
Set a password to restrict access to privileged EXEC mode
Set a password for incoming VTY (Telnet) sessions
questions.explanation: 'line console 0' enters console line configuration mode. From here you can set: 'password {password}' and 'login' to require a password on direct console access. For Telnet/SSH access use 'line vty 0 4'. For privileged mode use 'enable password' or 'enable secret'.

questions.question: What is a hold-down timer in distance vector routing, and when is it released?
options: A timer that delays route updates; released when the interface comes back up
A timer that prevents accepting worse routes after a route goes down; released only when a better route is received
A timer that controls how often routing updates are sent
A timer that limits the maximum hop count
questions.explanation: A hold-down timer is started when a route is marked as possibly down. During hold-down: the router WILL accept updates with a BETTER (lower metric) route. The router will NOT accept updates with a WORSE route. Administrators can configure the hold-down time. Hold-down timers + triggered updates + split horizon are loop prevention mechanisms in distance vector protocols.

questions.question: On an IP over Ethernet network, when Host A sends data to Host B, what type of address is used for actual frame delivery?
questions.explanation: At the Data Link layer, actual frame delivery uses physical (MAC) addresses. Even though IP addresses are used for routing between networks, within a single network segment the Ethernet frame must carry source and destination MAC addresses. ARP resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses for this purpose.

questions.question: What does ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) do?
options: Maps a MAC address to an IP address
Maps an IP address to a MAC address
Maps a hostname to an IP address
Maps a MAC address to a port number
questions.explanation: ARP maps a known IP address to an unknown MAC address. When Host A needs to send a frame to Host B (whose IP is known from routing), it broadcasts an ARP request asking 'Who has IP x.x.x.x?' Host B replies with its MAC address. The mapping is cached in the ARP table.

questions.question: Which of the following are benefits of segmenting a network with ROUTERS? Choose three.
options: Controls broadcasts (each router interface is its own broadcast domain)
questions.explanation: Routers provide: (1) increased per-node bandwidth by reducing collision domains, (2) broadcast control — routers do not forward broadcasts between interfaces, limiting broadcast storms, (3) reduced collisions — each router port is its own collision domain. Routers do NOT increase frame size.

questions.question: What are the primary benefits of IP subnetting?
options: Conserves IP addresses and creates a hierarchical addressing scheme
Eliminates the need for routing protocols
Reduces the number of routers required
questions.explanation: IP subnetting provides two main benefits: (1) Conservation of IP address space by dividing large networks into smaller subnets, and (2) Creation of a hierarchical addressing structure which enables efficient routing and summarization (CIDR).

questions.question: In Frame Relay, BECN and FECN bits are used for:
options: Providing keepalives to maintain PVC status
Error checking in the Frame Relay CRC
Notifying DTEs about congestion in the Frame Relay network
questions.explanation: BECN (Backward Explicit Congestion Notification) and FECN (Forward Explicit Congestion Notification) are congestion notification bits in Frame Relay frames. FECN is set in frames traveling toward the destination when congestion is encountered. BECN is set in frames traveling back toward the source to signal it to slow down.

questions.question: Which of the following is a ROUTED protocol (carries user data), NOT a routing protocol?
questions.explanation: ROUTED protocols carry user data through the network (IP, IPX, AppleTalk). ROUTING protocols determine the best path for routed protocols to travel (OSPF, RIP, EIGRP, IGRP, IS-IS, BGP). IP is a routed protocol — it defines packet addressing and routing. OSPF, RIP, and EIGRP are all routing protocols.

questions.question: Which statement is NOT true about link-state routing protocols (like OSPF)?
options: LSAs are flooded to all routers in the OSPF area
Each router runs the SPF (Dijkstra's) algorithm
LSAs include the originating router's full routing table
questions.explanation: Link-state protocols (OSPF, IS-IS) do NOT share routing tables. Instead, they share Link-State Advertisements (LSAs) describing their directly connected links. Each router independently builds a topological map and runs SPF to compute best paths. Distance vector protocols (RIP) share routing tables periodically.

questions.question: Which of the following would NOT appear in a router's routing table?
questions.explanation: Routing tables contain: destination network/mask, next-hop IP address, outgoing interface, administrative distance, and metric. MAC addresses are Layer 2 information found in a switch's MAC address table (CAM table) or ARP cache — NOT in the routing table. Routers operate at Layer 3.

questions.question: Given IP address 193.243.12.43 and subnet mask 255.255.255.128, what is the subnet address?
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.128 = /25 — the last octet divides at bit 128. Host octet is 43. 43 < 128, so this is in the zero subnet (193.243.12.0/25). Subnet boundaries: 193.243.12.0 (network) through 193.243.12.127 (broadcast). The host 193.243.12.43 falls in this range. Note: The answer 'none of the above' from the original archive was misleading; 193.243.12.0 is correct.

questions.question: What does the 'show version' command display on a Cisco router?
options: System hardware, IOS software version, boot images, and configuration register
questions.explanation: 'show version' displays: router model/hardware, IOS version, boot image source (flash/TFTP/ROM), uptime, RAM/Flash/NVRAM sizes, and the configuration register value (e.g., 0x2102 = normal boot from flash). It does NOT show the running configuration — use 'show running-config' for that.

questions.question: Which IOS command verifies connectivity at the Network layer by using ICMP Echo/Reply messages?
questions.explanation: Ping uses ICMP Echo Request and Echo Reply to verify Layer 3 (Network) connectivity and logical address reachability. Telnet verifies application-layer connectivity (Layer 7). Traceroute uses TTL values to identify each hop along the path. 'show ip route' displays the routing table without sending any packets.

questions.question: What does the IOS context-sensitive help system display if you type 'show ?' at the router prompt?
options: An error — '?' is not a valid character
A list of all commands starting with 'show'
questions.explanation: Typing '?' after a partial command (like 'show ?') displays a list of all valid completions for that partial command. Typing '?' with no preceding text shows all available commands at that prompt level. Note: 'sh ar?' (no space) shows commands that COMPLETE 'ar', while 'sh ar ?' (space) shows arguments for the 'ar' command.

questions.question: At which OSI layer does ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) operate?
questions.explanation: ARP bridges Layer 2 (Data Link - MAC addresses) and Layer 3 (Network - IP addresses). It is considered a Layer 2 protocol in operation but uses Layer 3 addresses. ARP sends a Layer 2 broadcast containing a Layer 3 IP address to discover the MAC address of a host.

questions.question: What is a triggered update in distance vector routing protocols?
options: An update sent at fixed intervals regardless of changes
An update immediately sent to neighbors when a route change occurs
An update that only occurs when hold-down timers expire
An update that increases the routing metric for slow links
questions.explanation: A triggered update is sent immediately when a router detects a topology change (e.g., an interface goes down), without waiting for the regular update interval. Combined with hold-down timers, triggered updates help prevent routing loops. Regular periodic updates still occur even when there are no changes.

questions.question: Which OSI layer sends and receives binary (bit-level) information over physical media?
questions.explanation: The Physical Layer (Layer 1) transmits and receives raw bits as electrical, optical, or radio signals. It defines voltage levels, timing, cable specifications, and connectors. It does not understand frames or packets — it simply moves 1s and 0s.

questions.question: Which OSI layer provides end-to-end (source-to-destination) connections between hosts?
questions.explanation: The Transport Layer (Layer 4) provides end-to-end logical communication between hosts. TCP provides reliable, connection-oriented service; UDP provides unreliable, connectionless service. The Network Layer routes between networks but doesn't provide end-to-end reliability.

questions.question: Which OSI layer controls access to the physical media (defines when devices can transmit)?
questions.explanation: The Data Link Layer (Layer 2), specifically the MAC (Media Access Control) sublayer, controls access to the physical medium. CSMA/CD (Ethernet) and Token Passing are MAC-layer mechanisms. The Physical Layer just converts bits to signals; it doesn't control who transmits when.

questions.question: Which OSI layer defines network addressing and determines the best path through the network?
questions.explanation: The Network Layer (Layer 3) uses logical addressing (IP addresses) to route packets through the internetwork. Routing protocols (OSPF, RIP, EIGRP) and IP itself operate here. Routers are Layer 3 devices.

questions.question: Which of the following are features of a connection-oriented transport service (TCP)? Choose all that apply.
questions.explanation: TCP (connection-oriented) provides: three-way handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK) to establish connections, flow control (windowing), error detection (checksum), error recovery (retransmission), and sequencing. UDP (connectionless) provides none of these — it just sends datagrams.

questions.question: In a TCP connection, when Host A receives a segment with SEQ number 100, what ACK value does Host A send back?
options: The ACK number from the previous exchange
questions.explanation: TCP uses 'expectational acknowledgements' — the ACK number means 'I received up to X, now send me X+1.' If you receive SEQ=100 (for a 1-byte packet), you ACK with 101, meaning 'I got 100, send 101 next.' This confirms receipt and requests the next byte.

questions.question: What UDP port number does SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) use?
questions.explanation: SNMP uses UDP port 161 for queries/gets (SNMP manager queries SNMP agent) and UDP port 162 for traps (SNMP agent sends unsolicited alerts to manager). SNMP uses UDP because management queries are simple, and lost queries are retried by the management system.

questions.question: In the TCP three-way handshake, the FIRST SYN packet has an ACK value of:
questions.explanation: The initial SYN packet has ACK = 0 because no data has been received yet to acknowledge. The sequence is: (1) Client → SYN (SEQ=x, ACK=0), (2) Server → SYN-ACK (SEQ=y, ACK=x+1), (3) Client → ACK (SEQ=x+1, ACK=y+1). The 'established' ACL keyword blocks packets with ACK=0 (initial SYN).

questions.question: A serial interface shows 'Serial0 is down, line protocol is up'. What does this mean?
options: The interface is disconnected but keepalives are being received
The interface works but keepalives are not received
The interface is in loopback mode
questions.explanation: The combination 'interface down, protocol up' is NOT possible. Valid states: (1) Up/Up = fully working, (2) Administratively Down/Down = 'shutdown' configured, (3) Down/Down = no carrier detect (physical problem, call the carrier), (4) Up/Down = interface OK but no keepalives (bad CSU/DSU), (5) Up/Down(looped) = CSU/DSU in loopback mode.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the TTL (Time To Live) field in an IP packet?
options: To measure the transit time for the packet
To prevent IP packets caught in routing loops from traversing the network forever
To set the packet's priority for QoS
questions.explanation: TTL is a counter decremented by 1 at each router hop. When TTL reaches 0, the router discards the packet and sends an ICMP 'Time Exceeded' message back to the source. This prevents packets from circulating endlessly in routing loops. Default TTL is typically 64 or 128 (OS-dependent), maximum is 255.

questions.question: What subnet mask would you use for a Class C network to support exactly 62 hosts per subnet?
questions.explanation: You need at least 62 hosts per subnet. 2^n - 2 ≥ 62. Try n=6: 2^6 - 2 = 62 ✓. Six host bits remain, so 2 bits are used for subnetting. Last octet mask = 11000000 = 192. Full mask: 255.255.255.192. Each subnet gives 2^6 - 2 = 62 usable hosts and 2^2 - 2 = 2 usable subnets (or 4 with ip subnet-zero).

questions.question: When you enter a configuration command on a Cisco router, when does it take effect?
questions.explanation: Configuration commands on Cisco IOS take effect IMMEDIATELY when entered — they go straight into the running-config in RAM. However, they are not persistent until saved to NVRAM with 'copy running-config startup-config'. If the router reboots without saving, the changes are lost.

questions.question: What is the purpose of NTP (Network Time Protocol)?
options: To translate domain names to IP addresses
To synchronize clocks on network devices to a common time source
To manage IP address assignments dynamically
questions.explanation: NTP synchronizes the clocks of network devices to a reference time source (NTP server). Accurate time is critical for log correlation, security certificates, scheduled events, and troubleshooting. NTP uses UDP port 123. Cisco devices: 'ntp server {IP}' configures NTP client; 'ntp master {stratum}' makes a router an NTP server.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 37: NTP; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What are the three logging severity levels used by Syslog on Cisco routers, from most to least severe?
options: Emergency(0), Alert(1), Critical(2) ... down to Debug(7)
questions.explanation: Syslog has 8 severity levels (0-7): 0=Emergency, 1=Alert, 2=Critical, 3=Error, 4=Warning, 5=Notice, 6=Informational, 7=Debug. Lower number = more severe. IOS: 'logging {ip}' sends to syslog server; 'logging trap {level}' sets minimum severity; 'logging buffered {level}' stores in RAM buffer. Mnemonic: Every Awful Cisco Engineer Wants Nice Delicious Pizza.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 41: Syslog; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What is SNMP used for in network management?
options: Monitoring and managing network devices by reading/writing MIB variables
Encrypting management traffic to network devices
questions.explanation: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) allows an NMS (Network Management System) to monitor and configure network devices by reading/writing variables in the MIB (Management Information Base). SNMP Get/GetNext read data; SNMP Set writes config; SNMP Trap sends unsolicited alerts. SNMPv3 adds authentication and encryption.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 40: SNMP; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What is a DHCP relay agent (ip helper-address) used for?
options: To run multiple DHCP servers on the same subnet
To forward DHCP broadcast requests from clients to a DHCP server on a different subnet
To assign static IP addresses to clients
To convert DHCP to BOOTP format
questions.explanation: DHCP clients send broadcast requests (destination 255.255.255.255). Routers don't forward broadcasts between subnets. The 'ip helper-address {DHCP server IP}' command on the router interface tells the router to forward DHCP broadcasts as unicasts to the DHCP server, enabling a single DHCP server to serve multiple subnets.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 38: DHCP; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: DNS (Domain Name System) uses which transport protocol and port?
options: UDP port 53 (also TCP port 53 for zone transfers)
questions.explanation: DNS primarily uses UDP port 53 for standard queries (speed is important). DNS also uses TCP port 53 for zone transfers (large data between DNS servers) and when responses exceed 512 bytes. DNS resolves hostnames to IP addresses and vice versa.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep 98-08-1-19 Q1 port list; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What does 'ip name-server' configure on a Cisco router?
options: The DNS server that the router uses for hostname resolution
questions.explanation: 'ip name-server {IP address}' configures one or more DNS servers for the router to use when resolving hostnames. Also requires 'ip domain-lookup' (enabled by default). Without this, the router will try to resolve any unrecognized commands as hostnames (causing delays). 'no ip domain-lookup' disables this behavior.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 36: DNS; CCNA lab exercises

questions.question: What is the difference between Puppet and Ansible in terms of architecture?
options: Both are agent-based; Puppet uses Ruby, Ansible uses Python
Puppet uses an agent on managed devices (pull model); Ansible is agentless (push model via SSH)
Both are agentless; Puppet uses YAML, Ansible uses XML
Puppet uses SSH; Ansible uses NETCONF
questions.explanation: Puppet uses an AGENT installed on managed devices that periodically PULLS configuration from the Puppet master (pull model, Ruby-based). Ansible is AGENTLESS — it pushes configuration to devices via SSH (Linux) or WinRM (Windows) using YAML playbooks (push model, Python-based). Chef is also agent-based (pull model, Ruby).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 57: Ansible, Puppet, Chef; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: In software-defined networking (SDN), what is the role of southbound APIs?
options: To communicate with network operators and management systems (northbound)
To communicate between the SDN controller and network devices (data plane)
To authenticate users to the SDN controller
To translate between IPv4 and IPv6
questions.explanation: Southbound APIs (like OpenFlow, NETCONF, RESTCONF, gRPC) are used for communication between the SDN controller (control plane) and the network devices (data plane). Northbound APIs (REST, YANG) are used for communication between the controller and management applications/operators.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 54: SDN; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What data serialization format does RESTCONF use for transmitting configuration data?
questions.explanation: RESTCONF uses HTTP/HTTPS as transport and supports both XML and JSON for data encoding. It is built on top of YANG data models and uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE). NETCONF uses XML over SSH (port 830). RESTCONF runs over HTTPS (port 443).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 57: NETCONF/RESTCONF; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What is the purpose of YANG (Yet Another Next Generation) in network automation?
options: A scripting language for writing Ansible playbooks
A data modeling language that defines the structure of configuration and state data exchanged by NETCONF/RESTCONF
A GUI tool for SDN controller configuration
questions.explanation: YANG is a data modeling language used to describe the structure, syntax, and semantics of network configuration and state data. YANG models are used by NETCONF and RESTCONF — they define what data can be configured (like a schema for network devices). Cisco IOS-XE, IOS-XR, and NX-OS all support YANG models.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 57: YANG; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: What is the subnet address for host 172.16.35.123/20?
questions.explanation: /20 mask = 255.255.240.0. Third octet subnet blocks: every 16 (256-240=16). 35 ÷ 16 = 2 remainder 3. Subnet starts at 2×16 = 32. So subnet = 172.16.32.0/20. Host range: 172.16.32.1 – 172.16.47.254. Broadcast: 172.16.47.255. Host 35.123 falls in this range ✓.
questions.source_ref: CCNA subnetting practice; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 14-15

questions.question: A /30 subnet is most commonly used for which type of connection?
options: Large enterprise LAN with hundreds of hosts
Point-to-point serial links between routers (only 2 hosts needed)
questions.explanation: /30 = 255.255.255.252. Host bits = 2. Usable hosts = 2^2 - 2 = 2. Perfect for point-to-point links connecting two routers — one IP per router end. Using a /30 conserves IP address space compared to assigning a /24 to a 2-device link. Alternatively, /31 or unnumbered interfaces can also be used for P2P links.

questions.question: Which of the following are private IP address ranges (RFC 1918)?
questions.explanation: RFC 1918 defines three private address ranges not routable on the Internet: 10.0.0.0/8 (Class A), 172.16.0.0/12 (Class B, range 172.16.0.0–172.31.255.255), and 192.168.0.0/16 (Class C). These require NAT to communicate with public Internet hosts. 127.0.0.0/8 is loopback; 169.254.0.0/16 is APIPA (link-local).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 44: NAT/Private IPs; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What is the broadcast address for the network 192.168.10.0/24?
questions.explanation: For 192.168.10.0/24: all host bits set to 1 = 192.168.10.255 (broadcast). Network address = 192.168.10.0 (all host bits 0). First usable host = 192.168.10.1. Last usable host = 192.168.10.254. Total usable hosts = 2^8 - 2 = 254.

questions.question: What is the maximum hop count (metric) for RIP (Routing Information Protocol)?
questions.explanation: RIP uses hop count as its metric (each router = 1 hop). Maximum hop count is 15 — a hop count of 16 means 'unreachable/infinity.' This limits RIP to networks with diameter ≤ 15 routers. RIP v1 sends classful updates every 30 seconds; RIP v2 adds VLSM support and authentication.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 24: RIP; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What is the EIGRP metric based on?
options: Bandwidth and delay (primarily); load, reliability, and MTU can also be used
Cost based on 100 Mbps / link bandwidth
questions.explanation: EIGRP uses a composite metric primarily based on bandwidth (of the slowest link) and delay (cumulative delay of all links). By default, load, reliability, and MTU are not included in the metric calculation (K values: K1=1, K2=0, K3=1, K4=0, K5=0). OSPF uses cost = 100Mbps / bandwidth.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 33: EIGRP; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What is the difference between 'enable password' and 'enable secret' on Cisco routers?
options: No difference — both encrypt the password the same way
'enable secret' uses MD5 hash (one-way, secure); 'enable password' is stored in cleartext
'enable secret' is only for Telnet; 'enable password' is for console
'enable password' uses SHA-256; 'enable secret' uses MD5
questions.explanation: 'enable secret' stores the password as an MD5 hash (one-way, Type 5) — it cannot be reversed. 'enable password' stores in cleartext (visible with 'show running-config') unless 'service password-encryption' is applied (Type 7, which is weak/reversible). 'enable secret' always takes precedence over 'enable password' if both are configured.
questions.source_ref: CCNA_CramGuide.htm section 21; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 5

questions.question: What does 'service password-encryption' do on a Cisco router?
options: Encrypts all passwords using MD5 (one-way hash)
Applies Type 7 (Vigenere cipher) weak encryption to all plaintext passwords in the config
Enables SSH encryption for all Telnet sessions
questions.explanation: 'service password-encryption' applies Cisco's proprietary Type 7 (reversible Vigenere cipher) encryption to all plaintext passwords in the running config (console, vty, enable password). This prevents casual shoulder-surfing but is NOT truly secure — Type 7 is easily decoded. Use 'enable secret' (Type 5 MD5) for privileged mode security.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 5: Password Security; Wendell Odom OCG

questions.question: What is 802.1X used for in network security?
options: Port-based Network Access Control — authenticating devices before granting network access
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1X provides port-based Network Access Control (PNAC). When a device connects to a switch port, 802.1X requires authentication via EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol) before the port is opened. It uses a Supplicant (client), Authenticator (switch), and Authentication Server (RADIUS). Commonly used in enterprise Wi-Fi and wired networks.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 50: 802.1X; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2

questions.question: Which Data Link sublayer gives upper layers independence from the LAN media access method?
questions.explanation: The LLC (Logical Link Control) sublayer sits above MAC and provides a common interface to upper layers, shielding them from the specifics of the underlying LAN technology (Ethernet, Token Ring, etc.).

questions.question: A MAC address is 48 bits long and composed of two parts. What are those two parts?
questions.explanation: The first 24 bits are the Organizationally Unique Identifier (OUI) assigned by IEEE to the vendor. The last 24 bits are a serial number assigned by the vendor to make each card unique. Example: Cisco OUI = 00:00:0C.

questions.question: What is the major difference between an Ethernet II frame and an IEEE 802.3 frame?
options: 802.3 uses a two-byte Length field where Ethernet II uses a Type field
Ethernet II uses a two-byte sequence number where 802.3 uses a Type field
Ethernet II uses a SNAP header to describe type
questions.explanation: Ethernet II (DIX Ethernet) uses a 2-byte Type field to indicate the upper-layer protocol. IEEE 802.3 replaced this with a 2-byte Length field; type information is carried in the 802.2 LLC header (SSAP/DSAP) or SNAP extension.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 802.3 SNAP (Subnet Access Protocol) format?
options: To identify the length of the frame
questions.explanation: SNAP is an extension of the 802.2 LLC header (SSAP=AA, DSAP=AA, Control=03) that includes a 5-byte SNAP header: 3-byte OUI + 2-byte protocol type. This allows identification of proprietary protocols (e.g., Cisco CDP) within 802.3 frames.

questions.question: What does the TCP Window Size field establish?
options: The maximum number of bytes in a single frame
The allowable bandwidth for the TCP session
The number of 32-bit words in the TCP header
The number of octets the sender is willing to accept
questions.explanation: The Window Size field in a TCP segment specifies how many octets the sender of the segment is willing to receive (its receive buffer size). This enables flow control — the receiver tells the sender how much data it can buffer.

questions.question: A Telnet session to a remote host is addressed to which port number?
questions.explanation: Telnet uses TCP port 23. This is a well-known port: the server listens on TCP/23, and the Telnet client connects to it. Note that Telnet is cleartext — SSH (TCP/22) is preferred for secure remote management.

questions.question: Which of the following does 'show cdp neighbors detail' reveal? (Choose all correct answers represented as one option)
options: Router platform, IOS version, all Layer 3 addresses, incoming/outgoing port, and router name
Only the IP address of the neighbor
SNMP community strings and VTP domain
questions.explanation: The 'show cdp neighbors detail' command reveals: Device ID (hostname), all Layer 3 addresses (one per protocol), platform/model, IOS version, local/remote port, and holdtime. It does NOT show subnet masks or SNMP strings.

questions.question: What is the IP address class and major network for the address 191.12.45.254?
questions.explanation: First octet 191 falls in the range 128-191, making this a Class B address. Class B addresses use the first two octets for the network, so the major network is 191.12.0.0 with a default mask of 255.255.0.0.

questions.question: What is most correct about the IP address 0.10.10.10?
options: It is a valid Class A address
It is a reserved private address
It is not a legal IP address
It can only be used on private networks
questions.explanation: No IP address may have a value of 0 in the first octet. Cisco IOS will reject it: 'Not a valid host address - 0.10.10.10'. The valid unicast range starts at 1.x.x.x (Class A starts at 1, not 0).

questions.question: What is the maximum number of hosts a Class C address can support with its default subnet mask?
questions.explanation: A Class C address has 8 host bits (fourth octet). Using the formula 2^N - 2 = 2^8 - 2 = 256 - 2 = 254 hosts. We subtract 2 for the network address (all host bits = 0) and broadcast address (all host bits = 1).

questions.question: Which IP address classes would an administrator most likely assign to a host?
options: Class A, B, C, and D
Class A, B, C, D, and E
questions.explanation: Only Class A (1-126), B (128-191), and C (192-223) addresses are assigned to hosts. Class D (224-239) is reserved for multicast groups. Class E (240-255) is reserved for experimental use. Neither D nor E can be assigned to a host.

questions.question: TCP uses 'expectational' acknowledgements. If the sender sent segments 12, 13, and 14 and all were received correctly, what does the receiver ACK with?
questions.explanation: TCP uses expectational acknowledgements: the ACK number indicates the NEXT expected segment, not the last received. After correctly receiving segments 12, 13, and 14, the receiver sends ACK=15, meaning 'I got 12-14, send me 15 next.'

questions.question: Which four of the following protocols most commonly use UDP? (Choose the option listing all four.)
questions.explanation: UDP is used by: DNS (port 53), TFTP (port 69), SNMP (port 161/162), and NFS. FTP (TCP 20/21), Telnet (TCP 23), and SMTP (TCP 25) all use TCP because they require reliable delivery.

questions.question: The IP protocol is best described as:
options: Determines data link addresses for known MAC addresses
Provides connection-oriented, reliable delivery of datagrams
Provides connectionless, best-effort delivery routing of datagrams
questions.explanation: IP is a connectionless, best-effort, unreliable protocol — it tries to deliver packets but does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or error recovery. Reliability is added by upper-layer protocols like TCP. IP simply routes datagrams toward the destination.

questions.question: ICMP is best described as:
options: Determines data link addresses for known IP addresses
Resolves MAC addresses to IP addresses
Adds reliable connection-oriented service to IP
Helps IP by providing control and messaging capabilities
questions.explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) is a helper protocol for IP. It provides error reporting and diagnostic functions such as Destination Unreachable, Time Exceeded, Echo Request/Reply (ping), and Source Quench. ICMP messages are carried inside IP datagrams.

questions.question: Two protocols are needed to send a 'host unreachable' message back to a source host. Which two?
questions.explanation: ICMP generates the 'Destination Unreachable' message, but ICMP messages are carried inside IP datagrams. So both IP (to route the message) and ICMP (to generate the control message) are needed to send a host unreachable notification.

questions.question: The OSI Presentation layer is primarily responsible for which two functions?
options: Code formatting/conversion (e.g. EBCDIC to ASCII) and encryption
Managing NFS and SQL application sessions
Segmentation and reassembly of application data
questions.explanation: Layer 6 (Presentation) handles data formatting: it translates between character sets (e.g., EBCDIC ↔ ASCII), manages data compression, and handles encryption/decryption. Sessions are managed at Layer 5 (Session); segmentation is Layer 4 (Transport).

questions.question: In the context of multiplexing/demultiplexing, a port number is best described as:
options: A method of ensuring correct encapsulation format for the receiving host
A numbering system that identifies data portions of segments
A method of distinguishing data from different applications on a single data stream
Assembly of data from different OSI layers into separate streams
questions.explanation: Port numbers allow the Transport layer to multiplex multiple application conversations over a single network connection. Each port number identifies a specific application, enabling the receiving host to demultiplex incoming data to the correct application.

questions.question: TCP handshaking (the 3-way handshake) occurs at which OSI layer?
options: Data-link layer during Token Ring framing
Network layer when using connection-oriented protocols
Transport layer when TCP is being used
questions.explanation: The TCP 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) is a Layer 4 (Transport) function. TCP is a connection-oriented Transport layer protocol. Layer 5 (Session) manages broader application sessions, but the handshake itself is TCP/Transport.

questions.question: TCP windowing allows for which two benefits?
options: Multiple frame types over one media type, and separate data streams
Multiple segments sent before the sender requires an ACK, and more efficient bandwidth use
Multiple segments sent before the receiver requires an ACK, and separate data streams
Guaranteed delivery and in-order packet processing
questions.explanation: TCP windowing allows the sender to transmit multiple segments before waiting for an acknowledgement (controlled by window size). This keeps the pipe full and makes much more efficient use of available bandwidth compared to stop-and-wait.

questions.question: In a Frame Relay map statement (`frame-relay map ip <address> <dlci>`), what does each field represent?
options: IP = local router IP address; DLCI = remote DLCI number
IP = next-hop (remote) IP address; DLCI = local DLCI number
IP = next-hop IP address; DLCI = remote DLCI number
IP = Frame Relay switch IP; DLCI = PVC circuit ID
questions.explanation: In `frame-relay map ip <next-hop-ip> <local-dlci>`, the IP address is the NEXT HOP address (the far-end router), and the DLCI is the LOCAL DLCI assigned to your interface by the Frame Relay provider. The DLCI has only local significance.

questions.question: Which Frame Relay parameter must match end-to-end between routers, and which only has local significance?
options: Encapsulation must match end-to-end; LMI type has only local significance
LMI type must match end-to-end; encapsulation has only local significance
Both encapsulation and LMI must match end-to-end
Neither needs to match; they are both locally significant
questions.explanation: Frame Relay encapsulation (Cisco or IETF) must match on both ends of the PVC because it determines the frame format. LMI (Local Management Interface) is a keepalive protocol between the local router and the local Frame Relay switch ONLY — it does not need to match the far end.

questions.question: What is HDLC an abbreviation for?
questions.explanation: HDLC (High-level Data Link Control) is a bit-oriented, synchronous data link layer protocol developed by ISO, derived from IBM's SDLC. It is the default encapsulation on Cisco serial interfaces. Cisco's version is proprietary and adds a type field not in the ISO standard.

questions.question: Frame Relay is best described as a variation of which protocol?
questions.explanation: Frame Relay is a packet-switched WAN protocol that is derived from HDLC. It is a streamlined version that removes error correction and flow control (done at higher layers), making it faster than X.25. DLCIs identify virtual circuits in Frame Relay.

questions.question: What is the last piece of ISDN equipment at the customer premises (CPE) before the local loop?
questions.explanation: NT1 (Network Termination 1) is the last customer-side ISDN device at the U interface (where the local loop from the CO ends). In the US, the NT1 is customer-owned. NT2 is optional PBX/router equipment; TE1 is ISDN-capable terminal equipment; TA adapts non-ISDN devices.

questions.question: What is the maximum OSPF link-state metric (cost) value?
questions.explanation: OSPF uses cost as its metric. The maximum OSPF cost is 65,535 (16-bit unsigned integer). Cost is calculated as 100 Mbps / interface bandwidth. A Fast Ethernet interface has a cost of 1 (100/100). Higher bandwidth = lower cost.

questions.question: What is the Cisco IOS keyboard shortcut to recall the NEXT command in command history?
questions.explanation: Ctrl-N (or Down Arrow) recalls the next command in the history buffer, moving forward in history. Ctrl-P (or Up Arrow) recalls the previous command. Ctrl-Z exits to privileged EXEC. Ctrl-C aborts the current command.

questions.question: A 'show interfaces serial0' output shows 'Serial0 is up, line protocol is down'. What does this most likely indicate?
options: No keepalives are being received (clocking/layer 2 issue)
The interface is administratively shut down
questions.explanation: Interface up / protocol down means Layer 1 is working (carrier detected) but Layer 2 has failed. Most commonly this means keepalives are not being received, which indicates a clocking issue, encapsulation mismatch, or the remote end is not responding. 'Administratively down' shows a different message.

questions.question: Given subnets 128.252.4.0, 128.252.8.0, 128.252.12.0, 128.252.16.0 — what subnet mask is in use?
questions.explanation: The subnets increment by 4 in the third octet (4, 8, 12, 16...). An increment of 4 means the block size is 4, which corresponds to borrowing 6 bits in the third octet: 255.255.252.0 (/22). 256 - 252 = 4 confirms the block size.

questions.question: A router interface is described as 10.10.1.1/23. What is its subnet mask in dotted-decimal?
questions.explanation: /23 means 23 bits of network mask. First two octets = 16 bits (255.255). Remaining 7 bits in third octet = 11111110 = 254. So /23 = 255.255.254.0. A /23 covers two Class C blocks (e.g., 10.10.0.0 - 10.10.1.255).

questions.question: What does the wildcard mask 0.0.7.255 specify in an ACL statement?
options: Match any address in the range .0 to .255 of the network
Match 8 contiguous subnets (the range 10.1.8.0 through 10.1.15.0 when combined with 10.1.8.0)
questions.explanation: Wildcard mask 0.0.7.255 means: octets 1,2 must match exactly; octet 3 can vary by up to 7 (binary: 00000111); octet 4 can be anything (255). So `10.1.8.0 0.0.7.255` matches addresses from 10.1.8.0 through 10.1.15.255 — a range of 8 networks.

questions.question: What are the four steps of the DHCP DORA process in order?
questions.explanation: DHCP DORA: (1) Discover — client broadcasts to find DHCP servers; (2) Offer — server responds with IP offer; (3) Request — client broadcasts to accept the offer (may have multiple servers); (4) Acknowledge — server confirms the lease. The client gets an IP only after the ACK.

questions.question: What is the difference between static NAT, dynamic NAT, and PAT?
options: Static=one-to-one fixed; Dynamic=many-to-pool, one-at-a-time; PAT=many-to-one using port numbers
Static=dynamic allocation; Dynamic=fixed mapping; PAT=no translation
Static=all use same port; Dynamic=random ports; PAT=fixed port 80
Static and Dynamic are the same; only PAT uses port numbers
questions.explanation: Static NAT: permanent one-to-one mapping (inside local ↔ inside global). Dynamic NAT: maps inside hosts to a pool of public addresses; exhaustible. PAT (Port Address Translation / NAT Overload): maps many inside hosts to ONE public address using unique source port numbers — allows thousands of simultaneous sessions.

questions.question: What are NTP stratum levels and what does stratum 0 represent?
options: Stratum 0 = NTP server; higher strata = clients
Stratum 0 = atomic clock/GPS reference; Stratum 1 = directly connected NTP server; each hop adds one stratum
Stratum 0-7 = reliability levels, 7 being most reliable
Stratum 0 = broadcast NTP; Stratum 1 = unicast NTP
questions.explanation: NTP stratum indicates distance from the authoritative time source. Stratum 0 = atomic clocks/GPS (reference clocks, not on network). Stratum 1 = NTP servers connected directly to stratum 0 (most accurate). Each NTP hop adds one stratum level (max 15; 16 = unreachable). Lower stratum = more accurate.

questions.question: What are the 8 Syslog severity levels from most severe (0) to least severe (7)?
options: Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notice, Informational, Debug
Debug, Informational, Notice, Warning, Error, Critical, Alert, Emergency
Emergency, Critical, Error, Alert, Warning, Notice, Debug, Informational
Error, Warning, Notice, Alert, Critical, Debug, Emergency, Informational
questions.explanation: Syslog severity levels 0-7: 0=Emergency (system unusable), 1=Alert (immediate action needed), 2=Critical, 3=Error, 4=Warning, 5=Notice (normal but significant), 6=Informational, 7=Debug (most verbose). Mnemonic: 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice Daily'. Logging buffered debug = all levels.

questions.question: What is QoS DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point) and how many bits does it use?
options: A 3-bit Layer 2 priority field in 802.1Q headers
A 6-bit field in the IP header used to classify and prioritize traffic
An 8-bit field replacing the entire ToS byte
questions.explanation: DSCP uses the top 6 bits of the IP header's ToS (Type of Service) byte to mark packet priority. With 6 bits, 64 values are possible (0-63). Common markings: EF (Expedited Forwarding, 46) for voice, AF (Assured Forwarding) classes 1-4 for data, CS0-CS7 for backward compatibility with IP Precedence.

questions.question: What is the function of SNMP traps versus SNMP polls?
options: Traps=NMS queries device; Polls=device sends unsolicited alerts
Traps=device sends unsolicited alerts to NMS (UDP 162); Polls=NMS queries device (UDP 161)
Traps and polls both use UDP 161 to the NMS
Traps are used for configuration; polls are used for monitoring
questions.explanation: SNMP GET/SET (polls): the NMS (Network Management Station) queries managed devices using UDP port 161. SNMP Traps: managed devices send UNSOLICITED alerts to the NMS using UDP port 162 when notable events occur (e.g., interface down, temperature threshold). Traps reduce NMS polling overhead.

questions.question: What Cisco IOS command configures a router as a DHCP server to exclude addresses .1 through .10 from being assigned?
questions.explanation: The correct command is `ip dhcp excluded-address <low-ip> <high-ip>` in global config. This prevents the DHCP server from assigning those addresses (typically used for routers, servers, and printers that have static IPs). The DHCP pool is then defined with `ip dhcp pool <name>` followed by `network`, `default-router`, `dns-server`, and `lease` commands.

questions.question: What are the minimum steps to configure SSH access on a Cisco router?
options: Set hostname, set domain name, generate RSA keys, configure VTY lines for SSH
Only configure vty lines with `transport input ssh`
Install SSL certificate and configure HTTPS
Set enable secret and configure VTY with password
questions.explanation: SSH configuration requires: (1) `hostname <name>` — router needs a unique name; (2) `ip domain-name <domain>` — needed for RSA key generation; (3) `crypto key generate rsa` — creates the RSA key pair (2048 bits recommended); (4) `line vty 0 4` then `transport input ssh` and `login local`; (5) create a local user with `username <x> secret <pw>`.

questions.question: What are the three port security violation modes in Cisco IOS?
questions.explanation: Port security violation modes: Protect — silently drops frames from unknown MACs, no notification. Restrict — drops frames AND increments violation counter, sends syslog. Shutdown (default) — puts port into err-disabled state, sends syslog/SNMP trap. The port must be manually re-enabled after shutdown: `shutdown` then `no shutdown`.

questions.question: What is the difference between RADIUS and TACACS+ for AAA?
options: RADIUS=TCP 49, encrypts entire packet; TACACS+=UDP 1812/1813, encrypts only password
RADIUS=UDP 1812/1813, encrypts only password; TACACS+=TCP 49, encrypts entire packet
Both use UDP; RADIUS is Cisco proprietary while TACACS+ is open standard
RADIUS is for authentication only; TACACS+ is for authorization only
questions.explanation: RADIUS: open standard, uses UDP (1812=auth, 1813=accounting), only encrypts the password. TACACS+: Cisco-developed, uses TCP port 49, encrypts the ENTIRE packet (more secure). TACACS+ separates Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting into independent services. RADIUS combines authentication and authorization.

questions.question: What is the difference between an IDS and an IPS in terms of traffic handling?
options: IDS blocks malicious traffic inline; IPS only monitors and alerts
IDS monitors and alerts (out-of-band); IPS sits inline and can block traffic in real-time
IDS is hardware-based; IPS is software-based
They are identical; IDS is the older name for IPS
questions.explanation: IDS (Intrusion Detection System) operates out-of-band — it receives a copy of traffic, analyzes it, and generates alerts but CANNOT block traffic. IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) is inline — all traffic passes through it, allowing real-time blocking of detected threats. IPS adds latency; IDS does not.

questions.question: What does 'AAA' stand for in network security, and which Cisco service implements it?
options: Authentication, Authorization, Accounting — implemented via RADIUS or TACACS+
Authentication, Availability, Access — implemented via ACLs
Authorization, Auditing, Alerts — implemented via Syslog
Access, Administration, Accounting — implemented via SNMP
questions.explanation: AAA = Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (what are you allowed to do?), Accounting (what did you do?). Cisco implements AAA via RADIUS (open standard, UDP 1812/1813) or TACACS+ (Cisco, TCP 49). Configured with `aaa new-model`, then `aaa authentication login default group radius local` etc.

questions.question: What is a common Layer 2 attack and how does Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) mitigate it?
options: STP manipulation — DAI blocks BPDU frames
ARP spoofing/poisoning — DAI validates ARP packets against a DHCP snooping binding table
MAC flooding — DAI limits MAC addresses per port
VLAN hopping — DAI enforces 802.1Q native VLANs
questions.explanation: ARP spoofing is a Layer 2 attack where a host sends fake ARP replies to poison ARP caches, redirecting traffic through the attacker (man-in-the-middle). DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection) mitigates this by checking ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table (IP-to-MAC mappings). Ports without valid DHCP bindings are untrusted by default.

questions.question: What is DHCP snooping and what attacks does it prevent?
options: Monitors DHCP traffic for unusual patterns; prevents DoS attacks
A Layer 2 security feature that filters DHCP messages — allows only DHCP servers on trusted ports; prevents rogue DHCP servers
Encrypts DHCP messages to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks
Blocks all DHCP broadcasts on untrusted VLANs
questions.explanation: DHCP Snooping is a Layer 2 security feature that distinguishes between trusted (uplink to legitimate DHCP server) and untrusted (client-facing) ports. DHCP server messages (OFFER, ACK) from untrusted ports are dropped, preventing rogue DHCP servers. It also builds an IP-MAC-port binding table used by DAI and IP Source Guard.

questions.question: What are the five standard HTTP methods used by REST APIs and what do they do?
options: GET=read, POST=create, PUT=replace, PATCH=partial update, DELETE=remove
questions.explanation: REST API HTTP methods: GET — retrieve resource (read-only, safe, idempotent). POST — create new resource. PUT — replace entire resource (idempotent). PATCH — partial update of resource. DELETE — remove resource. RESTCONF uses all these methods over HTTPS to manage network devices.

questions.question: What is NETCONF and what transport/port does it use?
options: A network configuration protocol using HTTPS/443 with JSON encoding
A network configuration protocol using SSH/830 with XML encoding and YANG data models
A SNMP replacement using UDP/830 with MIB data models
A REST-based API using HTTP/80 with XML or JSON
questions.explanation: NETCONF (Network Configuration Protocol, RFC 6241) is a network management protocol that uses SSH on port 830 and XML for encoding. It uses YANG data models to describe network configuration and state. NETCONF operations include get-config, edit-config, copy-config, delete-config, lock, unlock, and close-session.

questions.question: Compare Ansible vs Puppet for network automation. Which is agentless and which is agent-based?
options: Both are agentless; Ansible uses SSH, Puppet uses HTTPS
Both require agents; Ansible uses YAML, Puppet uses Ruby DSL
questions.explanation: Ansible: agentless (no software installed on managed nodes), uses SSH/NETCONF to push configurations, uses YAML playbooks, simple to set up. Puppet: agent-based (Puppet agent installed on nodes), uses pull model (agents check in to Puppet master on TCP 8140), uses Ruby DSL manifests. Chef is also agent-based like Puppet.

questions.question: In an SDN architecture, what is the difference between the northbound and southbound APIs?
options: Northbound=between controller and network devices; Southbound=between controller and applications
Northbound=between controller and applications (REST); Southbound=between controller and network devices (OpenFlow/NETCONF)
Both connect to network devices; northbound uses TCP, southbound uses UDP
questions.explanation: SDN API hierarchy: Southbound APIs (also called device APIs) connect the SDN controller to the network devices it manages — examples include OpenFlow, NETCONF, RESTCONF, gRPC. Northbound APIs connect the controller to applications and management tools — typically REST APIs. The controller sits in the middle, translating intent (northbound) to device config (southbound).

questions.question: What is YANG and what is its relationship to NETCONF and RESTCONF?
options: YANG is a query language for XML databases used independently of NETCONF
YANG is a data modeling language that defines the structure of network configuration/state data; NETCONF and RESTCONF use YANG models to encode/validate data
YANG is a replacement for SNMP MIBs and only works with RESTCONF
YANG is a Cisco-proprietary scripting language for IOS automation
questions.explanation: YANG (Yet Another Next Generation, RFC 6020/7950) is a data modeling language that defines the structure, syntax, and semantics of network configuration and operational data. NETCONF uses YANG with XML encoding; RESTCONF uses YANG with XML or JSON encoding. YANG models specify exactly what configuration options exist and what constraints apply, enabling machine-readable network management.

questions.question: In traditional networking, the control plane and data plane are co-located on each device. What does SDN change about this?
options: SDN removes the data plane from routers/switches
SDN separates the control plane from the data plane — the control plane is centralized in an SDN controller
SDN moves the data plane to cloud servers while keeping control local
SDN combines both planes into a single software process
questions.explanation: Traditional devices have both control plane (routing protocols, STP, ARP) and data plane (forwarding based on lookup tables) co-located. SDN separates them: the control plane moves to a centralized controller, while network devices retain only the data plane (forwarding). This enables centralized policy, programmability, and dynamic topology management.

questions.question: For a Class C network, how many subnets and hosts per subnet does a /27 mask (255.255.255.224) provide?
questions.explanation: /27 borrows 3 bits from the host portion of a Class C address. Subnets = 2^3 - 2 = 6. Remaining host bits = 5, so hosts = 2^5 - 2 = 30. Block size = 256 - 224 = 32. Subnet boundaries: .0, .32, .64, .96, .128, .160, .192, .224.

questions.question: For a Class C network, what mask gives 14 subnets with 14 hosts each?
questions.explanation: /28 (255.255.255.240) borrows 4 bits. Subnets = 2^4 - 2 = 14. Host bits = 4, hosts = 2^4 - 2 = 14. Block size = 256 - 240 = 16. This is a common exam pairing: 14 subnets AND 14 hosts with /28.

questions.question: What is the subnet mask for a /30 prefix (commonly used for point-to-point links)?
questions.explanation: /30 = 255.255.255.252. It provides only 2 usable host addresses (2^2 - 2 = 2), making it ideal for point-to-point WAN links. The block size is 4. Class C example: 62 subnets with 2 hosts each.

questions.question: What are the three basic methods of flow control used in networking?
options: Buffering, source quench messages, and windowing
CRC checking, framing, and sequence numbers
questions.explanation: The three flow control methods: (1) Buffering — temporarily stores excess data in memory; can overflow. (2) Source quench messages — receiver sends ICMP source quench to tell sender to slow down. (3) Windowing — sender must get ACK after sending N bytes before sending more; dynamically adjusts based on network conditions.

questions.question: Which WAN switching type uses a dedicated physical circuit established for each communication session?
questions.explanation: Circuit switching establishes a dedicated physical path for the entire duration of the communication (like a phone call). ISDN/LAPD uses circuit switching. Packet switching (Frame Relay, X.25) shares the network medium dynamically. Cell switching (ATM) uses fixed-length 53-byte cells.

questions.question: What is the Frame Relay DE (Discard Eligibility) bit used for?
options: Indicates the frame should be delivered with high priority
Marks frames that can be dropped first during network congestion (traffic beyond CIR)
Signals the destination that the frame is the last in a sequence
Enables dynamic routing updates to be forwarded
questions.explanation: The DE bit is set on oversubscribed traffic — traffic received after the CIR (Committed Information Rate) has been exceeded. When a Frame Relay switch detects congestion, it drops DE-marked frames first before dropping frames without the DE bit. CIR is the guaranteed average data rate.

questions.question: What are the channel configuration and speeds for ISDN BRI vs. PRI (US)?
questions.explanation: ISDN BRI: 2 Bearer channels (64 Kbps each for data/voice) + 1 Delta channel (16 Kbps for signaling) = 144 Kbps total. ISDN PRI US/Japan: 23B + 1D (all channels = 64 Kbps) = 24 × 64 = 1.536 Mbps + framing = 1.544 Mbps (T1). Europe PRI: 30B + 1D = 2.048 Mbps (E1).

questions.question: What Cisco IOS command verifies NAT translations currently in the NAT table?
questions.explanation: `show ip nat translations` displays the active NAT translation table, showing inside local (original private), inside global (translated public), outside local, and outside global addresses. `show ip nat statistics` shows hit counts, misses, and expired translations. `debug ip nat` shows real-time translation events.

questions.question: What NTP command configures a Cisco router to synchronize to an NTP server at 10.0.0.1?
questions.explanation: `ntp server <ip-address>` configures the router as an NTP client synchronizing to the specified server. Multiple `ntp server` commands can be used for redundancy. `ntp master [stratum]` makes the router an NTP server. `show ntp status` and `show ntp associations` verify NTP operation.

questions.question: What is the difference between QoS CoS (Class of Service) and DSCP?
options: CoS=3-bit Layer 2 field in 802.1Q tag (0-7); DSCP=6-bit Layer 3 IP header field (0-63)
CoS=6-bit Layer 3 field; DSCP=3-bit Layer 2 field
Both are 8-bit fields; CoS is in IP header, DSCP is in Ethernet frame
CoS and DSCP are identical; they just use different names
questions.explanation: CoS (Class of Service): 3-bit Priority Code Point (PCP) field in the 802.1Q VLAN tag — provides 8 priority levels (0-7). Only present when frames are tagged. DSCP: 6 bits in the IP header's ToS byte — provides 64 priority markings. When packets cross layer boundaries (L2→L3), QoS mappings translate between CoS and DSCP values.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of secure MAC addresses configurable with port security, and what command sets it?
options: Maximum is 128; configured with `switchport port-security maximum 128`
No maximum; limited only by switch MAC table
Maximum is 1 by default; configured with `switchport port-security mac-address max <n>`
questions.explanation: Port security default maximum is 1 MAC address per port. You can increase it: `switchport port-security maximum <1-132>`. The default violation action is shutdown. To enable: `switchport port-security`. To verify: `show port-security interface <interface>`. Sticky MACs: `switchport port-security mac-address sticky` learns and saves MACs to running-config.

questions.question: What is a VLAN hopping attack and what are two ways to mitigate it?
options: Attacker sends oversized frames; mitigation = jumbo frames
Attacker uses switch spoofing or double-tagging to access other VLANs; mitigation = disable DTP and change native VLAN from default (1)
Attacker ARP-poisons the default gateway; mitigation = DAI
Attacker floods MAC table; mitigation = port security
questions.explanation: VLAN hopping attacks: (1) Switch spoofing — attacker's port negotiates trunk (DTP). Mitigate: `switchport mode access` + `switchport nonegotiate` on access ports. (2) Double tagging — attacker sends 802.1Q-tagged frame with native VLAN, which gets forwarded to another VLAN. Mitigate: change native VLAN to an unused VLAN (not VLAN 1): `switchport trunk native vlan 999`.

questions.question: What command shows whether port security is enabled on a switch interface and its current state?
questions.explanation: `show port-security interface <if>` displays: port security status (Enabled/Disabled), violation mode, maximum/current MAC addresses, violation count, and last violating MAC. `show port-security address` shows the secure MAC table. An err-disabled port (after shutdown violation) shows as 'Secure-shutdown'.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 802.1X port-based authentication?
options: Encrypts all traffic on the access port using AES
Requires devices to authenticate to an AAA server (RADIUS) before gaining network access through a switch port
Assigns VLANs based on MAC address source
Provides physical layer authentication via cable type
questions.explanation: 802.1X uses EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol) for port-based NAC (Network Access Control). Three roles: Supplicant (end device with 802.1X client), Authenticator (switch/AP), Authentication Server (RADIUS). Until authenticated, the port only passes EAP frames. After auth, normal access is granted. Can dynamically assign VLANs based on auth result.

questions.question: What data format does RESTCONF use, and what transport does it run on?
options: XML only, runs over SSH port 830
XML or JSON, runs over HTTPS (TLS)
JSON only, runs over HTTP port 80
questions.explanation: RESTCONF (RFC 8040) is a REST-based protocol for network management. It uses HTTPS for transport (TLS-encrypted), and supports both XML and JSON as data encoding formats. It uses YANG data models (like NETCONF) but with RESTful HTTP methods (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) and standard URIs to address resources.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a network controller's 'intent-based networking' (IBN) capability?
options: Manually translates CLI commands to machine code
Allows administrators to declare WHAT the network should do (intent/policy), and the controller figures out HOW to implement it
Runs automated penetration tests against network devices
Provides real-time packet capture across the entire network
questions.explanation: Intent-Based Networking (IBN) abstracts network configuration: administrators define business outcomes or policies ('allow HR VLAN to access payroll server'), and the IBN controller translates this intent into specific device configurations automatically. This contrasts with traditional device-by-device CLI management. Cisco DNA Center is an example IBN controller.

questions.question: When using a REST API, which HTTP method is idempotent and used to completely replace an existing resource?
options: POST — creates or replaces any resource
GET — retrieves and caches for replacement
questions.explanation: PUT is idempotent (calling it multiple times produces the same result as calling it once) and completely replaces the target resource with the request body. POST is not idempotent (creates new resources each time). PATCH is for partial updates (modify specific fields). GET is read-only and safe. DELETE removes the resource.

questions.question: What is the difference between a traditional network device's control plane and data plane?
options: Control plane = hardware forwarding; Data plane = software routing decisions
Control plane = runs routing protocols and makes forwarding decisions; Data plane = actually forwards packets based on the FIB/CAM tables
Control plane = Layer 1-2 processing; Data plane = Layer 3-7 processing
They are the same thing on traditional devices
questions.explanation: Control plane: runs protocols (OSPF, STP, ARP) to build forwarding tables — CPU-intensive. Data plane (forwarding plane): uses the tables built by the control plane to forward packets/frames at line rate using specialized hardware (ASICs). Management plane: handles out-of-band management (SSH, SNMP). SDN separates these planes: control plane moves to a controller.

questions.question: In QoS, what is the difference between traffic policing and traffic shaping?
options: Policing=buffers excess traffic; Shaping=drops excess traffic
Policing=drops or re-marks excess traffic immediately; Shaping=buffers/queues excess traffic to smooth the rate
They are identical; policing is the Cisco term, shaping is the IETF term
Policing applies to inbound traffic only; shaping to outbound only
questions.explanation: Traffic Policing: enforces a rate limit by dropping or re-marking packets that exceed the rate — bursty traffic is discarded. Traffic Shaping: smooths traffic by buffering excess packets in a queue and releasing them at the contracted rate — more TCP-friendly but adds delay/jitter. Shaping is typically used on outbound WAN interfaces.

questions.question: What are the three QoS models from simplest to most complex?
questions.explanation: QoS models: (1) Best Effort — no QoS, first-in-first-out, no guarantees (default). (2) DiffServ (Differentiated Services) — classifies traffic into classes, applies per-hop behaviors using DSCP markings; scalable, most common. (3) IntServ (Integrated Services) — per-flow reservations using RSVP signaling; guaranteed bandwidth but not scalable for large networks.

questions.question: What is HSRP (Hot Standby Router Protocol) and what does it provide?
options: A dynamic routing protocol for fast convergence
A First Hop Redundancy Protocol (FHRP) that provides a virtual gateway IP/MAC for default gateway redundancy
A protocol that load-balances traffic across multiple equal-cost paths
A protocol for router configuration backup and restore
questions.explanation: HSRP (Cisco proprietary, RFC 2281) is a FHRP that creates a virtual router with a virtual IP and virtual MAC (0000.0c07.acXX where XX = group number). One router is Active, one is Standby. Hosts use the virtual IP as their default gateway. If the Active router fails, the Standby takes over within seconds without changing host configurations.

questions.question: In HSRP, what determines which router becomes the Active router?
options: The router with the lowest IP address
The router with the highest priority (default 100); tie-broken by highest IP address
The router that was configured first
The router with the fastest CPU
questions.explanation: HSRP Active router selection: highest priority wins (default priority = 100, range 0-255). If tied, highest IP address on the HSRP interface wins. Configured with `standby <group> priority <value>`. Preemption (taking over when a higher-priority router comes back) must be explicitly enabled: `standby <group> preempt`.

questions.question: What are the three main cloud service models?
options: IaaS (Infrastructure), PaaS (Platform), SaaS (Software) as a Service
Compute, Storage, and Networking as a Service
questions.explanation: Cloud service models: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) — provider supplies hardware/VMs/networking; customer manages OS up. PaaS (Platform as a Service) — provider manages OS/middleware; customer manages applications and data. SaaS (Software as a Service) — provider manages everything; customer just uses the app (e.g., Gmail, Salesforce). Each offers more/less customer control.

questions.question: What are the four cloud deployment models?
questions.explanation: Cloud deployment models: Public — owned by CSP (AWS, Azure, GCP), shared resources, OPEX model. Private — owned/operated by organization, exclusive use, on-premises or hosted. Community — shared by organizations with common concerns (government, healthcare). Hybrid — combination of two or more models interconnected. Most enterprises use hybrid.

questions.question: What are the wireless security protocols in order from weakest to strongest?
questions.explanation: Wireless security evolution: WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) — RC4 encryption, easily cracked, considered broken. WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access) — TKIP encryption, transitional, also vulnerable. WPA2 — AES-CCMP encryption, current standard (IEEE 802.11i), uses PSK or 802.1X/EAP. WPA3 — latest standard, SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) replaces PSK, forward secrecy, more secure.

questions.question: What is the difference between a virus, a worm, and a trojan horse?
options: All three are identical; just different names
Virus=requires host file, needs user action; Worm=self-replicates over network without user action; Trojan=disguised as legitimate software
Virus=spreads over network; Worm=requires email; Trojan=requires USB
questions.explanation: Virus: attaches to legitimate files/programs; requires user action (opening file) to spread; damages or modifies files. Worm: self-replicating, spreads automatically over network without user interaction; exploits vulnerabilities. Trojan horse: appears to be legitimate/useful software but contains malicious code; doesn't self-replicate but may open backdoors.

questions.question: What is a man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack?
options: An attack where the attacker floods the network with broadcast traffic
An attack where the attacker positions themselves between two communicating parties to intercept or alter communications
An attack where the attacker guesses passwords repeatedly
An attack where the attacker sends oversized packets to crash a host
questions.explanation: A MITM attack intercepts communications between two parties without their knowledge. The attacker may eavesdrop on or modify the data. Common MITM techniques include ARP spoofing (redirects traffic at Layer 2) and DNS spoofing. Mitigation: encryption (TLS/HTTPS), certificate validation, DAI (for ARP MITM), and DNSSEC.

questions.question: What is social engineering in the context of network security?
options: Engineering tools that make network management easier for social interactions
Manipulating people through psychological tactics to reveal confidential information or perform actions that compromise security
A method of configuring network devices via social media APIs
A collaborative approach to network design involving multiple teams
questions.explanation: Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Common techniques: Phishing (fraudulent emails/websites), Vishing (voice phishing via phone), Pretexting (creating a fabricated scenario), Baiting (offering something enticing), Tailgating/Piggybacking (following authorized person through secure door). User awareness training is the primary defense.

questions.question: What is Cisco SD-WAN (formerly Viptela) and what are its key components?
options: A MPLS WAN replacement using only ISP circuits
A software-defined WAN solution with vManage (management), vSmart (control), vBond (orchestration), and vEdge/cEdge (data plane)
A Cisco proprietary VPN technology for branch offices
A cloud-based load balancer for WAN traffic
questions.explanation: Cisco SD-WAN components: vManage — centralized NMS/GUI (management plane). vSmart — distributes policies/routes to vEdges (control plane). vBond — orchestrates initial connections, authenticates devices. vEdge/cEdge — physical/virtual WAN routers at sites (data plane). Benefits: centralized policy, transport independence (MPLS+internet+LTE), application-aware routing, lower cost.

questions.question: What is Cisco Catalyst Center (formerly DNA Center) and what does it provide?
options: A Cisco cloud-only router management portal
An on-premises SDN controller providing intent-based networking, automation, assurance, and security policy for campus/enterprise networks
A RADIUS server replacement for AAA
A tool for converting Cisco IOS configs to YAML format
questions.explanation: Cisco Catalyst Center (DNA Center) is an on-premises SDN controller for enterprise campus networks that provides: (1) Design — network hierarchy, templates.
