(2) Policy — intent-based access policy (group-based with SGTs). (3) Provision — automated device configuration/onboarding (PnP). (4) Assurance — AI/ML-based network monitoring and troubleshooting. Uses Cisco SD-Access for network fabric.

questions.question: What is the primary management model for Cisco Meraki devices?
options: On-premises controller that you install in a data center
Cloud-managed — all configuration and monitoring done through the Meraki cloud dashboard
SNMP-only management through a separate NMS server
questions.explanation: Meraki uses a cloud-managed architecture: all devices (switches, APs, firewalls, cameras) are managed through the Meraki cloud dashboard. Zero-touch provisioning — devices call home to Meraki cloud when connected. No on-premises controller needed. Configuration, monitoring, firmware updates, and analytics all done through the cloud portal.

questions.question: When using Python for network automation, what does the `requests` library provide?
options: HTTP/HTTPS client functionality for making REST API calls
questions.explanation: The Python `requests` library is the standard HTTP client library used for REST API interactions. It supports GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE methods. When working with RESTCONF or other REST APIs, `requests` sends HTTP requests with appropriate headers (Content-Type: application/json), handles authentication, and parses responses. Example: `requests.get('https://device/restconf/data/...', auth=('user','pass'))`

questions.question: What is JSON and why is it commonly used in network automation REST APIs?
options: JavaScript Object Notation — a verbose XML alternative used only in web browsers
JavaScript Object Notation — a lightweight, human-readable data format using key-value pairs; easy to parse in Python with json.loads()/json.dumps()
A Cisco-proprietary data format for IOS configuration storage
A compiled binary format for efficient network data transmission
questions.explanation: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight text-based data interchange format. It uses: objects {} (key-value pairs), arrays [] (ordered lists), strings, numbers, booleans, and null. REST APIs commonly return JSON. In Python: `json.loads(text)` parses JSON to dict, `json.dumps(dict)` converts to JSON. RESTCONF supports both JSON and XML. Compared to XML: shorter, easier to read.

questions.question: How do you calculate the wildcard mask for a subnet? What is the wildcard for 255.255.254.0?
questions.explanation: Formula: Wildcard mask = 255.255.255.255 − subnet mask. For 255.255.254.0: 255-255=0, 255-255=0, 255-254=1, 255-0=255 → Wildcard = 0.0.1.255. This means: match first two octets exactly, allow any value in byte 3 up to 1, allow any host byte. Covers two subnets: x.x.even.0 and x.x.odd.0.

questions.question: What command verifies the configured access lists on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `show access-lists` displays all configured access lists (IP, IPX, etc.) with match counts per line. `show ip access-lists` shows only IP access lists. `show ip interface <if>` shows which ACL is applied inbound/outbound on an interface. Both `show access-lists` and `show ip interface` are commonly used to verify ACL operation.

questions.question: In a WAN connection, what is the difference between DCE and DTE?
options: DCE=Data Center Equipment; DTE=Data Terminal Equipment
DCE=Data Communications Equipment (provides clocking, at carrier side); DTE=Data Terminal Equipment (customer router, receives clock)
DCE and DTE are identical; they are interchangeable
DCE=at the customer; DTE=at the carrier
questions.explanation: DCE (Data Communications Equipment): provides the clocking signal for WAN links (typically the CSU/DSU or Frame Relay switch). DTE (Data Terminal Equipment): the customer router that accepts the clock from the DCE. In lab simulations, the DCE side must be configured with `clock rate <speed>` (e.g., `clock rate 56000`). `show controllers serial0` identifies DCE vs DTE.

questions.question: What are the WAN terms CO, demarcation, and CPE?
options: CO=Central Office (carrier facility); Demarc=boundary between carrier and customer; CPE=Customer Premises Equipment
CO=Customer Office; Demarc=the firewall; CPE=Central Physical Equipment
CO=Circuit Opening; Demarc=the ISP router; CPE=Cloud Provider Edge
All three terms refer to the same equipment at the carrier facility
questions.explanation: WAN terms: CO (Central Office) — the telephone company/carrier's local switching facility. Demarcation (Demarc) — the physical boundary point between carrier and customer responsibility (usually a NID/CSU). CPE (Customer Premises Equipment) — all equipment on the customer side of the demarc (routers, switches, PBX). The local loop connects the demarc to the CO.

questions.question: What command displays all PVCs configured on a Frame Relay interface?
questions.explanation: `show frame-relay pvc` displays all PVC (Permanent Virtual Circuit) statistics including DLCI numbers, PVC status (Active/Inactive/Deleted), input/output packet counts, BECN/FECN/DE counts. `show frame-relay map` shows static and dynamic DLCI-to-IP address mappings. `show frame-relay lmi` shows LMI statistics.

questions.question: What command configures a Message of the Day (MOTD) banner on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `banner motd <delimiter><message><delimiter>` sets the MOTD banner shown to all users before login. The delimiter (e.g., #) marks the start and end of the message. Example: `banner motd # Authorized Access Only #`. Other banner types: `banner login` (shown at login prompt), `banner exec` (shown after login). MOTD is shown first.

questions.question: What are the OSPF router states in the neighbor establishment process?
options: Down → Init → 2-Way → ExStart → Exchange → Loading → Full
questions.explanation: OSPF neighbor states: Down → Init (hello received but not bidirectional) → 2-Way (bidirectional communication, DR/BDR election on multi-access) → ExStart (master/slave negotiation) → Exchange (DBD exchange) → Loading (LSR/LSU exchange) → Full (databases synchronized). Non-DR/BDR routers on broadcast networks stay in 2-Way with each other.

questions.question: How is OSPF cost calculated for an interface?
options: Cost = 100 Mbps / interface bandwidth (reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth)
Cost = hop count to the destination
questions.explanation: OSPF cost = reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth. Default reference = 100 Mbps. FastEthernet (100M) cost = 100/100 = 1. Gigabit Ethernet (1000M) also = 1 with default reference (problem!). Serial (1.544M T1) cost = 100/1.544 ≈ 64. To fix GigE: `ospf auto-cost reference-bandwidth 1000` makes GigE cost = 1, FastEthernet = 10. Lower cost = preferred path.

questions.question: What are the five STP port states in order from blocked to forwarding?
questions.explanation: 802.1d STP port states: Blocking (receives BPDUs, no data forwarding, 20 sec max). Listening (sends/receives BPDUs, elects root/designated ports, 15 sec). Learning (learns MACs but no data forwarding, 15 sec). Forwarding (normal operation). Disabled (manually shut down). Total convergence time: up to 50 seconds! RSTP (802.1w) reduces this dramatically.

questions.question: What is Rapid PVST+ and how does it improve on 802.1d STP?
options: It removes STP entirely, relying on ECMP for loop prevention
It is Cisco's implementation of 802.1w (RSTP) per-VLAN, converging in seconds instead of 30-50 seconds
It uses hardware ASICs instead of software for STP processing
It runs on Layer 3 instead of Layer 2
questions.explanation: Rapid PVST+ = Cisco's per-VLAN implementation of 802.1w (RSTP). Key improvements: port roles added (Alternate, Backup), faster state transitions (discarding → learning → forwarding), proposal/agreement mechanism for rapid transitions, edge ports (PortFast equivalent) go directly to forwarding. Convergence in <1 second vs 30-50 seconds for 802.1d. Default on modern Cisco switches.

questions.question: What are the IPv6 address types and their prefixes?
questions.explanation: IPv6 address types: Global Unicast (2000::/3) — routable on internet. Link-local (FE80::/10) — automatically configured, used for routing protocol neighbor discovery, not routable. Multicast (FF00::/8) — one-to-many. Anycast — same address on multiple devices, packet goes to nearest. Loopback = ::1/128. No broadcast in IPv6 (replaced by multicast). Unique local (FC00::/7) = IPv6 private addresses.

questions.question: What replaces ARP in IPv6 for address resolution?
options: NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) using ICMPv6 messages
questions.explanation: IPv6 uses NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol, RFC 4861) instead of ARP. NDP uses ICMPv6 messages: Neighbor Solicitation (NS, like ARP request) — sent to solicited-node multicast to find MAC for known IPv6 address. Neighbor Advertisement (NA, like ARP reply). Router Solicitation/Advertisement (RS/RA) — hosts find default gateways. NDP also handles SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration).

questions.question: What is the difference between an access port and a trunk port on a Cisco switch?
options: Access=carries multiple VLANs tagged with 802.1Q; Trunk=carries only one VLAN
Access=carries traffic for one VLAN (untagged); Trunk=carries traffic for multiple VLANs (802.1Q tagged)
Access=Layer 3 routed port; Trunk=Layer 2 switched port
Access=uplink to router; Trunk=connection to end devices
questions.explanation: Access port: connects to end devices (PCs, phones), carries traffic for ONE VLAN, frames are untagged. Configured with: `switchport mode access` + `switchport access vlan <id>`. Trunk port: connects to other switches or routers, carries traffic for MULTIPLE VLANs using 802.1Q tagging, each frame tagged with VLAN ID. Configured with: `switchport mode trunk`.

questions.question: What is the native VLAN on a trunk port and what security concern does it present?
options: The native VLAN carries management traffic only; no security concern
The native VLAN (default VLAN 1) carries untagged traffic on trunk ports; can be exploited by VLAN hopping (double-tagging)
The native VLAN is always VLAN 99; it carries spanning tree traffic
The native VLAN must match the management VLAN to work correctly
questions.explanation: The native VLAN is the VLAN that carries untagged frames on a trunk (default VLAN 1). Security concern: double-tagging attack — attacker sends a frame tagged with native VLAN ID; switch strips native tag, inner tag remains, frame forwarded to target VLAN. Mitigation: change native VLAN from default: `switchport trunk native vlan 999` (use an unused VLAN). Also tag native VLAN explicitly: `vlan dot1q tag native`.

questions.question: What is a floating static route and when would you use it?
options: A static route that automatically updates when the network changes
A static route with a higher AD than the dynamic routing protocol, used as a backup route that only activates when the primary dynamic route fails
A static route configured with a specific metric to control traffic flow
A default route (0.0.0.0/0) used as a gateway of last resort
questions.explanation: A floating static route has a manually configured AD higher than the dynamic routing protocol (e.g., AD=200 vs OSPF AD=110). When the dynamic route exists, it's preferred and the static route is invisible in the routing table (floats above). If the dynamic route fails, the static route activates as backup. Example: `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 192.168.1.1 200` — AD of 200 makes this a backup to any dynamic route.

questions.question: What is a default route and how is it configured on a Cisco router?
options: A route to the loopback interface; configured with `ip route loopback 0`
A catch-all route (0.0.0.0/0) used when no specific route matches; configured with `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>`
The first route loaded into the routing table during boot
A route that is automatically learned from DHCP
questions.explanation: A default route (0.0.0.0/0, also called the 'gateway of last resort') matches ANY destination when no more-specific route exists. It's the last resort before a packet is dropped. Configured with: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop-ip or exit-interface>`. Often distributed to other routers via `default-information originate` (OSPF) or `redistribute static` (EIGRP). Can also be learned from OSPF as an O*E2 route.

questions.question: For communication throughout an internetwork, which TWO things are required? (Choose the answer listing both.)
options: A hierarchical naming server (DNS) and a host file
A two-part addressing scheme (Network/Host) and a routable protocol (IP or IPX)
TCP protocol and a two-part address scheme
questions.explanation: Internetworking requires: (1) Two-part addressing (Network portion + Host portion) so routers can identify which network to forward to. (2) A routable protocol (IP, IPX) — protocols like NetBEUI or LAT have no Layer 3 addressing and cannot be routed. Note: TCP is a Transport layer protocol, not a routed protocol.

questions.question: In Cisco IOS, what does the caret symbol (^) in a failed command error message indicate?
options: Numeric data is required at this position
The point where an incorrect command, keyword, or argument was entered
The point where you press Return to complete the command
The beginning of a required value
questions.explanation: When IOS encounters an invalid command, it displays '% Invalid input detected at '^' marker.' The ^ (caret) points to the exact character where IOS could not parse the input. This helps quickly identify typos. Example: `R2#sh clack` → `^ % Invalid input detected` — the caret is under 'c' in 'clack'.

questions.question: Which subnet mask has the nickname 'serial mask' and why?
options: 255.255.255.252 — provides exactly 2 hosts, perfect for point-to-point serial links
255.255.255.240 — provides 14 hosts for medium WAN segments
255.255.255.192 — provides 62 hosts for large WAN segments
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.252 (/30) is called the 'serial mask' because a point-to-point WAN link needs exactly 2 hosts (one on each end). /30 provides 2^2 - 2 = 2 usable addresses. This minimizes IP address waste on WAN links. A /31 can also be used on point-to-point links (RFC 3021) — provides 2 addresses without subtraction (no network/broadcast).

questions.question: Which THREE of the following are valid reasons for creating VLANs?
options: Moves/Adds/Changes are simpler, limits broadcast domains, and the switch routes between VLANs
Moves/Adds/Changes are simpler, less administrative overhead, and limits broadcast domains
The switch routes between VLANs, limits collision domains, and reduces cabling
Limits broadcast domains, provides encryption, and enables Layer 3 switching
questions.explanation: VLANs provide: (1) Simplified Moves/Adds/Changes — a move within the same VLAN needs no IP reconfiguration. (2) Less administrative overhead — logical grouping independent of physical location. (3) Limited broadcast domains — each VLAN is its own broadcast domain, reducing unnecessary traffic. Note: switches do NOT route between VLANs by default — that requires a router (ROAS) or Layer 3 switch.

questions.question: What is the purpose of NAT (Network Address Translation) and what are the four NAT address types?
options: NAT encrypts IP headers; addresses: source, destination, port, protocol
NAT translates private IP addresses to public; types: inside local, inside global, outside local, outside global
NAT replaces MAC addresses with IP addresses for routing
NAT provides DHCP services; address types: static, dynamic, PAT, CGNAT
questions.explanation: NAT conserves public IPv4 addresses by translating private (RFC 1918) to public. Four address types: Inside Local = private IP of inside host (before translation). Inside Global = public IP representing inside host (after translation). Outside Local = IP of outside host as seen from inside network. Outside Global = actual IP of outside host. Most NAT = Inside Local ↔ Inside Global translation.

questions.question: What is the Cisco IOS command to verify which interfaces are configured as NAT inside or outside?
questions.explanation: `show ip nat statistics` shows total active translations, interfaces configured as inside/outside, hit/miss counts, and expired translations. `show ip nat translations` shows the actual translation table. `show running-config` also shows `ip nat inside`/`ip nat outside` interface commands and the NAT pool/access-list configuration.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network security architecture?
options: A military-grade secure network zone with no internet access
A perimeter network segment that hosts public-facing servers while isolating them from the internal network
A VLAN dedicated to network management traffic
A firewall configuration that blocks all inbound traffic
questions.explanation: A DMZ is a perimeter network segment between the internet and the internal network, typically created using two firewalls. Public-facing servers (web, email, DNS) are placed in the DMZ — they're accessible from the internet but isolated from internal systems. If a DMZ server is compromised, attackers can't directly reach internal resources. Traffic flow: Internet → outer firewall → DMZ → inner firewall → internal.

questions.question: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC) and how does it relate to network automation?
options: Writing CLI commands in Python scripts to configure one device at a time

Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files (e.g., Terraform, Ansible) rather than manual processes — enabling version control, consistency, and rapid deployment
Converting router configurations to C++ code for faster processing
Using hardware description languages to program FPGA-based network devices
questions.explanation: IaC treats infrastructure configuration as software code: stored in version control (Git), peer-reviewed, tested, and deployed automatically. Benefits: consistency (no configuration drift), repeatability, rollback, scalability. Tools: Terraform (cloud infrastructure), Ansible (network configuration), Puppet/Chef. For networks: IaC means defining intended network state in YAML/JSON and having automation tools enforce it.

questions.question: What is the difference between version control (Git) and CI/CD in the context of network automation?
options: They are identical; CI/CD is just an older name for Git
Git = tracks changes to configuration files and enables collaboration/rollback; CI/CD = automates testing and deployment of those configurations
Git = deploys configurations; CI/CD = stores them
Git is for programmers only; CI/CD is network-specific
questions.explanation: Git (version control): tracks every change to configuration files, who made it, and when. Enables branching, merging, and rollback to any previous state. CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment): automates the pipeline from code commit to deployment. CI = automatically tests changes when committed. CD = automatically deploys passing changes. Together they enable reliable, auditable network configuration changes.

questions.question: In ISDN, what are the four reference points and what do they describe?
options: R=non-ISDN to TA; S=user terminals to NT2; T=NT1 to NT2; U=NT1 to local loop
R=NT1 to NT2; S=local loop; T=TA to TE2; U=NT2 to switch
questions.explanation: ISDN reference points define the interface type between components: R = between non-ISDN equipment (TE2) and Terminal Adapter (TA). S = between user terminals (TE1 or TA) and NT2 (PBX). T = between NT2 and NT1. U = between NT1 and the carrier's local loop (2-wire). The S/T reference points are often combined as 'S/T interface' on Cisco BRI interfaces.

questions.question: What is CAPWAP and what role does it play in wireless networking?
options: A wireless encryption protocol that replaces WPA2
A tunneling protocol (UDP 5246/5247) that allows a WLC to centrally manage lightweight APs, carrying 802.11 data and control traffic
A Cisco-proprietary routing protocol for wireless mesh networks
A QoS protocol for prioritizing wireless VoIP traffic
questions.explanation: CAPWAP (Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points, RFC 5415) is the standard protocol used between a WLC (Wireless LAN Controller) and lightweight APs. It creates two UDP tunnels: Control (UDP 5246) for management/configuration, Data (UDP 5247) for 802.11 user traffic. All traffic goes through the WLC, enabling centralized policy, roaming, and management. APs in lightweight mode are called LAPs.

questions.question: What are the non-overlapping Wi-Fi channels for 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands?
options: Both bands have channels 1-13 with channels 1, 7, 13 non-overlapping
questions.explanation: 2.4 GHz band: channels 1-14 but only channels 1, 6, 11 are non-overlapping (each 22 MHz wide) in North America — essential to minimize co-channel interference. 5 GHz band: 24+ channels available (36, 40, 44, 48, 52, etc.), all non-overlapping because they're 20 MHz wide and spaced 5 MHz apart. 5 GHz has higher bandwidth but shorter range than 2.4 GHz.

questions.question: What is the difference between LACP and PAgP for EtherChannel negotiation?
options: LACP is Cisco proprietary; PAgP is IEEE 802.3ad standard
LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) is an open standard; PAgP is Cisco proprietary. Both negotiate EtherChannel formation between switches
LACP uses TCP; PAgP uses UDP for negotiation
LACP is for Layer 3; PAgP is for Layer 2 EtherChannel
questions.explanation: EtherChannel negotiation protocols: LACP (IEEE 802.3ad/802.1AX) — open standard, works between any vendor. Modes: Active (initiates), Passive (responds). PAgP (Port Aggregation Protocol) — Cisco proprietary, Cisco-to-Cisco only. Modes: Desirable (initiates), Auto (responds). Both require matching speed/duplex/VLAN settings. Static 'on' mode creates EtherChannel without negotiation.

questions.question: What is the Cisco troubleshooting methodology — what are the seven steps?
options: Ping → Trace → Debug → Reload → Call TAC → Replace hardware → Give up
Define problem → Gather info → Analyze info → Eliminate possible causes → Propose hypothesis → Test hypothesis → Solve/Document
Check Layer 1 → Check Layer 2 → Check Layer 3 → Check Routing → Check Application
Call TAC → Open ticket → Wait → Escalate → Resolve
questions.explanation: Cisco's structured troubleshooting: (1) Define the problem. (2) Gather information (show commands, logs). (3) Analyze the info. (4) Eliminate possible causes. (5) Propose a hypothesis (most likely cause). (6) Test the hypothesis (resolve or adjust). (7) Solve the problem and document. Approaches: bottom-up (OSI 1→7), top-down (7→1), divide-and-conquer (start at Layer 3). Always document changes.

questions.question: What Cisco IOS commands are used to test Layer 3 connectivity?
options: `ping` and `traceroute` — ping tests reachability, traceroute shows the path hop-by-hop
questions.explanation: `ping <ip>` sends ICMP Echo Requests and tests end-to-end reachability. A successful ping shows '!!!!!' (5 exclamation points = 5/5 success). `traceroute <ip>` shows each hop and its RTT. On Cisco: `traceroute` uses UDP probes (not ICMP as in Windows `tracert`). Extended ping: `ping` with no arguments enters interactive mode for more control (source IP, count, timeout).

questions.question: What are the three layers of the Cisco hierarchical network design model?
questions.explanation: Cisco's three-layer hierarchical model: Access Layer — connects end devices (PCs, phones, printers) to the network; provides port security, VLANs, PoE. Distribution Layer — aggregates access layer switches; enforces routing policies, ACLs, inter-VLAN routing; redundancy. Core Layer — high-speed backbone connecting distribution layers; optimized for speed, minimal policy processing. Collapsed core = distribution + core combined.

questions.question: What is STP PortFast and when should it be used?
options: A feature that speeds up STP on trunk ports between switches
A feature that transitions access ports directly to forwarding state (bypassing listening/learning), used on ports connected only to end devices
A feature that makes switches elect a new Root Bridge faster
A feature that enables rapid renegotiation of trunk links
questions.explanation: PortFast skips the STP Listening (15s) and Learning (15s) states, allowing a port to go directly to Forwarding. This prevents the 30-second delay that would cause DHCP timeouts or login failures on end devices. IMPORTANT: Use ONLY on access ports connected to end devices, NEVER on switch-to-switch links (would cause topology changes). Configured with: `spanning-tree portfast` on the interface, or `spanning-tree portfast default` globally.

questions.question: What is BPDU Guard and what does it do when a BPDU is received?
options: Prevents BPDUs from being sent; shuts down the switch if BPDUs exceed threshold
Protects PortFast ports — if a BPDU is received (indicating a switch connected), the port is immediately err-disabled
A feature that encrypts BPDUs for security
Blocks all BPDUs from non-root switches
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard protects PortFast ports from rogue switches. If a BPDU is received on a PortFast-enabled port, BPDU Guard immediately puts the port in err-disabled state — preventing that switch from participating in STP and potentially becoming Root Bridge. Configured with: `spanning-tree bpduguard enable` per-port, or `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` globally. Re-enable with shutdown/no shutdown.

questions.question: What is SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration) in IPv6?
options: A DHCPv6 server that assigns addresses to clients
A method where IPv6 hosts automatically generate their own global unicast address using Router Advertisements (prefix) + EUI-64 or random interface ID
A protocol that provides DNS server addresses to IPv6 hosts
A security mechanism that prevents duplicate IPv6 addresses
questions.explanation: SLAAC allows IPv6 hosts to self-configure without a DHCPv6 server. Process: (1) Host sends Router Solicitation (RS) to FF02::2 (all-routers). (2) Router replies with Router Advertisement (RA) containing the network prefix (e.g., 2001:db8::/64). (3) Host combines prefix + EUI-64 (from MAC) or random interface ID. (4) Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) verifies uniqueness. No server needed — hence 'stateless'.

questions.question: Compare RIPv2, OSPF, and EIGRP on these dimensions: metric, max hops, and classification.
options: All three use hop count as metric; only max hops differ
questions.explanation: Comparison: RIPv2 — metric=hop count, max=15 (16=unreachable), distance-vector, updates every 30s, AD=120. OSPF — metric=cost (100M/bandwidth), no hop limit, link-state (builds full topology map), SPF algorithm, AD=110. EIGRP — metric=composite (bandwidth+delay primarily), max hops=224, advanced distance-vector (DUAL algorithm), fast convergence, Cisco proprietary, AD=90 (internal) /170 (external).

questions.question: What is the difference between WPA2 Personal (PSK) and WPA2 Enterprise?
options: WPA2 Personal uses AES; WPA2 Enterprise uses TKIP
WPA2 Personal uses a shared password (Pre-Shared Key) for all users; WPA2 Enterprise requires individual authentication via 802.1X/EAP with a RADIUS server
WPA2 Enterprise is less secure but easier to deploy
WPA2 Personal is for businesses; WPA2 Enterprise is for home users
questions.explanation: WPA2 Personal (PSK): single shared key configured on all clients and the AP — simple but risky (one user leaves, all must change key). WPA2 Enterprise: each user authenticates individually using 802.1X/EAP to a RADIUS server — unique per-user credentials, centralized management, can revoke individual access without changing the entire network key. Both use AES-CCMP encryption. Enterprise is required for corporate environments.

questions.question: What Cisco IOS command enables logging to a syslog server and sets the logging level?
questions.explanation: `logging <ip-address>` specifies the syslog server. `logging trap <level>` sets which severity levels to send (e.g., `logging trap informational` = levels 0-6). `logging buffered <size> <level>` saves logs to RAM. `logging console <level>` controls console output. Common levels: emergencies(0), alerts(1), critical(2), errors(3), warnings(4), notifications(5), informational(6), debugging(7).

questions.question: What is SNMP v3 and how does it improve over SNMPv1/v2c?
options: SNMPv3 uses TCP instead of UDP for reliability
SNMPv3 adds authentication (MD5/SHA), privacy (AES/DES encryption), and access control — v1/v2c only used community strings with no encryption
SNMPv3 uses HTTPS instead of UDP 161
SNMPv3 only improves performance; security is unchanged from v2c
questions.explanation: SNMPv3 security improvements: Authentication — verifies the sender (MD5 or SHA). Privacy — encrypts SNMP messages (DES or AES). Access control — restricts which operations are allowed. v1/v2c use community strings (plaintext passwords) with no encryption — 'public' and 'private' are common defaults. SNMPv3 security levels: noAuthNoPriv (no auth/encrypt), authNoPriv (auth only), authPriv (both — recommended for production).

questions.question: Which Cisco router memory type stores the startup configuration file?
questions.explanation: NVRAM (Non-Volatile RAM) stores the startup-config. RAM holds the running-config and loaded IOS. Flash stores the IOS image. ROM holds bootstrap/POST and ROM monitor.

questions.question: Which router memory type stores the currently running IOS image and the running configuration?
questions.explanation: RAM (main memory) holds the running IOS image, running-config, routing tables, and ARP cache. Contents are lost on power-off. Flash holds the stored IOS image. NVRAM holds startup-config.

questions.question: What is stored in Flash memory on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: Flash memory stores the Cisco IOS software image(s). It is persistent (retains content without power) and typically uses PCMCIA cards or SIMMs. NVRAM stores startup-config; RAM stores the running IOS and running-config.

questions.question: What is the correct default Cisco IOS boot sequence for loading the IOS image?
questions.explanation: By default, the router first looks in Flash for an IOS image, then tries a TFTP server (if configured), and finally falls back to ROM (ROM monitor). The `boot system` commands in startup-config can change this order.

questions.question: Which command saves the running configuration to NVRAM?
questions.explanation: `copy running-config startup-config` (or older `write memory`) saves the current running config from RAM to NVRAM, making it the new startup-config. The reverse (`copy startup-config running-config`) merges startup-config into RAM.

questions.question: What is the difference between `enable password` and `enable secret` on a Cisco router?
options: They are identical; `enable secret` is just newer syntax
`enable secret` uses MD5 encryption; `enable password` is stored in cleartext by default
`enable password` uses MD5 encryption; `enable secret` is stored in cleartext
`enable secret` applies only to console access
questions.explanation: `enable secret` uses a Cisco-proprietary one-way MD5 hash and is always encrypted in the config. `enable password` is stored in cleartext unless `service password-encryption` is configured. When both are set, `enable secret` takes precedence.

questions.question: What metric does RIP use to determine the best path?
questions.explanation: RIP uses hop count as its only metric. A hop count of 15 is the maximum; 16 is considered unreachable (infinity). This simple metric makes RIP unsuitable for large networks but easy to configure.

questions.question: How often does RIP broadcast its full routing table updates by default?
questions.explanation: RIP broadcasts its complete routing table every 30 seconds by default. This periodic flooding is a characteristic of distance-vector protocols. IGRP updates every 90 seconds; OSPF and EIGRP use event-triggered updates.

questions.question: What is the purpose of split horizon in distance-vector routing?
options: It divides large networks into smaller segments
It prevents routing information from being sent back out the interface it was received on
It assigns a maximum hop count of 15 to prevent loops
It sends poison reverse updates to all neighbors
questions.explanation: Split horizon prevents a router from advertising a route back through the same interface it learned the route from. This prevents two-node routing loops. The rule: 'it is never useful to send information about a route back in the direction from which it came.'

questions.question: What is the purpose of hold-down timers in RIP?
options: To delay routing updates for 30 seconds on startup
To prevent a route from being reinstated prematurely after it has gone down
To hold routing table entries for 90 seconds before deletion
To time the interval between full routing table broadcasts
questions.explanation: Hold-down timers prevent incorrect routing updates from reinstating a downed route. When a route goes bad, the router holds down changes to that route for a period slightly longer than the time needed to propagate the failure throughout the network. This prevents the count-to-infinity problem.

questions.question: Which algorithm do link-state routing protocols such as OSPF use to calculate the shortest path?
questions.explanation: Link-state protocols like OSPF use Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm to calculate the best loop-free path. Distance-vector protocols like RIP and IGRP use the Bellman-Ford algorithm. EIGRP uses DUAL (Diffusing Update ALGorithm).

questions.question: Which tool verifies application-layer connectivity between source and destination?
questions.explanation: Telnet verifies application-layer (Layer 7) connectivity because it uses the full TCP/IP stack. Ping uses ICMP to verify Layer 3 (network layer) connectivity. Traceroute uses TTL expiry to identify routers along the path.

questions.question: Which switching mode forwards the frame as soon as the destination MAC address is read, without waiting for the full frame?
questions.explanation: Cut-through switching begins forwarding the frame immediately after reading the destination MAC address (first 6 bytes). This minimizes latency but passes corrupt frames since no CRC check is performed. Store-and-forward waits for the entire frame and checks the CRC.

questions.question: Which switching mode receives the complete frame, checks the CRC, and only then forwards it?
questions.explanation: Store-and-forward switching receives the entire frame before forwarding. It checks the CRC (cyclic redundancy check) and discards corrupt frames. This mode has higher latency than cut-through but guarantees error-free forwarding.

questions.question: Which switching mode waits until the first 64 bytes of a frame are received before forwarding?
questions.explanation: Fragment-free (also called modified cut-through) waits for the first 64 bytes — the collision window — before forwarding. Most frame errors and collisions occur within the first 64 bytes. This provides better error detection than cut-through with only slightly more latency.

questions.question: What is a key characteristic of full-duplex Ethernet?
options: Both stations share a single collision domain
CSMA/CD is still required for collision detection
Frames can be transmitted and received simultaneously, creating a collision-free environment
Maximum bandwidth is half of the rated link speed
questions.explanation: Full-duplex Ethernet uses separate transmit and receive circuits, allowing simultaneous transmission in both directions with no collisions. CSMA/CD is disabled in full-duplex mode. Each full-duplex connection offers 100% efficiency in both directions.

questions.question: At which OSI layer does a bridge operate?
questions.explanation: Bridges operate at Layer 2 (Data Link). They learn MAC addresses by examining source addresses of incoming frames and build a MAC address table. Bridges are protocol-independent and transparent to end users. Routers operate at Layer 3.
questions.source_ref: CCNA CramGuide - LAN Segmentation with Bridges

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)?
options: To provide load balancing across redundant links
To provide path redundancy while preventing layer 2 loops
To elect a root bridge for each VLAN
To assign VLANs to switch ports
questions.explanation: STP (IEEE 802.1D) prevents Layer 2 loops while maintaining path redundancy. It calculates a loop-free tree topology by blocking redundant paths. If the active path fails, STP activates the standby path. Without STP, broadcast storms and MAC table instability occur in redundant switched networks.

questions.question: What IEEE standard defines Fast Ethernet?
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.3u defines Fast Ethernet (100 Mbps). It uses CSMA/CD like standard 10 Mbps Ethernet (802.3) but operates 10 times faster. 802.3ab is Gigabit Ethernet over copper (1000BASE-T). 802.3z is Gigabit Ethernet over fiber.

questions.question: What is the number range for standard IP access lists?
questions.explanation: Standard IP access lists use numbers 1–99 (and 1300–1999 for extended range). Extended IP ACLs use 100–199 (and 2000–2699). Standard ACLs filter based on source IP address only. Extended ACLs can filter on source/destination IP, protocol, and port numbers.

questions.question: What does a standard IP access list filter on?
options: Source IP, destination IP, protocol, and port
questions.explanation: Standard IP access lists (1–99) filter only on the source IP address. Extended ACLs (100–199) can match on source IP, destination IP, protocol type, and port numbers, giving administrators much finer control.
questions.source_ref: CCNA CramGuide - Standard vs Extended ACLs

questions.question: In an ACL wildcard mask, what does a binary 0 bit mean?
options: Ignore the corresponding bit in the address
Check (match) the corresponding bit in the address
The bit must be set to 1
The bit can be any value
questions.explanation: In wildcard masks: bit 0 means 'check/match this bit' and bit 1 means 'ignore this bit.' This is the opposite of subnet masks. For example, wildcard 0.0.0.255 means check the first 3 octets exactly and ignore the last octet (matches any host in that /24 network).

questions.question: In NAT terminology, what does 'inside global' refer to?
options: Private IP address of an inside host as seen on the inside network
Public IP address representing an inside host on the outside network
Private IP address of an outside host
questions.explanation: NAT address types: Inside local = private IP of inside host (e.g., 192.168.1.10). Inside global = public IP representing the inside host on the Internet (e.g., 203.0.113.5). Outside global = public IP of the remote server. Outside local = how the outside host appears on the inside network.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the DHCP DORA process?
questions.explanation: DHCP uses the DORA process: 1) Discover (client broadcasts to find a DHCP server), 2) Offer (server responds with available IP), 3) Request (client requests the offered IP), 4) Acknowledge (server confirms the lease). The Discover and Request messages are broadcasts; Offer and Acknowledge are unicast (or broadcast).

questions.question: What does the NTP stratum level indicate?
options: The priority of the NTP server in elections
The distance (number of hops) from a reference clock source
The version of NTP protocol being used
The authentication strength of the NTP server
questions.explanation: NTP stratum indicates distance from the reference time source. Stratum 0 = atomic clocks / GPS (reference clocks, not on network). Stratum 1 = directly connected to stratum 0. Each hop adds 1 to the stratum. Lower stratum = more accurate. Stratum 16 = unsynchronized.

questions.question: What are the syslog severity levels from most to least severe?
questions.explanation: Syslog has 8 severity levels 0–7: 0=Emergency, 1=Alert, 2=Critical, 3=Error, 4=Warning, 5=Notice, 6=Informational, 7=Debug. Lower number = more severe. Setting a logging level captures all messages at that level AND more severe levels.

questions.question: What is the difference between traffic policing and traffic shaping in QoS?
options: Policing buffers excess traffic; shaping drops it
Policing drops excess traffic; shaping buffers and delays it
Policing marks traffic; shaping encrypts it
They are identical; just different terms
questions.explanation: Traffic policing drops (or re-marks) packets that exceed the defined rate — no buffering. Traffic shaping buffers excess packets in a queue and transmits them when bandwidth is available, smoothing the traffic rate. Shaping adds latency but avoids drops; policing can cause TCP retransmissions.

questions.question: In HSRP, which router becomes the Active router by default?
options: The router with the lowest priority
The router with the highest priority (default 100), or highest IP if tied
The router with the lowest IP address
questions.explanation: The HSRP Active router is the one with the highest priority (default is 100). If priorities are tied, the router with the highest IP address on the HSRP interface wins. The Standby router monitors the Active and takes over if it fails. Use `standby <group> preempt` to allow a higher-priority router to reclaim Active status.

questions.question: What UDP ports does SNMP use for polls (manager queries) and traps (agent alerts)?
options: UDP 161 for polls, UDP 162 for traps
UDP 162 for polls, UDP 161 for traps
TCP 161 for polls, TCP 162 for traps
UDP 514 for polls, UDP 162 for traps
questions.explanation: SNMP uses UDP 161 for manager-to-agent queries (Get/Set) and UDP 162 for agent-to-manager traps (unsolicited alerts). Syslog uses UDP 514. SNMP is connectionless (UDP) by design for lightweight monitoring. SNMPv3 adds authentication and encryption over the same ports.

questions.question: Where should standard IP ACLs be placed relative to the traffic source?
options: As close to the destination as possible
As close to the source as possible
On every interface in the network
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs filter only on source IP, so placing them close to the destination prevents them from blocking too much traffic (they can't be selective about destination). Extended ACLs, which can match source AND destination, should be placed close to the source to block traffic early and save bandwidth.

questions.question: In 802.1X port-based authentication, what are the three roles?
questions.explanation: 802.1X uses three roles: Supplicant (end device requesting access), Authenticator (network device, typically a switch, that controls port access), and Authentication Server (typically a RADIUS server that validates credentials). EAP carries authentication between supplicant and server through the authenticator.

questions.question: What does the `sticky` keyword do in a port security configuration?
options: Prevents the port from going to err-disable state
Dynamically learns MAC addresses and saves them to the running-config
Allows only statically configured MAC addresses
Enables the port to accept unlimited MAC addresses
questions.explanation: The `switchport port-security mac-address sticky` command enables sticky learning: the switch dynamically learns MAC addresses on the port and converts them to sticky secure addresses, saving them to the running-config (and startup-config if saved). This is easier than manually typing MAC addresses.

questions.question: What type of attack involves an attacker intercepting and potentially altering communication between two parties without their knowledge?
questions.explanation: A Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack occurs when an attacker secretly relays and possibly alters communication between two parties who believe they are communicating directly. ARP spoofing is a common technique used for MITM attacks on local networks.

questions.question: What is phishing?
options: Flooding a network with traffic to cause a denial of service
Social engineering via email or fake websites to steal credentials
Scanning a network to discover open ports and services
questions.explanation: Phishing is a social engineering attack where attackers send deceptive emails or create fake websites to trick users into revealing sensitive information like passwords or credit card numbers. Spear phishing targets specific individuals. Vishing uses voice calls.

questions.question: What is the difference between a site-to-site VPN and a remote-access VPN?
options: Site-to-site uses SSL; remote-access uses IPsec
Site-to-site connects two networks permanently; remote-access connects individual users to the network
Site-to-site is less secure than remote-access
questions.explanation: A site-to-site VPN creates a permanent encrypted tunnel between two networks (e.g., branch office to HQ). A remote-access VPN allows individual users (telecommuters) to securely connect to the corporate network from any location. Both typically use IPsec or SSL/TLS.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of a stateful firewall over a stateless packet filter?
options: It operates faster because it doesn't maintain any tables
It tracks the state of TCP connections and allows return traffic automatically
It can filter based on application content
questions.explanation: A stateful firewall maintains a connection state table and tracks active TCP/UDP sessions. It automatically allows return traffic for established connections without needing explicit rules. A stateless packet filter inspects each packet independently without context. Next-Generation Firewalls (NGFW) add application-level inspection (Layer 7).

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of DHCP snooping on a switch?
options: To speed up DHCP address allocation
To prevent rogue DHCP servers from assigning addresses on the network
To enable DHCP relay across VLANs
To limit the number of DHCP leases per VLAN
questions.explanation: DHCP snooping protects against rogue DHCP servers. It classifies switch ports as trusted (uplinks to legitimate DHCP servers) or untrusted (client access ports). DHCP Offer/Ack messages from untrusted ports are dropped. It also builds a binding table used by DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection).

questions.question: What does Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) protect against?
options: Duplicate IP addresses on the same subnet
questions.explanation: DAI prevents ARP spoofing/poisoning by validating ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table (IP-to-MAC-to-port mappings). If an ARP reply's IP-to-MAC mapping doesn't match the binding table, the packet is dropped. This prevents attackers from redirecting traffic by sending false ARP replies.

questions.question: What is required before you can generate RSA keys for SSH on a Cisco IOS device?
options: An enable secret must be set
A hostname and domain name must be configured
VTY lines must be configured for local login
A crypto policy must be applied
questions.explanation: To use SSH on Cisco IOS, you must: 1) Set a hostname (not 'Router'), 2) Set a domain name (`ip domain-name`), 3) Generate RSA keys (`crypto key generate rsa`), 4) Configure VTY lines to use SSH (`transport input ssh`) with local or AAA authentication. The hostname + domain name are required because they form the RSA key name.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key difference between RADIUS and TACACS+?
options: RADIUS uses TCP; TACACS+ uses UDP
TACACS+ encrypts the entire payload; RADIUS encrypts only the password
RADIUS supports command authorization; TACACS+ does not
TACACS+ is an open standard; RADIUS is proprietary
questions.explanation: TACACS+ (TCP 49) encrypts the entire packet body, making it more secure. RADIUS (UDP 1812/1813) only encrypts the password field. TACACS+ separates Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting into independent functions. RADIUS combines Authentication and Authorization. TACACS+ is Cisco proprietary; RADIUS is an IETF standard.

questions.question: What authentication method does WPA3-Personal use instead of the Pre-Shared Key (PSK) handshake?
questions.explanation: WPA3-Personal replaces the PSK 4-way handshake with Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE), also known as Dragonfly. SAE provides forward secrecy and is resistant to offline dictionary attacks — even if an attacker captures the handshake, they cannot brute-force the password offline.

questions.question: Which HTTP status code indicates that a REST API request succeeded and a new resource was created?
questions.explanation: HTTP status codes: 200 = OK (successful GET/PUT/PATCH), 201 = Created (successful POST that created a resource), 204 = No Content (successful DELETE), 400 = Bad Request (malformed syntax), 401 = Unauthorized, 403 = Forbidden, 404 = Not Found, 500 = Internal Server Error.

questions.question: What is the difference between HTTP PUT and PATCH methods in a REST API?
options: PUT deletes a resource; PATCH creates it
PUT replaces the entire resource; PATCH updates only specified fields
PUT is read-only; PATCH makes changes
questions.explanation: PUT replaces the entire resource with the provided data (idempotent full replace). PATCH partially updates an existing resource, modifying only the fields provided. GET retrieves data, POST creates new resources, DELETE removes resources. PUT and DELETE are idempotent (same result regardless of how many times called).

questions.question: Which of the following best describes Ansible's architecture?
options: Agent-based; requires an agent installed on each managed node
Agentless; uses SSH (or APIs) to push configurations from a control node
Pull-based; managed nodes pull configs from a server
questions.explanation: Ansible is agentless — no software needs to be installed on managed devices. It uses SSH (or APIs for network devices) to push configurations from the control node. It uses YAML playbooks and an inventory file. Puppet uses agents (pull-based, TCP 8140). Chef also uses agents. Ansible is simplest to set up.

questions.question: How does Puppet manage device configurations?
options: Agentless SSH push from control node
Agent-based pull: agents request catalogs from the Puppet master
questions.explanation: Puppet uses an agent-based pull model: agents installed on managed nodes periodically pull configuration catalogs from the Puppet master server. Configurations are written in Puppet's Ruby-based DSL (manifests). Communication uses TCP 8140. This is the opposite of Ansible's agentless push model.

questions.question: Which transport protocol and port does NETCONF use?
questions.explanation: NETCONF (Network Configuration Protocol) uses SSH over TCP port 830. It uses XML for data encoding and YANG for data modeling. NETCONF operations include get, get-config, edit-config, and commit. RESTCONF is a REST-based alternative that runs over HTTPS and supports both XML and JSON.

questions.question: What is the purpose of YANG in network automation?
options: A scripting language for writing automation playbooks
A data modeling language that defines the structure of network configuration and state data
A network protocol for delivering configurations to devices
A GUI tool for managing network automation workflows
questions.explanation: YANG (Yet Another Next Generation) is a data modeling language used to define the structure, syntax, and semantics of network configuration and operational data. YANG models are used by both NETCONF and RESTCONF to describe what data is available on a device. Think of YANG as the schema that defines what you can GET or SET.

questions.question: In SDN architecture, what does the northbound API connect?
options: The SDN controller to physical network devices
The SDN controller to applications/orchestration systems
The data plane to the control plane
questions.explanation: SDN APIs: Northbound APIs (e.g., REST) connect the SDN controller to applications and orchestration systems (north = toward applications). Southbound APIs (e.g., OpenFlow, NETCONF) connect the controller to the network devices (south = toward infrastructure). The controller provides a centralized view of the entire network.

questions.question: Which of the following is valid JSON syntax?
questions.explanation: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) uses double-quoted strings as keys and values, with objects in curly braces {} and arrays in square brackets []. Option A is XML. Option B is JavaScript object literal notation (not valid JSON — keys must be quoted). Option D uses TOML/INI-style syntax. JSON is used by RESTCONF and REST APIs.

questions.question: What is Cisco DNA Center (Catalyst Center) primarily used for?
options: Intent-based networking: automated network management, assurance, and policy enforcement
questions.explanation: Cisco DNA Center (now Catalyst Center) is Cisco's Intent-Based Networking (IBN) controller for on-premises enterprise networks. It provides: network automation (provision devices via GUI or API), assurance (AI/ML analytics), security policy (group-based access), and SD-Access fabric management. It uses REST APIs for integration.

questions.question: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
options: Compiling network device firmware from source code
Managing and provisioning infrastructure through version-controlled machine-readable definition files
Using CLI scripts to configure devices manually
Converting network diagrams to configuration files
questions.explanation: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) manages infrastructure (networks, servers, etc.) through declarative or procedural definition files stored in version control (e.g., Git). This enables consistent, repeatable provisioning, automated testing, and rollback. Tools like Terraform, Ansible, and Puppet implement IaC principles.

questions.question: In Cisco SD-WAN, what is the role of vManage?
options: Data plane — forwards actual traffic between sites
Centralized management plane — provides GUI and APIs for configuration, monitoring
Orchestration plane — authenticates and onboards new devices
Control plane — distributes routing and policy information
questions.explanation: Cisco SD-WAN components: vManage = centralized management GUI/API (NMS), vSmart = control plane (distributes policies/routes), vBond = orchestration (authenticates new devices), vEdge/cEdge = data plane (edge routers). The management, control, and data planes are separated in SD-WAN.

questions.question: What Python library is commonly used to make HTTP/REST API calls to network devices?
questions.explanation: The `requests` library is the standard Python library for HTTP/REST API calls. It supports GET, POST, PUT, DELETE with simple syntax. `paramiko` is for SSH connections. `netmiko` is built on paramiko for multi-vendor SSH CLI automation. `ncclient` is for NETCONF. For REST APIs (RESTCONF, DNA Center), `requests` is the go-to library.

questions.question: What is a zero-day vulnerability?
options: A vulnerability that has been known for exactly one day
A vulnerability that is unknown to the vendor and has no available patch
A vulnerability that only affects old (zero-support) software
A vulnerability that requires zero user interaction to exploit
questions.explanation: A zero-day vulnerability is a software flaw that is unknown to the software vendor and/or the public, for which no patch or fix is available. Attackers who discover zero-days can exploit them with no defense available until the vendor releases a patch. The term refers to the 'zero days' the vendor has had to fix it.

questions.question: What is the key operational difference between an IDS and an IPS?
options: IDS uses signatures; IPS uses anomaly detection
IDS is inline and can block traffic; IPS only monitors and alerts
IDS passively monitors and alerts; IPS is inline and can block traffic
questions.explanation: IDS (Intrusion Detection System) operates out-of-band — it receives a copy of traffic, detects threats, and generates alerts but cannot block traffic. IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) is inline — all traffic passes through it, allowing it to detect AND actively block/drop malicious traffic in real time.

questions.question: What is VLAN hopping and how does double-tagging exploit it?
options: Moving a port between VLANs; double-tagging adds two 802.1Q headers to bypass trunk filtering
Creating VLANs from the CLI; double-tagging uses two VLAN IDs simultaneously
Trunking between VLANs; double-tagging uses STP to hop between VLANs
Accessing VLANs via SSH; double-tagging exploits SSH key weakness
questions.explanation: VLAN hopping allows traffic to reach unauthorized VLANs. Double-tagging: attacker sends a frame with two 802.1Q tags (outer = native VLAN, inner = target VLAN). The first switch strips the outer tag (native VLAN is untagged on trunks) and forwards the inner-tagged frame to the target VLAN. Mitigation: change the native VLAN to an unused VLAN.

questions.question: What happens to traffic that does not match any ACL entry?
options: It is dropped by the implicit deny any
It is sent to the next router for processing
questions.explanation: Every Cisco ACL has an implicit 'deny any' (deny all) at the end that is not shown in the configuration. Traffic that does not match any explicit permit or deny statement is dropped. This is a critical security principle — if you apply an ACL and don't have at least one permit statement, ALL traffic will be blocked.

questions.question: Which routing source has the lowest (most trusted) administrative distance?
questions.explanation: Administrative Distance (AD) indicates trustworthiness: lower = more trusted. Directly connected = 0, Static = 1, EIGRP summary = 5, BGP external = 20, EIGRP internal = 90, IGRP = 100, OSPF = 110, RIP = 120. A directly connected network is the most trusted. A higher AD means the router prefers routes from other sources.

questions.question: What is the OSPF cost formula and what is the default reference bandwidth?
questions.explanation: OSPF cost = Reference Bandwidth / Interface Bandwidth. Default reference bandwidth is 100 Mbps (100,000,000 bps). FastEthernet (100 Mbps) = cost 1, Ethernet (10 Mbps) = cost 10. Problem: GigE and faster all get cost 1 by default. Use `auto-cost reference-bandwidth 1000` to differentiate GigE links (cost 1) from FastEthernet (cost 10).

questions.question: Which command assigns a switchport to VLAN 10 as an access port?
options:
switchport mode trunk
switchport trunk allowed vlan 10

switchport mode access
switchport access vlan 10
questions.explanation: To configure an access port: enter interface config mode, use `switchport mode access` to set it as access, then `switchport access vlan 10` to assign it to VLAN 10. The VLAN must also exist in the VLAN database (`vlan 10` in global config). Trunk ports use `switchport mode trunk` and can carry multiple VLANs.

questions.question: At which OSI layer does a router operate?
questions.explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network). They make forwarding decisions based on IP addresses (logical addresses). Switches operate at Layer 2 (MAC addresses). Hubs at Layer 1 (physical signal). Firewalls can operate at Layers 3-7.

questions.question: What is the correct top-to-bottom order of the OSI model layers?
options: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical
Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Application, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical, Session, Presentation
Application, Session, Presentation, Network, Transport, Data Link, Physical
questions.explanation: OSI layers top-to-bottom: 7-Application, 6-Presentation, 5-Session, 4-Transport, 3-Network, 2-Data Link, 1-Physical. Mnemonic top-to-bottom: 'All People Seem To Need Data Processing.' Bottom-to-top: 'Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away.'

questions.question: What is the correct name for the PDU (Protocol Data Unit) at the Transport layer?
questions.explanation: PDU names by layer: Layer 7-5 = Data, Layer 4 (Transport) = Segment, Layer 3 (Network) = Packet, Layer 2 (Data Link) = Frame, Layer 1 (Physical) = Bit. TCP uses Segments; UDP also produces Segments (or Datagrams). IP produces Packets. Ethernet produces Frames.

questions.question: When would you use a crossover cable to connect two devices directly?
questions.explanation: Crossover cables connect like devices: switch-to-switch, hub-to-hub, PC-to-PC, router-to-router (same type). Straight-through cables connect unlike devices: PC-to-switch, switch-to-router. Modern devices with Auto-MDI/MDIX can auto-detect and adjust, making the cable type less critical. Fiber uses SC/LC/ST connectors.

questions.question: A host has IP 192.168.10.65 with subnet mask 255.255.255.192. What is the network address?
questions.explanation: /26 = 255.255.255.192. Block size = 256 - 192 = 64. Subnets: .0, .64, .128, .192. Host .65 falls in the .64 subnet. Network address = 192.168.10.64, Broadcast = 192.168.10.127, Usable range = .65 to .126.

questions.question: How many usable host addresses does a /25 subnet provide?
questions.explanation: /25 = 255.255.255.128. 2^7 = 128 total addresses. Usable hosts = 128 - 2 = 126 (subtract network address and broadcast). Block size = 256 - 128 = 128. Compare: /24 = 254 hosts, /25 = 126, /26 = 62, /27 = 30, /28 = 14, /29 = 6, /30 = 2.

questions.question: You need to subnet 10.0.0.0/8 to support 500 hosts per subnet. Which subnet mask should you use?
questions.explanation: /23 = 2^9 - 2 = 510 usable hosts (sufficient for 500). /24 = 2^8 - 2 = 254 hosts (too few). /22 = 2^10 - 2 = 1022 hosts (works but wastes addresses). Choose the smallest block that still accommodates the requirement: /23 is optimal.

questions.question: What is the broadcast address for the subnet 172.16.4.0/22?
What metric components does EIGRP use by default?
options: Bandwidth, delay, load, reliability, and MTU
questions.explanation: EIGRP uses bandwidth and delay by default in its composite metric formula. Load, reliability, and MTU are also available (K-values) but disabled by default (K1=1, K2=0, K3=1, K4=0, K5=0). EIGRP's metric uses: Metric = [K1×BW + (K2×BW)/(256-load) + K3×Delay] × [K5/(reliability+K4)].

questions.question: What is the OSPF Router ID and how is it selected?
options: The IP address of the highest-numbered interface
Manually configured, or highest loopback IP, or highest active interface IP
The IP address of the management interface
questions.explanation: OSPF Router ID selection priority: 1) Manually configured (`router-id` command), 2) Highest IP on a loopback interface, 3) Highest IP on any active interface at OSPF startup. The Router ID uniquely identifies the OSPF router in the LSDB. Loopback interfaces are preferred because they never go down.

questions.question: What is the role of the Designated Router (DR) in OSPF?
options: The DR is the router with the highest priority that becomes the active router
The DR reduces LSA flooding by acting as a central point for OSPF adjacencies on multi-access networks
The DR is responsible for propagating default routes
The DR processes all inter-area routing
questions.explanation: On multi-access networks (Ethernet), OSPF elects a DR and BDR to reduce the number of OSPF adjacencies and LSA flooding. All routers form adjacency with the DR and BDR. The DR collects and distributes LSAs. Without DR/BDR, n routers would need n(n-1)/2 adjacencies. DR election: highest priority (default 1), then highest Router ID.

questions.question: What command configures a default route pointing to next-hop 203.0.113.1?
questions.explanation: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>` configures a default route (gateway of last resort). 0.0.0.0/0 matches all destinations. `ip default-gateway` is used on switches (not routers) when IP routing is disabled. The default route is used when no more specific route exists in the routing table.

questions.question: What encapsulation protocol is used for VLAN tagging on modern Cisco switches?
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1Q is the industry-standard VLAN tagging protocol. It inserts a 4-byte tag into the Ethernet frame header, including a 12-bit VLAN ID field (supporting 4094 VLANs). ISL was Cisco's proprietary predecessor but is now deprecated. VTP manages VLAN databases. DTP negotiates trunk links.

questions.question: What is 'Router-on-a-Stick' inter-VLAN routing?
options: Using multiple physical router interfaces, one per VLAN
Using a single router interface with subinterfaces, each with 802.1Q encapsulation
Using a Layer 3 switch with SVIs for each VLAN
Connecting VLANs through a dedicated WAN link
questions.explanation: Router-on-a-Stick uses a single physical router interface connected to a trunk port on a switch. The router has logical subinterfaces (e.g., Gi0/0.10, Gi0/0.20), each with `encapsulation dot1q <vlan-id>`. Inter-VLAN traffic goes through the switch trunk to the router and back. Alternatively, a Layer 3 switch with SVIs is faster and more scalable.

questions.question: What is the function of VTP (VLAN Trunking Protocol)?
options: It negotiates trunk ports between switches
It synchronizes the VLAN database across switches in a VTP domain
It tags Ethernet frames with VLAN IDs
It provides redundancy for trunk links
questions.explanation: VTP (Cisco proprietary) allows VLAN configuration to be synchronized across switches in a VTP domain. A VTP Server manages the VLAN database; VTP Clients receive and sync the database. VTP Transparent mode forwards VTP messages but doesn't sync. VTP is risky — a misconfigured switch can delete all VLANs network-wide. Many engineers disable VTP.

questions.question: What is EtherChannel?
options: A protocol for running Ethernet over fiber
Bundling multiple physical links into one logical link for increased bandwidth and redundancy
A method for segmenting Layer 2 domains
questions.explanation: EtherChannel (also called LAG - Link Aggregation Group) bundles 2-8 physical links into one logical link. Benefits: increased bandwidth (up to 8x), redundancy (traffic redistributes if a member fails), and STP sees it as one link (no blocking). Uses LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) or PAgP (Cisco proprietary) for negotiation.

questions.question: How is the STP Root Bridge elected?
options: The switch with the highest MAC address becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the lowest Bridge ID (priority + MAC) becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the most ports becomes Root Bridge
The Root Bridge is manually assigned only
questions.explanation: STP Root Bridge election: the switch with the lowest Bridge ID wins. Bridge ID = 2-byte Priority + 6-byte MAC address. Default priority = 32768. Ties broken by lowest MAC. To influence election, lower the priority (`spanning-tree vlan <x> priority 4096`). The Root Bridge sends BPDUs; all other switches calculate paths to root.

questions.question: What is PAT (Port Address Translation) and what problem does it solve?
options: PAT translates port numbers only; it does not translate IP addresses
PAT allows many private hosts to share a single public IP address using unique port numbers
PAT is a type of static NAT for server publishing
PAT translates MAC addresses at the network layer
questions.explanation: PAT (also called NAT Overload) maps multiple private IP addresses to a single public IP address by using unique source port numbers to distinguish sessions. This is how most home and enterprise internet connections work — thousands of hosts share one public IP. The NAT table tracks IP+port combinations.

questions.question: What command configures a Cisco router to relay DHCP broadcasts to a server at 10.1.1.1?
questions.explanation: `ip helper-address <server-ip>` configured on the client-facing interface causes the router to forward DHCP Discover broadcasts (UDP port 67/68) as unicasts to the DHCP server. This allows a single DHCP server to serve multiple subnets. The command also forwards several other broadcast protocols (TFTP, DNS, NTP, etc.).

questions.question: Which FHRP is an open standard (non-Cisco proprietary)?
questions.explanation: VRRP (Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol, RFC 5798) is the open IEEE/IETF standard for First Hop Redundancy. HSRP is Cisco proprietary. GLBP is Cisco proprietary (also provides load balancing). VRRP terms: Master router (active), Backup routers. Default priority 100. The router with highest priority becomes Master.

questions.question: What field in the IP header does Differentiated Services (DiffServ) QoS use for traffic marking?
options: DSCP field (6 bits in the ToS/DSCP byte)
CoS field (3 bits in the 802.1Q tag)
questions.explanation: DiffServ uses the 6-bit DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point) field in the IP header's ToS byte. This allows 64 traffic classes. Common DSCP markings: EF (Expedited Forwarding, 46) for VoIP, AF classes for data, CS0 = best effort. CoS (802.1Q) is a 3-bit Layer 2 marking in the VLAN tag. DSCP is copied to CoS at trust boundaries.

questions.question: Which wireless security protocol provides the strongest encryption for enterprise networks?
questions.explanation: Security ranking strongest to weakest: WPA3 > WPA2 (AES/CCMP) > WPA (TKIP) > WEP. WEP uses RC4 with known vulnerabilities and should never be used. WPA uses TKIP (still RC4 based). WPA2 uses AES-based CCMP, which is currently strong. WPA3 adds SAE and 192-bit security suite for enhanced protection.

questions.question: What is a DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network security?
options: A military-restricted network zone with no civilian access
A network segment between internal and external firewalls, hosting public-facing services
A VLAN reserved for security monitoring tools
A NAT zone where private IPs are demilitarized
questions.explanation: A DMZ is a network segment that sits between two firewalls (or uses a multi-homed firewall). It hosts servers that need to be accessible from the Internet (web servers, mail servers, DNS) while keeping the internal corporate network isolated. Traffic from the Internet can reach the DMZ but not directly reach the internal network.

questions.question: How does RESTCONF differ from NETCONF?
options: RESTCONF uses XML only; NETCONF uses JSON only
RESTCONF uses HTTP/HTTPS methods (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE); NETCONF uses SSH with XML RPCs
RESTCONF is for physical devices; NETCONF is for virtual
They are identical protocols with different names
questions.explanation: RESTCONF (RFC 8040) maps NETCONF operations to REST-style HTTP methods over HTTPS. It supports both JSON and XML. NETCONF uses SSH (port 830) with XML-encoded RPCs. Both use YANG data models. RESTCONF is easier to integrate with web applications; NETCONF provides full transaction support with commit/rollback.

questions.question: In traditional networking, what is the 'data plane'?
options: The management plane where administrators configure devices
The control plane where routing protocols run
The forwarding plane that actually moves packets based on the forwarding table
The application plane where network services run
questions.explanation: Network planes: Control plane = routing protocols, STP, ARP — decides where traffic goes. Data plane (forwarding plane) = actually forwards packets using the FIB/CAM table built by the control plane. Management plane = out-of-band management (SSH, SNMP, syslog). In SDN, the control plane is centralized in the controller, separating it from the distributed data plane.

questions.question: What is a hypervisor and what types exist?
options: A network device that virtualizes VLANs; Type 1 is hardware, Type 2 is software
Software that creates/manages VMs; Type 1 runs on bare metal, Type 2 runs on top of a host OS
A software-defined network controller; Type 1 is centralized, Type 2 is distributed
A container orchestration platform; Type 1 uses Docker, Type 2 uses Kubernetes
questions.explanation: A hypervisor (VMM) creates and manages virtual machines. Type 1 (bare-metal): runs directly on hardware (VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM) — enterprise grade, more efficient. Type 2 (hosted): runs on top of a host OS (VMware Workstation, VirtualBox) — for desktop use. VMs enable server consolidation, rapid provisioning, and cloud computing.

questions.question: Which cloud service model gives customers the MOST control over the underlying infrastructure?
questions.explanation: IaaS gives the most customer control: customers manage OS, applications, middleware, and data. The provider manages physical hardware, networking, and virtualization. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute. PaaS: provider also manages OS and runtime (customer manages apps/data). SaaS: provider manages everything (customer only uses the app, e.g., Office 365, Salesforce).

questions.question: How many subnets and hosts per subnet does 172.16.0.0/20 provide?
questions.explanation: 172.16.0.0 is a Class B network (/16). Borrowing 4 bits gives /20. Subnets = 2^4 = 16. Hosts per subnet = 2^12 - 2 = 4094. Block size in third octet = 16. Subnets: 172.16.0.0, 172.16.16.0, 172.16.32.0... up to 172.16.240.0.

questions.question: What is VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)?
options: Using the same subnet mask for all subnets in a network
Assigning different subnet masks to different subnets to use address space efficiently
Varying the subnet mask based on traffic load
A technique for aggregating routes using summarization
questions.explanation: VLSM allows different subnets within the same network to have different prefix lengths, matching the actual number of hosts needed. For example: a WAN link gets /30 (2 hosts), a server subnet gets /26 (62 hosts), an office gets /24 (254 hosts). VLSM requires classless routing protocols (RIPv2, EIGRP, OSPF, BGP).

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of UDP but NOT TCP?
questions.explanation: UDP is connectionless — no 3-way handshake, no acknowledgments, no sequencing, no flow control. This makes UDP fast with minimal overhead. TCP has all of those features (connection setup, ACKs, sequencing, windowing). UDP is used for DNS, DHCP, TFTP, VoIP, video streaming where speed > reliability.

questions.question: What is the correct sequence of the TCP three-way handshake?
questions.explanation: TCP connection establishment: 1) Client sends SYN (synchronize), 2) Server responds with SYN-ACK (synchronize-acknowledge), 3) Client sends ACK (acknowledge). Connection is established. For termination: FIN → FIN-ACK → FIN → FIN-ACK (4-way). The handshake sets initial sequence numbers for both directions.

questions.question: Match these protocols to their correct port numbers: FTP, SSH, Telnet, SMTP
questions.explanation: Key port numbers: FTP data=20, FTP control=21, SSH=22, Telnet=23, SMTP=25, DNS=53, HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, POP3=110, IMAP=143, SNMP=161/162, RDP=3389. Also: DHCP server=67, DHCP client=68, TFTP=69, NTP=123, Syslog=514.

questions.question: Which port does HTTPS use?
questions.explanation: HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses TCP port 443. HTTP uses TCP port 80. Port 8080 is commonly used as an alternative HTTP port. HTTPS uses TLS/SSL to encrypt web traffic. When configuring ACLs, remember: `eq www` = port 80, `eq 443` for HTTPS.

questions.question: What summary route covers 192.168.0.0/24 through 192.168.3.0/24?
questions.explanation: 4 networks: 192.168.0.0, 192.168.1.0, 192.168.2.0, 192.168.3.0. In binary, these differ in the last 2 bits of the third octet (00, 01, 10, 11). The common prefix is the first 22 bits: 192.168.00xxxxxx. Summary = 192.168.0.0/22. This covers .0.0 to .3.255. /21 covers 8 networks (too broad).

questions.question: When multiple routes to the same destination exist, which one does a router prefer?
options: The route with the highest administrative distance
The route with the lowest administrative distance; if tied, lowest metric
The route with the most specific prefix (longest match)
The most specific prefix first, then lowest AD, then lowest metric
questions.explanation: Router route selection: 1) Longest prefix match (most specific route wins), 2) Among routes with same prefix length, lowest Administrative Distance wins, 3) Among routes with same AD, lowest metric wins. Example: a /28 route beats a /24 even if the /24 has lower AD, because it's more specific.

questions.question: Which command displays the IP routing table on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `show ip route` displays the IP routing table, including connected (C), static (S), RIP (R), OSPF (O), EIGRP (D), BGP (B) routes along with their AD, metric, next-hop, and outgoing interface. `show ip protocols` shows which routing protocols are running. `show arp` shows the ARP table.

questions.question: Which command shows all interfaces and their current status (up/down) and IP addresses?
questions.explanation: `show ip interface brief` provides a concise table of all interfaces with: interface name, IP address, OK? status, Method, Status (physical), and Protocol (Layer 2). It's the quickest way to verify interface status. `show interfaces` shows detailed statistics. `show ip interface` shows ACL/IP configuration per interface.

questions.question: What is the difference between an autonomous AP and a lightweight AP?
options: Autonomous APs are newer; lightweight APs are legacy devices
Autonomous APs are self-contained and configured independently; lightweight APs are managed by a WLC
Lightweight APs have more features; autonomous APs are stripped-down devices
questions.explanation: Autonomous APs are standalone, self-contained units — each requires individual configuration. Lightweight APs (LWAP) offload management to a Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) using CAPWAP tunnels. Lightweight APs have minimal intelligence; the WLC handles all configuration, roaming, RF management, and security policies. CAPWAP uses UDP 5246 (control) and UDP 5247 (data).

questions.question: Which 802.11 amendment operates ONLY in the 5 GHz band?
questions.explanation: 802.11a operates only in 5 GHz (up to 54 Mbps). 802.11b/g operate in 2.4 GHz. 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) operates in both 2.4 and 5 GHz. 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) operates only in 5 GHz. 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) operates in both 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz. 5 GHz has more non-overlapping channels but shorter range.

questions.question: In traditional STP (802.1D), a port moves through which sequence of states?
questions.explanation: 802.1D STP port states in order: Disabled (admin down) → Blocking (receiving BPDUs, not forwarding) → Listening (participating in election, 15 sec) → Learning (building MAC table, 15 sec) → Forwarding (normal operation). Convergence takes ~50 seconds. Rapid PVST+ (802.1w) reduces this to under 1 second.

questions.question: Which ACL matches HTTP traffic (port 80) from any source to host 10.1.1.1?
options: access-list 100 permit tcp any host 10.1.1.1 eq 80
access-list 100 permit ip any host 10.1.1.1 eq 80
questions.explanation: Extended ACL syntax: `access-list <100-199> permit|deny <protocol> <source> <dest> [operator port]`. HTTP is TCP port 80. `host 10.1.1.1` is the shorthand for `10.1.1.1 0.0.0.0`. ACL 99 would be standard (wrong range). `ip` as protocol doesn't filter by port. TCP must be specified for port matching.

questions.question: Which command shows the active NAT translation table on a Cisco router?
