questions.explanation: `ip routing` enables Layer 3 forwarding on a multilayer switch. Combined with SVIs (`interface vlan <id>` + IP address), the switch can route between VLANs without an external router.

questions.question: What is LLDP and why is it preferred over CDP in multi-vendor environments?
options: Link Layer Discovery Protocol; it is Cisco-proprietary
Link Layer Discovery Protocol; it is an IEEE 802.1AB standard supported by all vendors
Layer 2 Link Discovery Protocol; it works at Layer 3
Loop Link Detection Protocol; prevents STP loops
questions.explanation: LLDP (IEEE 802.1AB) is the vendor-neutral equivalent of Cisco's CDP. It discovers directly connected neighbor devices and their capabilities across all vendor platforms.

questions.question: What multicast address does HSRPv1 use for hello messages?
questions.explanation: HSRPv1 uses 224.0.0.2 (all routers). HSRPv2 uses 224.0.0.102. OSPF uses 224.0.0.5 (DR) and 224.0.0.6 (all DR/BDR). RIPv2 uses 224.0.0.9.

questions.question: Why is accurate NTP synchronization important for network security?
options: Accurate timestamps are critical for log correlation, certificate validation, and forensic analysis
It enables DHCP to assign correct lease times
questions.explanation: Accurate timestamps are essential for: correlating security logs across devices, validating digital certificates (expiry checks), and forensic investigation. NTP drift causes log gaps and certificate errors.

questions.question: Which DSCP value (Per-Hop Behavior) is typically used for VoIP bearer traffic?
questions.explanation: EF (Expedited Forwarding, DSCP 46) is the standard DSCP marking for VoIP bearer traffic. It guarantees low latency, low jitter, and low packet loss — the 'premium' traffic class.

questions.question: What Python library is commonly used for SSH-based CLI automation on network devices?
questions.explanation: Netmiko is a Python library that simplifies SSH connections to multi-vendor network devices, handling prompts, scrolling, and connection management. Built on Paramiko.

questions.question: Which Cisco SD-WAN component is responsible for centralized policy management and route control?
questions.explanation: vSmart is the SD-WAN controller that manages routing policies and pushes them to vEdge routers via OMP. vManage is the GUI. vBond orchestrates initial authentication.

questions.question: What REST API HTTP method would you use to partially update an existing resource?
questions.explanation: PATCH partially updates a resource (only the specified fields). PUT replaces the entire resource. POST creates a new resource. DELETE removes it.

questions.question: What is Zero Touch Provisioning (ZTP)?
options: A method to provision devices without physical console access by automatically loading configs on first boot
A CLI tool for bulk configuration
A Cisco IOS command for factory reset
questions.explanation: ZTP allows new network devices to automatically download and apply a configuration when they first boot, with no human intervention. The device uses DHCP to find a provisioning server (HTTP/TFTP).

questions.question: Which OSPFv3 feature allows it to support IPv6?
options: It uses the same packet format as OSPFv2
OSPFv3 runs over IPv6 link-local addresses and supports multiple address families
OSPFv3 uses UDP instead of IP protocol 89
OSPFv3 requires manual router IDs in IPv6 format
questions.explanation: OSPFv3 forms adjacencies over IPv6 link-local addresses (FE80::/10) and supports IPv6 (and optionally IPv4 in address-family mode). Router IDs remain 32-bit dotted decimal.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 address ::1 used for?
questions.explanation: ::1 is the IPv6 loopback address, equivalent to 127.0.0.1 in IPv4. It is used for testing the local IP stack and is never forwarded by routers.

questions.question: Which IPv6 transition mechanism encapsulates IPv6 packets inside IPv4 packets?
questions.explanation: Tunneling (e.g., 6in4, 6to4) encapsulates IPv6 packets within IPv4 headers for transport across IPv4-only infrastructure. Dual-stack runs both protocols natively. NAT64 translates between IPv4 and IPv6.

questions.question: What is Metro Ethernet (MetroE) and what advantage does it offer over traditional WAN?
options: Ethernet-based WAN service using familiar interface standards and typically higher bandwidth at lower cost
A DSL variant for metropolitan areas
questions.explanation: Metro Ethernet extends Ethernet-based connectivity across a metropolitan area. It uses familiar Ethernet interfaces (no serial/WAN card needed) and typically offers higher bandwidth at lower cost than traditional Frame Relay/ATM.

questions.question: What is the primary business driver for SD-WAN adoption?
options: Using multiple WAN links (MPLS, internet, LTE) intelligently based on application policy, reducing cost
questions.explanation: SD-WAN uses software policy to intelligently route traffic across multiple WAN transports (dedicated MPLS, public internet, 4G/LTE), optimizing cost and performance. Applications get the best path automatically.

questions.question: What does 'line protocol is down' on a serial interface typically indicate?
options: The interface is administratively shut down
Layer 2 (keepalives/encapsulation) issue — often a clock rate mismatch or encapsulation mismatch
questions.explanation: 'Line protocol down' = Layer 2 failure. On serial links: missing clock rate on DCE end, PPP/HDLC encapsulation mismatch, or missing keepalives. 'Interface down' = physical layer issue.

questions.question: In MPLS Layer 3 VPN, what is the role of the PE (Provider Edge) router?
options: Forwards labeled packets between P routers
Connects directly to the customer CE router, adds/removes MPLS labels, maintains per-VPN routing tables (VRFs)
Acts as the DNS server for the MPLS network
questions.explanation: PE routers interface with customer CE routers. They maintain separate VRF (Virtual Routing and Forwarding) tables per customer and impose/remove MPLS labels. P routers only forward labeled packets.

questions.question: What does 'asymmetric' mean in ADSL broadband?
options: Upload and download speeds are equal
Download speed is faster than upload speed
The connection uses two separate lines
questions.explanation: In ADSL, download speed exceeds upload speed. This matches typical home/office use (more download than upload). SDSL provides symmetric speeds at higher cost.

questions.question: Which WAN technology uses a shared coaxial cable network with a cable modem and DOCSIS standard?
questions.explanation: Cable broadband uses the CATV coaxial cable infrastructure and DOCSIS (Data Over Cable Service Interface Specification). Bandwidth is shared among subscribers in a neighborhood.

questions.question: What type of VPN is typically used to securely connect an individual remote worker to a corporate network?
questions.explanation: Remote-access VPN (e.g., Cisco AnyConnect with IPsec/SSL) allows individual users to connect securely to the corporate network from home or travel, requiring client software.

questions.question: Which PPP feature allows two routers to authenticate each other bidirectionally?
questions.explanation: CHAP supports mutual (bidirectional) authentication where each peer challenges the other. PAP only authenticates the caller (one-way). CHAP uses MD5 challenge-response, never sending the password.

questions.question: On a back-to-back serial connection in a lab, which end must configure the clock rate?
questions.explanation: The DCE end (typically the router connected to a DCE cable) must configure `clock rate` on the serial interface. The DTE end receives the clock. In production, the service provider's CSU/DSU provides the clock.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of MPLS over traditional IP routing for enterprise WAN?
options: MPLS is cheaper than internet-based VPNs
MPLS provides guaranteed SLAs, QoS, and can be configured as an any-to-any Layer 2 or Layer 3 VPN
MPLS eliminates the need for routers at customer sites
questions.explanation: MPLS provides carrier-grade SLAs with guaranteed latency, jitter, and packet loss — critical for voice/video. It supports L2VPN and L3VPN, enabling any-to-any connectivity between sites.

questions.question: Which WAN solution provides the highest bandwidth at the lowest latency for a data center interconnect within the same city?
questions.explanation: Dark fiber (dedicated unlit fiber leased from a carrier) or Metro Ethernet provides extremely high bandwidth and low latency for local/metropolitan data center interconnects.

questions.question: What is the difference between configuration management (Ansible/Puppet) and IaC (Terraform)?
options: Config management configures software/OS on existing hosts; IaC provisions the infrastructure (VMs, networks) itself
Terraform is for on-premises only; Ansible is cloud-only
IaC uses YAML; config management uses HCL
questions.explanation: Configuration management (Ansible, Puppet, Chef) manages software/settings on existing hosts. IaC tools (Terraform) provision the infrastructure (VMs, VPCs, load balancers) from scratch.

questions.question: What does 'stateless' mean in the context of REST APIs?
options: Each API request contains all information needed — the server maintains no client session state between requests
REST APIs cannot handle stateful sessions
Stateless means no authentication is used
questions.explanation: REST is stateless — each HTTP request is independent and self-contained. The server doesn't store session state. Authentication tokens/credentials must be included in each request.

questions.question: What is CI/CD in the context of network automation?
options: Continuous Integration / Continuous Delivery — automated testing and deployment pipeline for configuration changes
questions.explanation: CI/CD pipelines automate testing of configuration changes (CI) and their deployment to devices (CD). This enables safe, frequent, and consistent network changes with automated rollback on failure.

questions.question: What are the four NETCONF operations (RPC calls)?
questions.explanation: Core NETCONF operations: get-config (read config datastore), edit-config (modify config), copy-config (copy between datastores), delete-config (delete a datastore). Also: get (state+config), lock, unlock.

questions.question: What is a hybrid cloud deployment model?
options: A cloud used exclusively by one company on-premises
A combination of private on-premises infrastructure and public cloud, integrated together
A cloud managed by multiple companies
questions.explanation: Hybrid cloud combines private (on-premises) and public cloud resources, allowing data/applications to move between them. Enables 'cloud bursting' for peak demand while keeping sensitive data on-premises.

questions.question: What is a golden configuration template in network automation?
options: A configuration saved only on gold-tier devices
A master configuration blueprint used to ensure consistent, compliant configurations across all devices
A Cisco DNA Center feature only
questions.explanation: A golden config template defines the standard, approved configuration for a device type. Automation tools use it to provision new devices consistently and to detect/remediate configuration drift.

questions.question: What is the purpose of Network Access Control (NAC) with posture assessment?
options: Assigns VLANs based on switch port number
Verifies that endpoint devices meet security policy requirements (AV, patches) before granting network access
Provides DHCP to wireless clients only
questions.explanation: NAC checks device posture (antivirus status, OS patches, firewall state) before admission. Non-compliant devices are quarantined or denied — preventing compromised devices from joining the network.

questions.question: What is a deauthentication attack in wireless networking?
options: Sending spoofed deauth frames to disconnect clients from a legitimate AP, often preceding an evil twin attack
Intercepting DHCP traffic to assign rogue IPs
Flooding the wireless channel with data frames
questions.explanation: A deauth attack sends forged 802.11 deauthentication frames to kick clients off their AP. Attackers use it to force clients to reconnect to an evil twin AP they control.

questions.question: What is a stateful firewall and how does it differ from a stateless (packet filter) firewall?
options: A stateful firewall processes packets faster
A stateful firewall tracks connection state and only allows return traffic for established sessions; a packet filter evaluates each packet independently
A stateless firewall is more secure
A stateful firewall operates at Layer 2 only
questions.explanation: Stateful firewalls maintain a connection state table. Return traffic matching an established session is automatically permitted without an explicit rule. Stateless ACLs evaluate each packet individually regardless of session context.

questions.question: Which physical security control is most effective at preventing unauthorized access to a data center?
options: Mantrap / badge-access door with video surveillance
questions.explanation: Physical security controls like mantraps, biometric/badge access, and video surveillance prevent unauthorized physical access to networking equipment — the most fundamental layer of defense-in-depth.

questions.question: What three security services does TLS provide?
questions.explanation: TLS provides: Authentication (certificate-based server/mutual auth), Encryption (AES), and Integrity (HMAC). These three together ensure secure communications over HTTPS, SMTP, etc.

questions.question: Which application uses UDP instead of TCP and why?
options: HTTP — because UDP is faster for web pages
DNS queries — because the overhead of TCP connection setup is not worth it for small, quick exchanges
FTP — because UDP supports file transfer
questions.explanation: DNS queries use UDP 53 for speed — a query/response is small enough to fit in one packet. The overhead of TCP handshake is unnecessary. TCP is used for zone transfers (large data) or responses > 512 bytes.

questions.question: What type of network topology connects all devices to a central switch or hub?
questions.explanation: A star topology connects all devices to a central device (switch/hub). Most modern LANs use star topology. Failure of the central device brings down all connections.

questions.question: Which WAN topology provides the most redundancy by connecting every site to every other site?
questions.explanation: Full mesh topology connects every node to every other node, providing maximum redundancy. However, it's expensive (n*(n-1)/2 links for n nodes). Partial mesh balances cost and redundancy.

questions.question: What is auto-MDIX and why is it useful?
options: Automatic detection and configuration of crossover vs straight-through cable requirements, eliminating the need for correct cable type
questions.explanation: Auto-MDIX (Automatic Medium-Dependent Interface Crossover) allows modern switches and NICs to automatically detect whether a crossover or straight-through cable is needed and adjust internally.

questions.question: What is a collision domain?
options: A set of VLANs that share the same IP subnet
A network segment where two devices transmitting simultaneously cause a collision
A security zone separated by a firewall
questions.explanation: A collision domain is a network segment (e.g., a shared hub segment) where simultaneous frame transmissions cause collisions. Each switch PORT creates a separate collision domain. Switches reduce collisions; full-duplex eliminates them.

questions.question: What is a broadcast domain?
options: A group of devices sharing the same collision domain
A set of all devices that will receive a broadcast frame — bounded by routers (Layer 3) or VLAN boundaries
All devices connected to the same switch
A group of devices with the same subnet mask
questions.explanation: A broadcast domain includes all devices that receive a Layer 2 broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF). Routers and VLANs segment broadcast domains. All ports on a switch in the same VLAN share one broadcast domain.

questions.question: How many subnets and hosts per subnet result from borrowing 3 bits from a Class C network?
questions.explanation: Borrowing 3 bits from host portion: 2^3=8 subnets. Remaining host bits = 5. Usable hosts = 2^5 - 2 = 30. Subnet mask = 255.255.255.224 (/27).

questions.question: What is the subnet mask for a /22 prefix length?
questions.explanation: /22 = 8+8+6 bits = 255.255.252.0. The third octet: 11111100 = 252. Block size = 4 in the third octet. 2^10 - 2 = 1022 usable hosts per subnet.

questions.question: What is an OSPF stub area?
options: An area that does not receive Type 5 External LSAs, receiving a default route instead
An area with only one router
An area that does not run SPF
questions.explanation: An OSPF stub area blocks Type 5 (external) LSAs from entering, replacing them with a default route (0.0.0.0/0) injected by the ABR. Reduces router memory/CPU in remote areas.

questions.question: What type of updates does EIGRP send (unlike RIP's full-table updates)?
options: Partial, bounded updates only for changed routes
Unicast to all known routers every 10 seconds
questions.explanation: EIGRP sends partial updates only for routes that changed, and only to routers that need the information (bounded). This is much more efficient than RIP's full table every 30s.

questions.question: What is a directly connected route and what administrative distance does it have?
options: A route learned via OSPF; AD=110
A route to a network configured on a router's interface that is up; AD=0
A route from another router; AD=1
questions.explanation: A directly connected (C) route represents a network configured on an active router interface. AD=0 — most trusted source. These routes are automatically in the routing table when an interface is up/up.

questions.question: What command configures a static route to network 10.2.0.0/16 via next-hop 192.168.1.1?
questions.explanation: IOS static route syntax: `ip route <network> <subnet-mask> <next-hop-ip or exit-interface>`. Alternatively use the exit interface: `ip route 10.2.0.0 255.255.0.0 GigabitEthernet0/1`.

questions.question: What command configures a static IPv6 default route on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `ipv6 route ::/0 <next-hop>` configures the IPv6 default route. ::/0 is the IPv6 equivalent of 0.0.0.0/0. `ipv6 unicast-routing` must be enabled first.

questions.question: What command manually sets a switch as the root bridge for VLAN 10?
options: Both A and B are valid
questions.explanation: Both `spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary` (sets priority to 24576 or lower to win) and `spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096` (manually set low priority) can make a switch the root bridge.

questions.question: What IOS command verifies EtherChannel status and shows which physical interfaces are bundled?
questions.explanation: `show etherchannel summary` shows port-channel groups, protocol (LACP/PAgP/static), and member interface status (P=in port-channel, I=standalone, D=down).

questions.question: What is the range of extended VLANs in Cisco IOS?
questions.explanation: Normal VLANs: 1-1005 (stored in vlan.dat). Extended VLANs: 1006-4094 (stored in running-config when VTP mode is transparent or off). VLAN 1 is the default; 1002-1005 are reserved for Token Ring/FDDI.

questions.question: What is an Infrastructure BSS wireless topology?
options: Direct device-to-device communication without an AP
Devices connect to an AP (Access Point) which serves as the central connection point
A wired and wireless hybrid network
questions.explanation: Infrastructure BSS (Basic Service Set) has all devices connecting through an AP. The AP manages medium access and provides connection to the wired network. IBSS (ad-hoc) is direct device-to-device.

questions.question: What is the difference between an SNMP trap and an SNMP inform?
options: Traps are more reliable than informs
Informs require acknowledgment from the manager; traps are fire-and-forget (unreliable)
Traps use TCP; informs use UDP
Informs are SNMPv1 only; traps are SNMPv3 only
questions.explanation: SNMP traps (UDP) are sent without acknowledgment — if lost, they're gone. SNMP informs (SNMPv2c/v3) require an acknowledgment from the manager and will be retransmitted if not acknowledged.

questions.question: Which show command displays the total number of active NAT translations and translation misses?
questions.explanation: `show ip nat statistics` shows total active translations, hits, misses, expired translations, and the interfaces configured as inside/outside. `show ip nat translations` shows the actual translation table.

questions.question: What is the virtual MAC address format used by HSRP group 1?
questions.explanation: HSRP virtual MACs follow the format 00:00:0C:07:AC:XX where XX is the HSRP group number in hex. Group 1 = 0x01. This virtual MAC is used by ARP for the virtual IP.

questions.question: Which IOS command sets the minimum syslog severity level that will be sent to the logging buffer?
questions.explanation: `logging buffered <severity-level>` sets the minimum severity stored in the RAM logging buffer. `logging trap` sets the level sent to external syslog servers. `logging console` sets the console level.

questions.question: What security weakness does WPA2-Personal (PSK) have that WPA2-Enterprise does not?
options: WPA2-Personal uses a shared passphrase for all users — if one user is compromised, all must change the passphrase
questions.explanation: WPA2-Personal uses a single PSK shared by all users. WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X with individual user credentials (RADIUS), so a single compromised user doesn't affect others and their access can be revoked individually.

questions.question: What is the 'protect' violation mode in port security?
options: Drops violating frames and sends an SNMP trap
Drops violating frames silently — no log or trap
Drops violating frames and increments the violation counter only
questions.explanation: Protect mode silently drops frames from unauthorized MACs without logging or sending traps. Restrict mode drops frames AND logs/increments counter. Shutdown mode err-disables the port.

questions.question: Which management protocols use encryption to protect data in transit?
questions.explanation: SSH (replaces Telnet) and HTTPS (replaces HTTP) encrypt management traffic. Telnet, HTTP, SNMPv1/v2c, and TFTP transmit data in cleartext.

questions.question: When using REST APIs for network automation, which authentication method provides the most granular access control?
options: OAuth 2.0 with bearer tokens and scoped permissions
questions.explanation: OAuth 2.0 with bearer tokens allows fine-grained scopes (read-only, specific resources), token expiry, and revocation without exposing credentials. Basic auth is simple but less secure. API keys lack per-user granularity.

questions.question: What TCP/IP model layer corresponds to both the OSI Physical and Data Link layers?
questions.explanation: The TCP/IP model's Network Access (Link) layer encompasses the functions of both OSI Layer 1 (Physical) and Layer 2 (Data Link). The TCP/IP model has 4 layers vs OSI's 7.

questions.question: Which ports does FTP use and what is each for?
questions.explanation: FTP uses TCP 21 for control (commands like LIST, RETR) and TCP 20 for data transfer in active mode. In passive mode, a random high port is used for data.

questions.question: What port does SSH use?
questions.explanation: SSH (Secure Shell) uses TCP 22. Telnet uses TCP 23. FTP uses TCP 20/21. HTTPS uses TCP 443.

questions.question: What is the loopback address range for IPv4?
questions.explanation: The entire 127.0.0.0/8 range is reserved for loopback. 127.0.0.1 is the most commonly used. Traffic to loopback never leaves the device and is used to test the IP stack.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a subnet mask?
options: Determines which portion of an IP address is the network part and which is the host part
Maps IP addresses to MAC addresses
questions.explanation: The subnet mask uses 1s for network bits and 0s for host bits. ANDing an IP with its subnet mask yields the network address. It allows devices to determine if a destination is local or remote.

questions.question: What is the first usable host address in the subnet 10.0.4.0/22?
questions.explanation: /22 = 255.255.252.0. Block size 4 in 3rd octet. Subnet 10.0.4.0 → network=10.0.4.0, broadcast=10.0.7.255, first usable=10.0.4.1, last usable=10.0.7.254.

questions.question: A /30 subnet is most commonly used for what purpose?
questions.explanation: /30 provides 2 usable hosts — exactly what's needed for a point-to-point link between two routers. This conserves IP space compared to using /24 for a two-device segment.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid host address in the 192.168.1.128/25 subnet?
questions.explanation: /25 = block size 128. Subnet .128: network=.128, broadcast=.255. Valid hosts: .129 to .254. .127 is in the .0 subnet. .128 is the network address. .200 is valid.

questions.question: Using VLSM, you need to subnet 172.16.0.0/16 to support: Site A=500 hosts, Site B=250 hosts, Site C=60 hosts. What prefix for Site A?
questions.explanation: 500 hosts needs 2^n-2>=500 → n=9 host bits → /23. /23 gives 510 usable hosts. Assign 172.16.0.0/23 to Site A, then 172.16.2.0/24 for Site B (254 hosts), then 172.16.3.0/26 for Site C (62 hosts).

questions.question: During data encapsulation, at what OSI layer does segmentation occur?
questions.explanation: Layer 4 (Transport) segments data from the upper layers into smaller pieces (segments for TCP, datagrams for UDP) to fit within the network's MSS/MTU.

questions.question: Which OSI layer adds a source and destination MAC address header?
questions.explanation: Layer 2 (Data Link) encapsulates packets into frames by adding a Layer 2 header (source MAC, destination MAC) and trailer (FCS for error detection).

questions.question: What IOS command prevents the router from attempting DNS lookups when you mistype a command?
questions.explanation: `no ip domain-lookup` (or `no ip domain lookup` in newer IOS) prevents the router from treating mistyped commands as hostnames and broadcasting DNS queries — which adds a long delay.

questions.question: What does the DHCP `lease` parameter configure?
options: How long a client can use an assigned IP address before renewal
The default gateway pushed to clients
questions.explanation: The DHCP lease time determines how long a client may use an IP address. Default is 1 day. `lease <days> <hours> <minutes>` in the DHCP pool. Shorter leases reclaim addresses faster; longer leases reduce DHCP traffic.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using SVIs for inter-VLAN routing compared to router-on-a-stick?
options: SVIs route traffic within the switch at hardware speed (ASIC), providing much higher throughput than router-on-a-stick
questions.explanation: Layer 3 switches route between SVIs using dedicated hardware (ASICs) at wire speed — potentially millions of pps. Router-on-a-stick funnels all inter-VLAN traffic through a single physical link, creating a bottleneck.

questions.question: What IOS command verifies the MAC address table on a switch?
questions.explanation: `show mac address-table` displays the switch's MAC address table, showing which MAC addresses are learned on which ports and VLANs. Can filter with `show mac address-table dynamic`.

questions.question: Which security best practice should be applied to management interfaces (VTY lines)?
options: Allow all IP addresses for management flexibility
Use ACLs to restrict access to management IPs only and use SSH instead of Telnet
questions.explanation: VTY lines should be restricted with `access-class` ACLs to only permit known management stations, combined with SSH (not Telnet) and strong authentication — following least-privilege principles.

questions.question: What is co-channel interference in wireless networking?
options: Interference from non-Wi-Fi devices like microwaves
Interference between APs using the same channel, reducing throughput
A security attack on the wireless channel
questions.explanation: Co-channel interference occurs when multiple APs in the same area use the same channel. All APs on the same channel must contend for medium access, reducing overall throughput. Use non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11).

questions.question: What is a CRL (Certificate Revocation List) used for in PKI?
options: A list of trusted root CAs
A list of certificates that have been revoked before their expiry date
A list of clients with certificates
A catalog of cipher suites supported by the CA
questions.explanation: A CRL contains serial numbers of certificates revoked by the CA (due to key compromise, employee departure, etc.). Clients check the CRL or OCSP to verify a certificate hasn't been revoked before trusting it.

questions.question: What is a YANG 'leaf' node?
options: A container that holds other nodes
A single data value (string, integer, boolean) with no child nodes
A list of instances with keys
questions.explanation: In YANG: leaf = single value node (like a variable). container = grouping of related leaves. list = repeating set of entries identified by a key. module = the top-level YANG file.

questions.question: What is configuration drift and how does automation address it?
options: When IOS versions differ between devices
When device configurations deviate from the desired/golden state due to manual changes; automation detects and remediates this automatically
questions.explanation: Configuration drift occurs when individual manual changes accumulate, causing devices to diverge from the intended configuration. Automation tools continuously validate and enforce the golden config, preventing/remediating drift.

questions.question: What does HTTP status code 404 mean in a REST API response?
questions.explanation: 404 Not Found = the requested resource doesn't exist. Common REST status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 500 Server Error.

questions.question: What does `show ip protocols` display?
options: Active routing protocols, their timers, networks advertised, and neighbors
questions.explanation: `show ip protocols` shows which dynamic routing protocols are running, their configured networks, timers, redistribution, and neighbor information — useful for verifying routing protocol configuration.

questions.question: What command enables OSPF on all interfaces in the 10.0.0.0/8 network, assigning them to area 0?
questions.explanation: OSPF `network` command uses wildcard masks: `network 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255 area 0`. This matches any interface with an IP starting with 10. and enables OSPF on it in area 0.

questions.question: What is an ESS (Extended Service Set) in wireless networking?
options: A single AP with its associated clients
Multiple APs with the same SSID connected to the same wired distribution system, allowing seamless roaming
An ad-hoc network between two devices
questions.explanation: An ESS consists of multiple BSSs (APs) sharing the same SSID and connected via a wired distribution system. Clients roam between APs without reconfiguring network settings.

questions.question: Which protocol uses TCP port 25?
questions.explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) uses TCP 25 for sending email between mail servers. POP3=110 (retrieve), IMAP=143 (retrieve+sync). DNS=53, SNMP=161, Syslog=514.

questions.question: What is the well-known port for TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol)?
questions.explanation: TFTP uses UDP port 69. It's a simple, lightweight file transfer protocol with no authentication — used for transferring IOS images and configs to/from routers in lab/recovery scenarios.

questions.question: What is OpenFlow in SDN?
options: A southbound protocol that allows an SDN controller to program flow tables in switches
A northbound REST API for applications
questions.explanation: OpenFlow is a southbound protocol that enables an SDN controller to add, modify, or delete flow entries in a switch's flow table, decoupling the control plane from the data plane.

questions.question: Which approach describes 'off-box' automation?
options: Scripts running directly on the network device's CLI
Automation scripts running on an external system (server/workstation) that communicate with devices via APIs or SSH
Using SNMP MIBs to configure devices
Configuring devices through a web browser
questions.explanation: Off-box automation runs on an external controller/server using Python, Ansible, or similar tools that communicate with devices via SSH, NETCONF, or REST APIs. On-box automation runs scripts directly on the device (e.g., Cisco EEM, Guest Shell).

questions.question: What is the purpose of Cisco EEM (Embedded Event Manager)?
options: An on-box automation tool that monitors events and triggers TCL/CLI scripts in response
questions.explanation: Cisco EEM is an on-box automation feature that detects events (syslog messages, interface up/down, CPU thresholds) and triggers applets (CLI scripts) in response — without an external controller.

questions.question: What file format are Ansible playbooks written in?
questions.explanation: Ansible playbooks use YAML (Yet Another Markup Language) format — human-readable, indentation-based syntax. Each playbook contains plays which define tasks to execute on hosts.

questions.question: What is an Ansible inventory file?
options: A list of installed Python packages
A file listing the managed hosts and groups that Ansible playbooks target
questions.explanation: The Ansible inventory file (hosts file) defines the managed nodes (hostnames/IPs) organized into groups. Playbooks target specific hosts or groups from this inventory.

questions.question: What protocol does gRPC/gNMI use for transport?
questions.explanation: gRPC uses HTTP/2 as transport with TLS for encryption. HTTP/2 enables multiplexed streams and bidirectional communication — key for efficient streaming telemetry from network devices.

questions.question: What is SaaS (Software as a Service)?
options: Provides virtualized compute and storage infrastructure
Provides a development platform and runtime environment
Delivers fully managed applications over the internet — user manages nothing but their data and access
questions.explanation: SaaS delivers complete applications over the internet (Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Webex). Users access the software via browser — no OS, middleware, or infrastructure management required.

questions.question: What is the core principle of Zero Trust security architecture?
options: Trust all internal network traffic by default
Verify every user and device explicitly before granting minimum-required access, regardless of location
Block all external traffic and allow all internal traffic
Use perimeter firewalls as the only security control
questions.explanation: Zero Trust assumes no implicit trust based on network location. Every access request is authenticated, authorized, and continuously validated. Principle of least privilege is applied to all users, devices, and services.

questions.question: Which type of social engineering attack uses email to deceive users into revealing credentials or installing malware?
questions.explanation: Phishing uses deceptive emails that appear legitimate to trick users. Spear phishing targets specific individuals. Vishing=voice/phone, Smishing=SMS/text, Whaling=targets executives.

questions.question: What is the primary function of a router?
options: Forward packets between different networks using Layer 3 IP addressing
questions.explanation: A router connects multiple networks and forwards packets between them based on destination IP address and routing table entries. Routers also create separate broadcast domains.

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid IPv4 Class D multicast address?
questions.explanation: Class D addresses (224.0.0.0 - 239.255.255.255) are reserved for multicast. 224.0.0.1 = all hosts multicast. 224.0.0.5 = OSPF DRs. Class D has no subnet mask.

questions.question: What is the purpose of TCP's acknowledgment (ACK) mechanism?
options: Confirms receipt of data segments, enabling reliable delivery and retransmission of lost segments
questions.explanation: TCP uses cumulative ACKs — the receiver acknowledges the next expected byte number. If the sender doesn't receive an ACK within the timeout, it retransmits, ensuring reliable ordered delivery.

questions.question: What connector type is used with UTP Ethernet cables (Cat5e/Cat6)?
questions.explanation: UTP Ethernet cables use RJ-45 connectors (8-pin modular). RJ-11 is used for telephone. BNC for coaxial. LC/SC/ST for fiber optic.

questions.question: What OSPF command enables authentication using a simple plaintext password on an interface?
questions.explanation: Simple OSPF auth requires two commands on the interface: `ip ospf authentication` (enables simple auth) and `ip ospf authentication-key <password>`. MD5 auth uses `ip ospf authentication message-digest`.

questions.question: What does the 'C' code in the routing table indicate?
questions.explanation: Route codes: C = Connected, S = Static, O = OSPF, D = EIGRP, R = RIP, B = BGP, L = Local (the interface's own address, added since IOS 15.x).

questions.question: What is the STP port cost for a 1 Gbps interface using the IEEE 802.1D-2004 long path cost?
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1D-2004 long path costs: 10 Mbps=2,000,000, 100 Mbps=200,000, 1 Gbps=20,000, 10 Gbps=2,000. Original 802.1D short method: 10M=100, 100M=19, 1G=4, 10G=2.

questions.question: What WLAN architecture is recommended for large enterprise deployments with centralized management?
options: Lightweight APs with a WLC (Wireless LAN Controller)
questions.explanation: Large enterprise WLANs use lightweight APs with a centralized WLC for scalable management, consistent policy enforcement, seamless roaming, and RF optimization across hundreds of APs.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the ICMP protocol?
options: Provides error reporting and diagnostic functions (ping, traceroute)
questions.explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) provides error reporting (destination unreachable, time exceeded) and diagnostics. `ping` uses ICMP Echo Request/Reply. `traceroute` uses ICMP Time Exceeded.

questions.question: What is TCP port multiplexing used for?
options: Allowing multiple applications on one host to use the network simultaneously via unique source port numbers
Splitting one connection across multiple physical links
questions.explanation: TCP/UDP source ports (randomly chosen high ports 1024-65535) allow multiple simultaneous connections from one host. A unique 4-tuple (src IP, src port, dst IP, dst port) identifies each connection.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a firewall in a network?
options: Controls network traffic by permitting or blocking based on security rules
questions.explanation: A firewall enforces security policies by inspecting traffic and allowing or blocking connections based on rules (ACLs, stateful inspection, application awareness). It creates a security boundary between network zones.

questions.question: Which protocol operates at both Layer 3 and Layer 4 depending on context?
questions.explanation: ICMP is technically a Layer 3 protocol (encapsulated directly in IP, protocol number 1), but it provides Layer 4-like messaging. Some sources place it between L3/L4. It's generally considered a Layer 3 protocol.

questions.question: What IOS command verifies NTP synchronization status and stratum level?
questions.explanation: `show ntp status` shows sync status, stratum, reference clock, and offset. `show ntp associations` shows NTP peers and their sync state (asterisk = current sync source).

questions.question: What is Cisco IP SLA used for?
options: Monitors and measures network performance metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss) using synthetic traffic
Assigns IP addresses based on SLA requirements
Monitors CPU and memory on Cisco devices
questions.explanation: IP SLA sends synthetic test traffic (ICMP echo, UDP jitter, HTTP, DNS) to measure end-to-end performance. Results can trigger object tracking to modify routing (floating static routes) when performance degrades.

questions.question: What is a botnet?
options: A network of compromised computers (bots) controlled by an attacker for DDoS, spam, or credential theft
questions.explanation: A botnet is a collection of internet-connected devices infected with malware and controlled remotely by a threat actor (bot herder). Used to launch DDoS attacks, send spam, or mine cryptocurrency.

questions.question: What is ransomware?
options: Malware that encrypts victim's files and demands payment for the decryption key
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts files on infected systems and demands a ransom (usually cryptocurrency) for the decryption key. Often spreads via phishing emails or exploit kits.

questions.question: What is the advantage of using out-of-band (OOB) management?
options: Management traffic uses a separate dedicated network, so it remains accessible even during a production network outage
Provides higher bandwidth for device configuration
questions.explanation: OOB management uses a dedicated management network (separate from production) — often via console server or dedicated management interfaces. Devices can be accessed even when production links fail.

questions.question: Which RADIUS attribute separates authentication and authorization in RADIUS accounting?
options: RADIUS uses separate ports for each function: UDP 1812 (auth/authz), UDP 1813 (accounting)
RADIUS combines all three AAA functions in one packet type
RADIUS only supports authentication, not authorization
questions.explanation: RADIUS separates authentication/authorization (UDP 1812) from accounting (UDP 1813). In practice, RADIUS combines authentication and authorization in the Access-Accept response (which also returns authorization attributes).

questions.question: What is a T1 line and what is its bandwidth?
options: A fiber optic WAN line with 100 Mbps
A digital WAN circuit composed of 24 DS0 channels with 1.544 Mbps aggregate bandwidth
A satellite connection with 1.5 Mbps download
questions.explanation: A T1 (DS1) is a digital WAN circuit with 24 DS0 (64 Kbps) channels = 1.544 Mbps total. E1 (European equivalent) = 32 channels = 2.048 Mbps. T3 = 28 T1s = 44.736 Mbps.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a CSU/DSU in WAN connectivity?
options: Converts digital data from the router's serial interface to the digital signal format used on the WAN circuit
Acts as a wireless access point for WAN connectivity
questions.explanation: CSU/DSU (Channel Service Unit/Data Service Unit) connects a router's serial interface to the WAN provider circuit (T1/E1). It converts between the router's internal signaling and the carrier's line format, and provides clocking.

questions.question: What is the OSPF passive-interface command used for?
options: Disables OSPF on the interface entirely
Prevents OSPF hello packets from being sent out an interface while still advertising its network
Increases hello timer on the interface
Makes the interface a stub network
questions.explanation: `passive-interface <int>` stops hello packets from being sent/received on that interface (no OSPF neighbors), but the connected network is still included in OSPF advertisements. Used on LAN interfaces facing end devices.

questions.question: Which table does EIGRP use to store all possible routes to a destination, including feasible successors?
questions.explanation: EIGRP maintains three tables: (1) Neighbor table (active EIGRP peers), (2) Topology table (all paths, including feasible successors), (3) Routing table (only the best routes/successors).

questions.question: Which command verifies the operational trunk status and active VLANs on a trunk interface?
questions.explanation: `show interfaces trunk` shows: trunk ports, encapsulation (802.1Q), allowed VLANs, active VLANs, and which VLANs are in STP forwarding state on each trunk.

questions.question: What is the Root Port in STP?
options: The port on the Root Bridge that leads to the rest of the network
The port on each non-root switch that provides the lowest-cost path back to the Root Bridge
The port blocked to prevent loops
The port connected to the default gateway
questions.explanation: Each non-root switch has exactly one Root Port — the port with the lowest-cost path to the Root Bridge. On the Root Bridge, all ports are Designated Ports (no Root Port).

questions.question: What determines which port is blocked on a non-root switch segment with two paths of equal cost to the root?
options: The port with the lower port number is blocked
The port connected to the switch with the lower Bridge ID is the Designated Port; the other is blocked
questions.explanation: When costs are equal, STP compares sending Bridge IDs. The segment's Designated Port is on the switch with the lower Bridge ID. The other switch's port toward that segment becomes Non-Designated (blocked).

questions.question: How does RESTCONF differ from NETCONF in terms of transport and encoding?
options: RESTCONF uses SSH/XML; NETCONF uses HTTPS/JSON
RESTCONF uses HTTPS with REST semantics and supports JSON or XML; NETCONF uses SSH with XML only
RESTCONF is for IPv6 only; NETCONF is for IPv4
questions.explanation: NETCONF: SSH/830, XML encoding only, RPC-based operations. RESTCONF: HTTPS, REST semantics (GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE), supports both XML and JSON. Both use YANG data models.

questions.question: What Cisco DNA Center feature uses AI/ML to automatically identify network issues?
questions.explanation: DNA Center Assurance uses AI/ML analytics to monitor network health, identify root causes of issues, and proactively notify administrators — providing a 360-degree view of devices, clients, and applications.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using version control (e.g., Git) for network configurations?
options: Complete history of changes, ability to roll back to known-good state, and collaboration on configuration changes
questions.explanation: Git version control provides: full change history, diff between versions, rollback capability, branching for testing, and collaborative workflows (pull requests/code review) for configuration changes.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 unique local address range and what is it analogous to in IPv4?
options: FC00::/7 — analogous to RFC 1918 private addresses
questions.explanation: FC00::/7 (includes FD00::/8 which is the usable range) is the IPv6 unique local address — similar to RFC 1918 private space. Not routed on the public Internet; used within organizations.

questions.question: How many hexadecimal groups are in a full IPv6 address?
questions.explanation: IPv6 is 128 bits, written as 8 groups of 4 hex digits separated by colons (e.g., 2001:0DB8:ACAD:0001:0000:0000:0000:0001). :: shortens consecutive all-zero groups.

questions.question: What IOS command displays interface statistics including input/output errors and packet counts?
questions.explanation: `show interfaces <int>` displays detailed statistics: packet counts, error counters (input errors, CRC, giants, runts), bandwidth, duplex, MTU, and line/protocol state.

questions.question: What command quickly shows all interfaces, their IP addresses, and up/down status?
questions.explanation: `show ip interface brief` provides a compact summary: interface name, IP address, OK (valid IP), Method (manual/DHCP), Status (physical), and Protocol (Layer 2) for all interfaces.

questions.question: What does the 'line vty 0 4' IOS configuration accomplish?
options: Configures virtual terminal lines 0 through 4 (5 simultaneous remote sessions)
Sets the VTY password to 0 or 4
questions.explanation: `line vty 0 4` enters configuration for 5 VTY (Virtual Terminal) lines — the maximum number of simultaneous Telnet/SSH remote management sessions allowed.

questions.question: What is the broadcast address of the 172.16.32.0/20 subnet?
questions.explanation: /20 = 255.255.240.0. Block size 16 in 3rd octet. Subnet 172.16.32.0: network=172.16.32.0, broadcast=172.16.47.255 (32+16-1=47). Next subnet starts at 172.16.48.0.

questions.question: You have 192.168.10.0/24 and need to divide it into subnets of exactly 30 hosts each. Which prefix provides this?
questions.explanation: /27 gives 2^5-2=30 usable hosts — exactly 30. /26=62 hosts (too many), /28=14 (too few), /29=6 (too few). This creates 8 subnets of 30 hosts from the /24.

questions.question: What is the difference between a unicast, multicast, and broadcast IP address?
options: Unicast=one sender, one receiver; Multicast=one sender, many specific receivers; Broadcast=one sender, all receivers on segment
They are all identical in IPv4
Broadcast is for WAN; unicast is for LAN
Multicast requires TCP; broadcast requires UDP
questions.explanation: Unicast: one-to-one. Multicast: one-to-many (specific group subscribers, 224.0.0.0-239.255.255.255). Broadcast: one-to-all on subnet (e.g., 192.168.1.255). IPv6 eliminates broadcast using multicast.

questions.question: Which port and protocol does IMAP use for email retrieval?
questions.explanation: IMAP4 (Internet Message Access Protocol) uses TCP 143 (or TCP 993 for IMAP over TLS/SSL). POP3 uses TCP 110. SMTP uses TCP 25 for sending. IMAP leaves mail on server and synchronizes folders.

questions.question: At the Physical layer, what is the function of the preamble in an Ethernet frame?
options: Contains the source and destination MAC addresses
A sequence of alternating 1s and 0s allowing receiving NIC to synchronize its clock before the frame data
questions.explanation: The Ethernet preamble (7 bytes of alternating 1s and 0s + 1 byte SFD) allows the receiving NIC to synchronize its clock before the actual frame data arrives. It's stripped by the NIC and is not considered part of the frame.

questions.question: What is the maximum cable length for Cat5e/Cat6 UTP Ethernet (100BASE-TX/1000BASE-T)?
questions.explanation: The maximum UTP segment length for 100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet) and 1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet) is 100 meters (328 feet). Beyond this, signal degradation causes excessive errors.

questions.question: What does CSMA/CD stand for and which Ethernet mode requires it?
options: Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection; required in half-duplex Ethernet
Carrier Signal Multiplexing / Collision Domain; required in full-duplex
Channel State Monitor Allocation / Circuit Detection; required in fiber Ethernet
Collision Shared Media Access / Circuit Detect; required in 10GE
questions.explanation: CSMA/CD: listen before transmitting (Carrier Sense), share the medium (Multiple Access), detect and handle collisions (Collision Detection). Only needed in half-duplex. Full-duplex Ethernet has no collisions.

questions.question: What is the VRRP equivalent of the HSRP Active router?
questions.explanation: VRRP terms: Master router (forwards traffic for virtual IP) = HSRP Active. Backup router = HSRP Standby. Unlike HSRP, the VRRP Master can be the owner of the virtual IP (set as physical IP on the Master).

questions.question: What is CBWFQ (Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing)?
options: A strict priority queue for VoIP
A queuing method that provides minimum bandwidth guarantees to user-defined traffic classes
A method to drop traffic when queues are full
questions.explanation: CBWFQ allows administrators to define traffic classes and guarantee minimum bandwidth to each class. During congestion, each class gets at least its allocated bandwidth. LLQ adds a strict priority class on top of CBWFQ.

questions.question: What is the 'inside local' address in NAT terminology?
options: The public IP assigned to the internal host after translation
The private IP address of the internal host before translation
The IP address of the NAT pool
questions.explanation: NAT terms: Inside local = private IP of internal host (e.g., 10.1.1.1). Inside global = public IP after translation (e.g., 203.0.113.1). Outside global = real IP of external server.

questions.question: What happens to a switch port in 802.1X before the supplicant authenticates?
options: The port immediately goes to forwarding
The port is placed in the unauthorized state and only EAP traffic is allowed
The port sends DHCP to the client
questions.explanation: Before 802.1X authentication, the switch port is in unauthorized state — only EAP (authentication) traffic passes. After successful RADIUS authentication, the port transitions to authorized state for normal traffic.

questions.question: What replaced SNMPv1/v2c for secure network management?
questions.explanation: SNMPv3 with authPriv provides authentication (SHA/MD5) and encryption (AES/DES) — addressing SNMPv1/v2c's cleartext community strings. SNMPv3 is required in any security-conscious environment.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a honeypot in network security?
options: A decoy system that lures attackers to study their techniques and divert them from real assets
questions.explanation: A honeypot is an intentionally vulnerable decoy system. Attackers targeting it reveal their methods, tools, and attack patterns while being distracted from real production systems.

questions.question: What does 'least privilege' mean as a security principle?
options: Users should have no access by default
Users and systems should be granted only the minimum access required to perform their job function
All users share the same privilege level
questions.explanation: Least privilege limits access rights to the minimum necessary — reducing attack surface. If an account is compromised, the attacker has limited capabilities. Applied to users, processes, and network segments.

questions.question: What is the Content-Type header value when sending JSON data in a REST API request?
questions.explanation: The HTTP Content-Type header `application/json` tells the server the request body is JSON-encoded data. `Accept: application/json` tells the server the client wants a JSON response.

questions.question: What is a Jinja2 template used for in network automation?
options: A templating language for generating configuration files with variable substitution
questions.explanation: Jinja2 is a Python templating engine used to generate network device configurations from templates + variable data. Ansible uses Jinja2 natively for config templates, enabling one template to configure many devices.

questions.question: What is the Cisco ACI (Application Centric Infrastructure) approach to SDN?
options: A policy-based data center SDN solution with a centralized APIC controller using an application-centric policy model
questions.explanation: Cisco ACI is a data center SDN solution where the APIC (Application Policy Infrastructure Controller) manages network policy using an object model. Policy is defined in terms of application requirements (Endpoints, EPGs, Contracts).

questions.question: What is the difference between tunnel mode and transport mode in IPsec?
options: Tunnel mode is faster; transport mode is more secure
Tunnel mode encapsulates the entire original IP packet with a new IP header; transport mode only encrypts the payload, keeping the original IP header
Transport mode is for site-to-site; tunnel mode is for remote access
questions.explanation: IPsec Tunnel mode adds a new outer IP header — used for VPNs between gateways (the inner IP is hidden). Transport mode encrypts only the payload, using the original IP header — used for host-to-host encryption.

questions.question: Which WAN technology provides low latency but very high deployment cost for last-mile connectivity?
questions.explanation: FTTP/FTTH (Fiber to the Premises/Home) offers the lowest latency and highest bandwidth of any last-mile technology but requires significant civil engineering (trenching, cable installation), making deployment expensive.

questions.question: What is the main disadvantage of satellite internet for enterprise WAN use?
options: Very high latency (~600ms round trip) due to signal travel distance to geostationary orbit
questions.explanation: Geostationary satellites orbit at ~35,786 km, causing ~600ms round-trip latency. This makes real-time applications (VoIP, video conferencing) unusable and impacts TCP performance. Used only when no other option exists.

questions.question: What is DHCPv6 used for and what does SLAAC not provide that DHCPv6 can?
options: DHCPv6 can provide additional parameters (DNS server, domain name) that SLAAC Router Advertisements alone cannot reliably provide
DHCPv6 only works in IPv6-only networks
questions.explanation: SLAAC provides prefix and default gateway via RA but cannot push DNS/DHCP options natively. DHCPv6 (stateful or stateless) provides DNS server addresses and other parameters. DHCPv6 uses UDP 546 (client) / 547 (server).

questions.question: What does the 'M' flag (Managed Address Configuration) in an IPv6 Router Advertisement indicate?
options: Multicast is available on the link
Hosts should use stateful DHCPv6 to obtain their IPv6 address
The router is the default gateway
questions.explanation: The M flag (Managed) in RA tells hosts to use stateful DHCPv6 for address assignment. The O flag (Other) tells hosts to use DHCPv6 for other info (DNS) but use SLAAC for the address.

questions.question: What IOS commands configure an interface as an access port in VLAN 10?
questions.explanation: Two commands: (1) `switchport mode access` sets the port to access mode (not trunk). (2) `switchport access vlan 10` assigns it to VLAN 10. Without the first command, the port may negotiate trunk mode via DTP.

questions.question: What wireless security vulnerability does TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol) address compared to WEP?
options: TKIP adds per-packet key mixing and message integrity check (MIC) to fix WEP's weak static keys
TKIP eliminates the need for a passphrase
TKIP uses AES instead of RC4
questions.explanation: TKIP was a software fix for WEP-based hardware: adds per-packet key rotation, MIC (Michael), and replay protection. However, TKIP (used in WPA) is still RC4-based and is now deprecated — use AES/CCMP (WPA2).

questions.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for establishing, managing, and terminating sessions between applications?
questions.explanation: Layer 5 (Session) manages dialog control: establishing, maintaining, and terminating communication sessions. Examples: NetBIOS, NFS, SQL sessions. In practice, TCP handles much of this in the TCP/IP model.

questions.question: What is the function of the Presentation layer (OSI Layer 6)?
options: Translates data formats, handles encryption/decryption and compression
questions.explanation: Layer 6 (Presentation) ensures data is in a usable format: data translation (ASCII to EBCDIC), encryption/decryption (SSL/TLS starts here conceptually), and compression. Examples: JPEG, MPEG, SSL.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an IDS sensor placed in promiscuous (monitor) mode?
options: To passively capture and analyze a copy of traffic for threat detection without affecting packet flow
questions.explanation: Promiscuous mode IDS sensors analyze a mirrored copy of traffic (from a SPAN port or network tap) without being in the traffic path. They detect but cannot block — alerting only.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the FCS (Frame Check Sequence) field in an Ethernet frame?
options: A 4-byte CRC value used to detect bit errors introduced during transmission
questions.explanation: The FCS (last 4 bytes of an Ethernet frame) contains a CRC-32 value. The receiving NIC recalculates the CRC; if it doesn't match, the frame is discarded as corrupted.

questions.question: A host has IP 192.168.100.150/26. What is its default gateway's likely network?
questions.explanation: /26 = block size 64. Subnets: .0, .64, .128, .192. .150 falls in the .128 subnet (range .128-.191). The gateway would be in this same subnet, e.g., 192.168.100.129.

questions.question: How many valid host addresses are available in a /16 subnet?
questions.explanation: /16 has 16 host bits: 2^16 = 65,536 total addresses. Minus network and broadcast = 65,534 usable host addresses.

questions.question: What is BGP primarily used for?
options: Routing between autonomous systems on the internet (EGP) — the routing protocol of the internet
Providing fast convergence in enterprise networks
Load balancing within a data center
questions.explanation: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the internet's routing protocol — an EGP (Exterior Gateway Protocol) that exchanges routes between autonomous systems (ASes). Uses TCP 179. ISPs and large enterprises use it for internet connectivity.

questions.question: What is policy-based routing (PBR) used for?
options: Routing traffic based on criteria beyond destination IP (e.g., source IP, application, QoS mark) using route maps
questions.explanation: PBR overrides normal destination-based routing using route maps. It can route traffic differently based on source address, packet size, or DSCP marking — useful for traffic engineering.

questions.question: What is the OSPF DR election priority rule?
options: Highest OSPF priority wins (default 1); ties broken by highest Router ID
questions.explanation: On broadcast networks, the router with the highest OSPF priority (default 1) becomes DR. Priority 0 = never elected. Ties broken by highest Router ID. Priority can be changed with `ip ospf priority <0-255>`.

questions.question: Which STP timer controls how long a port waits in the Listening and Learning states?
questions.explanation: Forward Delay (default 15 seconds) is the time a port spends in each of the Listening and Learning states. Total STP convergence without PortFast = 30 seconds in L/L + Max Age for topology change detection.

questions.question: What WLAN component handles roaming between APs without client re-authentication in a WLC-based architecture?
options: The WLC manages roaming, maintaining the client session as it moves between APs
questions.explanation: In centralized WLC architecture, the WLC tracks client sessions. When a client roams to another AP, the WLC seamlessly transfers the session without requiring re-authentication (fast/seamless roaming).

questions.question: What is a 'voice VLAN' and why is it used on access ports?
options: A VLAN dedicated to router management
A separate VLAN configured on an access port to carry tagged IP phone traffic while the port also carries untagged data traffic
A VLAN for wireless voice clients only
questions.explanation: A voice VLAN allows one switch port to carry both: untagged data (PC) in the access VLAN and tagged voice traffic in the voice VLAN. Command: `switchport voice vlan <id>`. Enables QoS separation of voice/data.

questions.question: What IOS command displays DHCP bindings (assigned leases) on a Cisco router acting as a DHCP server?
questions.explanation: `show ip dhcp binding` lists all active DHCP leases: client MAC address, assigned IP, lease expiration, and type (automatic/manual). Useful for troubleshooting connectivity issues.

questions.question: What command clears all dynamic NAT translations from the NAT translation table?
questions.explanation: `clear ip nat translation *` removes all dynamic NAT entries from the translation table immediately. This is useful when changing NAT configuration or troubleshooting stuck translations.

questions.question: What is an SNMP community string and what security concern does it have?
options: A public/private key pair for SNMPv3
A plaintext password sent in SNMPv1/v2c packets — anyone capturing traffic can read it and potentially modify device config
An encrypted token for SNMP authentication
A VLAN name for SNMP traffic isolation
questions.explanation: SNMP community strings (SNMPv1/v2c) are cleartext 'passwords' included in every packet. Read-only (RO) allows polling; Read-write (RW) allows configuration changes. They're easily sniffed — use SNMPv3 for security.

questions.question: What IOS command enables syslog timestamps on log messages?
questions.explanation: `service timestamps log datetime msec` adds date/time timestamps (with millisecond precision) to syslog messages — essential for correlating events across devices during troubleshooting.

questions.question: What IOS command verifies port security configuration and violation counts on an interface?
questions.explanation: `show port-security interface <int>` displays: max MACs, current MACs, violation mode, violation count, and security status. `show port-security` shows a summary for all ports.

questions.question: What IOS command enables AAA globally on a Cisco device?
questions.explanation: `aaa new-model` enables the AAA framework globally. After this, you configure authentication methods: `aaa authentication login default group radius local` — use RADIUS first, fall back to local.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a digital signature?
options: Provides authentication and non-repudiation — proves the message came from the claimed sender and was not altered
questions.explanation: A digital signature (hash signed with sender's private key) proves authenticity (sender identity) and integrity (message not altered). The recipient verifies using the sender's public key. Provides non-repudiation.

questions.question: What is network segmentation and why is it a security best practice?
options: Combining all VLANs into one large network
Dividing the network into isolated zones (using VLANs, firewalls, ACLs) to limit lateral movement if one zone is compromised
questions.explanation: Segmentation limits blast radius — if an attacker compromises one zone (DMZ, guest VLAN), they can't easily pivot to other zones (production, finance). Zones are separated by firewalls/ACLs enforcing least-privilege access.

questions.question: What is the purpose of API versioning (e.g., /api/v1/, /api/v2/)?
options: Allow backward-compatible API changes without breaking existing clients using the older version
Provide separate APIs for different users
questions.explanation: API versioning allows the API to evolve (adding/changing endpoints) without breaking existing integrations. Clients can explicitly request a version. Older versions remain supported for a deprecation period.

questions.question: What is an Ansible role and why is it used?
options: A security privilege level in Ansible Tower
A reusable, structured way to organize playbook tasks, variables, and files for a specific function (e.g., OSPF config, VLAN setup)
A Python module for network devices
questions.explanation: Ansible roles organize automation content into a standard directory structure. Roles are reusable — write once, use across multiple playbooks. They promote modularity and consistency in large automation projects.

questions.question: What are NETCONF datastores?
options: Named repositories for configuration data: running (active config), startup (saved config), and candidate (work-in-progress, if supported)
questions.explanation: NETCONF defines standard datastores: running (currently active config), startup (config loaded on boot), and candidate (editable copy of config before committing). Not all devices support all datastores.

questions.question: What is OMP (Overlay Management Protocol) in Cisco SD-WAN?
options: The control-plane routing protocol used between vSmart controllers and vEdge/cEdge routers to distribute routes and policies
A physical WAN link monitoring protocol
A replacement for OSPF in SD-WAN
questions.explanation: OMP (Overlay Management Protocol) runs between vEdge/cEdge routers and vSmart controllers via DTLS/TLS tunnels. It distributes routing information, policies, and crypto keys across the SD-WAN fabric.

questions.question: What does a WAN 'handoff' typically refer to?
options: The physical interface and protocol used to connect the customer premises to the service provider network
questions.explanation: The WAN handoff is the point where the service provider's circuit terminates at the customer premises — typically a serial interface (T1/E1 with CSU/DSU), Ethernet (for fiber/Metro Ethernet), or a cable/DSL modem.

questions.question: What does the `traceroute` command reveal that `ping` does not?
options: The path (each router hop) packets take to reach the destination and latency at each hop
questions.explanation: `traceroute` uses TTL manipulation to reveal each router in the path and round-trip time to each hop. `ping` only confirms end-to-end reachability. Both help locate where in the path failures occur.

questions.question: A PC cannot ping its default gateway but can ping 127.0.0.1. What does this isolate?
options: The TCP/IP stack is working but there is a Layer 1/2/3 issue between the PC and gateway (cable, switchport, IP config, gateway config)
questions.explanation: 127.0.0.1 (loopback) success = TCP/IP stack is working. Failure to ping gateway = problem is external: check physical connection, IP address/mask config, VLAN assignment, or gateway configuration.

questions.question: What are the four layers of the TCP/IP model from bottom to top?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP model: (1) Network Access / Link (Physical + Data Link), (2) Internet (IP routing), (3) Transport (TCP/UDP), (4) Application (HTTP, DNS, SMTP, etc.). This is the practical model for TCP/IP networks.

questions.question: What is a Designated Port in STP?
options: The port on each switch closest to the Root Bridge
The one forwarding port on each network segment that provides the lowest-cost path toward the Root Bridge
The port connected to the default gateway
questions.explanation: Each LAN segment has one Designated Port — the port on that segment providing the best (lowest cost) path toward the root bridge. All ports on the Root Bridge are Designated. Only one per segment forwards traffic.

questions.question: What QoS mechanism drops packets before a queue fills completely to avoid synchronization of TCP connections?
questions.explanation: WRED probabilistically drops packets before the queue fills, favoring lower-priority traffic (lower DSCP gets higher drop probability). Avoids TCP global synchronization where all flows slow simultaneously when tail-drop occurs.

questions.question: What is the purpose of network function virtualization (NFV)?
options: Runs network functions (firewall, load balancer, IDS, NAT) as software on standard x86 servers instead of dedicated hardware appliances
questions.explanation: NFV decouples network functions from proprietary hardware, running them as Virtual Network Functions (VNFs) on COTS (commercial off-the-shelf) x86 servers. Reduces CapEx, improves agility and scalability.

questions.question: What is ARP spoofing/poisoning?
options: Flooding the ARP table with valid entries
Sending gratuitous ARP replies with a fake MAC address to redirect traffic through the attacker's device
Blocking all ARP traffic with an ACL
questions.explanation: ARP poisoning sends fake ARP replies associating the attacker's MAC with a legitimate IP (e.g., the gateway). Victims update their ARP cache and send traffic to the attacker — enabling MitM or DoS attacks.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a proxy server?
options: Acts as an intermediary between clients and servers — caching content, providing anonymity, and enforcing access policies
questions.explanation: A proxy server sits between clients and the internet, forwarding requests on behalf of clients. Benefits: caching (reduces bandwidth), content filtering, anonymity, and access control.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of active physical links in an LACP EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: LACP supports up to 8 active member links in an EtherChannel (with up to 8 standby/hot-standby links in reserve). PAgP supports up to 8 active links. Total = 16 bundled, 8 active.

questions.question: Which two IPv6 addresses are required on any IPv6-enabled interface?
options: A global unicast and a unique local address
A link-local address (FE80::/10) and at least one unicast address (global or unique local)
A link-local and a multicast address
questions.explanation: Every IPv6-enabled interface automatically gets a link-local address (FE80::/10) via EUI-64 or random generation. Additionally, it needs a routable unicast address (global or unique local) for communication beyond the link.

questions.question: Which device operates at OSI Layer 1 and simply repeats signals to extend cable distance?
questions.explanation: A repeater (or hub) operates at Layer 1, amplifying and retransmitting signals to extend cable distance. It does not examine frames — all traffic is repeated out every port, creating one large collision domain.

questions.question: What is a DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network design?
options: A network segment between the public internet and the private internal network, hosting publicly accessible servers
A VLAN for network management only
questions.explanation: A DMZ is a semi-trusted network zone (between two firewall interfaces) that hosts public-facing servers (web, email, DNS). Compromise of a DMZ server doesn't directly expose the internal private network.

questions.question: What is the purpose of route redistribution?
options: Imports routes from one routing protocol domain into another (e.g., OSPF into EIGRP) to enable communication between mixed-protocol networks
Provides load balancing between equal-cost routes
questions.explanation: Route redistribution injects routes learned by one routing protocol into another. Example: `redistribute ospf 1 metric 10000 100 255 1 1500` injects OSPF routes into EIGRP. Requires careful metric conversion to avoid loops.

questions.question: What does the IOS command `service password-encryption` do?
options: Enables AES encryption on all passwords
Applies a reversible Type 7 cipher to plaintext passwords in the configuration — weaker than enable secret's MD5
questions.explanation: `service password-encryption` applies a simple, reversible obfuscation (Type 7) to plaintext passwords like `enable password` and VTY passwords in the config. It is NOT strong encryption — anyone with the algorithm can decode it.

questions.question: What is the main difference between Ansible and Puppet in terms of architecture?
options: Ansible requires Python; Puppet does not
Ansible is agentless (push/SSH); Puppet uses agents on managed nodes that pull config from the Puppet master (TCP 8140)
questions.explanation: Ansible: agentless, push-based via SSH, YAML playbooks, no master required. Puppet: agent-based, pull model — agents check in with Puppet master (TCP 8140) every 30 minutes, Ruby-based manifests.

questions.question: Which FHRP allows the physical IP address of a router to be the same as the virtual IP?
questions.explanation: VRRP allows the Master router's actual IP to be the virtual IP address — making it the 'IP address owner'. HSRP and GLBP always use a separate virtual IP distinct from all physical interface IPs.

questions.question: What technology does LTE (Long-Term Evolution) use for mobile broadband WAN connectivity?
options: 4G cellular radio technology operating in licensed spectrum bands
questions.explanation: LTE (4G) uses cellular radio technology in licensed spectrum. Provides mobile broadband WAN backup or primary connectivity — useful for remote sites without wired infrastructure.

questions.question: What does WPA2 use for encryption in enterprise (802.1X) mode?
options: AES with CCMP (Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol)
questions.explanation: WPA2 mandates AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) with CCMP for encryption and message integrity. CCMP replaced TKIP (RC4-based, used in WPA). WPA2 with AES/CCMP is the current enterprise security standard.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the IPv6 solicited-node multicast address?
options: Used by routers to advertise default routes
Used for Neighbor Discovery (NDP) to resolve IPv6 addresses to MAC addresses, replacing ARP broadcasts
questions.explanation: Each IPv6 address has a corresponding solicited-node multicast (FF02::1:FF<last 24 bits of IPv6>). NDP Neighbor Solicitation is sent to this address — only the target host listens, avoiding broadcast flooding.

questions.question: What is a DHCP conflict and how does Cisco IOS detect it?
options: When two DHCP servers offer the same lease
When the DHCP server finds an IP address already in use before assigning it — detected by sending a ping before the offer
When a client requests two IP addresses
When the DHCP pool is exhausted
questions.explanation: Before assigning an IP, Cisco IOS DHCP server pings the address. If a reply is received, there's a conflict (address in use). The server logs the conflict and skips to the next address. `show ip dhcp conflict` displays detected conflicts.

questions.question: Which OSI layer provides reliable, connection-oriented delivery services?
questions.explanation: Layer 4 (Transport) provides end-to-end reliability via TCP: sequencing, acknowledgment, retransmission, flow control. Layer 3 routes packets. UDP is also Layer 4 but is connectionless (unreliable).

questions.question: What is a managed switch compared to an unmanaged switch?
options: A managed switch is always Layer 3; unmanaged is Layer 2
A managed switch supports configuration (VLANs, STP, port security, QoS) via CLI/GUI; unmanaged switches have no configuration interface
A managed switch has fewer ports
questions.explanation: Managed switches can be configured for VLANs, trunking, STP, QoS, port security, and monitoring. Unmanaged switches work out of the box with no configuration capability — suitable only for simple home/small office use.

questions.question: What is social engineering in cybersecurity?
options: Manipulating people into divulging confidential information or performing actions that compromise security
Physically breaking into a data center
questions.explanation: Social engineering exploits human psychology rather than technical vulnerabilities. Examples: phishing emails, pretexting (impersonating IT support), baiting (USB drops), tailgating (following someone into secured area).

questions.question: What is the benefit of using a source-of-truth (e.g., NetBox, Nautobot) in network automation?
options: Provides a CLI backup for devices
Acts as an authoritative record of intended network state (IP addresses, VLANs, device inventory) that automation uses as input
questions.explanation: A Source of Truth (SoT) like NetBox provides a centralized, authoritative record of the intended network state. Automation tools query the SoT for device data, IP assignments, and topology to generate configurations.

questions.question: What is an ASBR in OSPF?
options: Autonomous System Boundary Router — a router that redistributes external routes (from BGP, EIGRP, or static) into OSPF, generating Type 5 External LSAs
A router with only one OSPF area
An ABR with more than two areas
questions.explanation: An ASBR (Autonomous System Boundary Router) connects OSPF to other routing domains (BGP, EIGRP, RIP, static). It generates Type 5 LSAs advertising external routes into OSPF. ABRs connect OSPF areas to Area 0.

questions.question: A user reports they cannot access a website by name but can access it by IP address. What is the most likely cause?
options: DNS resolution failure — the DNS server is unreachable or misconfigured
