hints: VTP client: receives VLAN database only; cannot create/modify VLANs
Use VTP transparent or VTP off for local VLAN management

questions.question: What is the Cisco default VLAN for untagged traffic on a trunk port?
options: VLAN 1 — the default native VLAN on Cisco IOS
questions.explanation: Cisco IOS default native VLAN on 802.1Q trunk ports is VLAN 1. Untagged frames received on a trunk are assigned to the native VLAN. Best practice: change the native VLAN to an unused VLAN (e.g. VLAN 999) and ensure both sides match to prevent VLAN hopping attacks and misassignment of untagged traffic.
hints: Default native VLAN = 1 on Cisco
Change to unused VLAN for security (mitigate VLAN hopping)

questions.question: STP is calculating the root port on a non-root switch. Two paths exist: path A (cost 4) and path B (cost 4, tie). How is the tiebreaker resolved?
options: Lowest sender Bridge ID → lowest sender port priority → lowest sender port ID
questions.explanation: STP root port tiebreaker order (all for the path toward root): 1. Lowest root path cost. 2. Lowest sender (upstream) Bridge ID. 3. Lowest sender port priority (default 128). 4. Lowest sender port ID (port number). These tiebreakers apply in sequence — move to the next only if previous is equal.
hints: Tiebreakers in order: lowest root cost → lowest sender BID → lowest sender port priority → lowest sender port ID
Port priority default = 128; lower = preferred

questions.question: What is the purpose of "switchport trunk native vlan 999" and "vlan 999" — why configure both?
options: Only "switchport trunk native vlan 999" is needed; "vlan 999" is for a different feature
Both are required: the native VLAN must exist in the VLAN database (vlan 999) before traffic is placed in it; without both, untagged traffic may be dropped or assigned incorrectly
Only "vlan 999" is needed; native VLAN changes automatically
VLAN 999 is reserved and cannot be used as native
questions.explanation: "switchport trunk native vlan 999" sets the native VLAN for untagged traffic. For the VLAN to be active, it must also exist in the local VLAN database ("vlan 999"). If the native VLAN is not in the VLAN database, the port will place untagged frames into a non-existent VLAN — they will be dropped by STP (inactive VLAN).
hints: Native VLAN must be set AND created in VLAN database
Untagged frames assigned to a non-existent VLAN are dropped

questions.question: What is the difference between PortFast and BPDU Guard?
options: PortFast and BPDU Guard are the same feature
PortFast skips listening/learning states for faster access port activation; BPDU Guard disables the port if a BPDU is received (protects against rogue switches on PortFast ports)
BPDU Guard enables rapid RSTP convergence; PortFast blocks unauthorized BPDUs
PortFast is for trunk ports; BPDU Guard is for access ports only
questions.explanation: PortFast: transitions access ports directly to forwarding (skips 802.1D listening+learning or RSTP learning delay). Suitable for end-device ports. BPDU Guard: if a PortFast port receives a BPDU (indicating a switch connected instead of a host), err-disables the port immediately. They work together: PortFast speeds up, BPDU Guard protects.
hints: PortFast: skip STP transitions for end-device ports
BPDU Guard: err-disable if BPDU received (rogue switch protection)

questions.question: An access port has "switchport access vlan 30". The VLAN 30 is deleted from the VLAN database. What happens to traffic on that port?
options: Traffic continues — the port remembers VLAN 30
The port is placed in VLAN 1 automatically
The port becomes inactive — it is associated with a non-existent VLAN and traffic is dropped
questions.explanation: If the VLAN assigned to an access port is deleted from the VLAN database, that port becomes inactive (shown as "inactive" in "show interfaces status"). Traffic is dropped because the VLAN does not exist. The port assignment remains (still shows "switchport access vlan 30") but no traffic flows until VLAN 30 is recreated.
hints: Deleted VLAN = access port becomes inactive
Port assignment remains; recreate the VLAN to restore connectivity

questions.question: "switchport mode trunk" — does this generate DTP frames?
options: Yes — trunk mode actively sends DTP to negotiate trunking with the neighbor (unless "nonegotiate" is added)
Only if the neighbor is in "auto" mode
DTP frames are sent only on access ports
questions.explanation: "switchport mode trunk" sets the port to unconditional trunk AND by default continues to send DTP frames to help the other side also become a trunk. To fully disable DTP (recommended for security on trunks to non-Cisco devices or static configs), add "switchport nonegotiate". "mode trunk" + "nonegotiate" = forced trunk, no DTP frames.
hints: trunk mode still sends DTP by default
Add "nonegotiate" to stop DTP frames: "switchport nonegotiate"

questions.question: Root Guard is configured on a switch port. What happens if a superior BPDU is received on that port?
options: The switch elects a new root based on the superior BPDU
The port is placed in root-inconsistent state and stops forwarding — the superior BPDU is ignored
The switch sends a TCN to the current root
questions.explanation: Root Guard prevents designated ports from becoming root ports. If a superior BPDU (with a lower BID than the current root) is received on a Root Guard port, the port enters "root-inconsistent" STP state (BLK) and drops all traffic until the superior BPDUs stop. It automatically recovers once superior BPDUs cease — unlike BPDU Guard which requires manual recovery.
hints: Root Guard: prevents topology changes from unauthorized root elections
root-inconsistent = blocking; auto-recovers when superior BPDUs stop

questions.question: What is Rapid PVST+ and how does it improve convergence over PVST+?
options: Rapid PVST+ uses faster timers (5s max-age) vs PVST+ (20s)
Rapid PVST+ (IEEE 802.1w per-VLAN) uses edge ports, alternate ports, and proposal/agreement mechanism for near-instant convergence vs PVST+ (802.1D) which relies on timers (30-50s)
Rapid PVST+ only works on GigE links; PVST+ works on all speeds
Rapid PVST+ eliminates the need for root bridge election
questions.explanation: Rapid PVST+ implements RSTP (802.1w) per VLAN. Key improvements: edge ports (PortFast equivalent) go straight to forwarding. Proposal/agreement handshake between switches enables link-by-link rapid transitions without waiting for max-age + 2×forward_delay (up to 50s in 802.1D). Alternate and backup ports provide instant failover without reconvergence.
hints: Rapid PVST+ = RSTP per VLAN (802.1w implementation)
Proposal/agreement = near-instant convergence vs timer-based 802.1D (up to 50s)

questions.question: What is the maximum number of VLANs supported in a Cisco switch VLAN database (normal range)?
questions.explanation: Normal VLAN range: 1–1005 (1-4 and 1006 are reserved by Cisco; usable: 2-1001). Extended VLAN range: 1006–4094 (requires VTP v3 or transparent mode on most platforms). Total possible: 4094 (12-bit VLAN ID field in 802.1Q: 2^12 = 4096, minus reserved values 0 and 4095).
hints: Normal range: 1-1005; extended: 1006-4094
802.1Q has 12-bit VLAN field = 4096 values (0 and 4095 reserved)

questions.question: EtherChannel is configured. "show etherchannel summary" shows Po1 as "SU". What does "SU" indicate?
options: S=Layer 2 (switched), U=In-use (the bundle is up and operating)
questions.explanation: EtherChannel summary flags: S = Layer 2 EtherChannel (switched). U = in-use (bundle is active and forwarding). Common combinations: SU = L2 in-use. RU = Layer 3 (routed) in-use. D = down. P = bundled in port-channel. I = stand-alone. H = hot-standby (LACP only).
hints: S = Layer 2 EtherChannel
U = in-use/active

questions.question: CDP is a security risk because it reveals device information to any connected device. How do you disable it on a single interface?
options: On the interface: "no cdp enable" — disables CDP on that interface only while keeping it global
CDP cannot be disabled per interface
Use "cdp filter" to block specific information
questions.explanation: "no cdp run" disables CDP globally on the device. "no cdp enable" on an interface disables CDP on just that interface. Best practice: disable CDP on external-facing ports (internet, DMZ, provider connections) while keeping it on internal inter-device links for troubleshooting value.
hints: "no cdp enable" (interface command) = per-interface disable
"no cdp run" = global disable

questions.question: What is the purpose of "spanning-tree loopguard default"?
options: Prevents BPDU loops on access ports
Prevents a port that stops receiving BPDUs from transitioning to forwarding (loop guard puts it in loop-inconsistent state instead of designated/forwarding)
Enables loop detection on all physical ports regardless of STP state
Reduces STP convergence time by bypassing loop detection
questions.explanation: Loop Guard prevents unidirectional link failures from causing STP loops. Normally if BPDUs stop arriving on a blocking port, STP transitions it to designated/forwarding (thinking the neighbor disappeared) — a loop occurs if the link is still physically up. Loop Guard instead places the port in "loop-inconsistent" (blocking) state. Auto-recovers when BPDUs resume.
hints: Loop Guard: port stops receiving BPDUs → loop-inconsistent (not forwarding)
Prevents unidirectional link failure from creating STP loops

questions.question: What command sets a switch port as an access port and assigns it to VLAN 50?
options: In interface config: "switchport mode access" then "switchport access vlan 50"
In global config: "interface vlan 50" then "switchport access"
questions.explanation: Two required commands in interface configuration mode: (1) "switchport mode access" — sets the port to permanent access mode (no DTP negotiation). (2) "switchport access vlan 50" — assigns the port to VLAN 50. Order matters: "mode access" first prevents unintended trunk negotiation.
hints: "switchport mode access" = access mode (no DTP)
"switchport access vlan 50" = assign to VLAN 50

questions.question: PAgP modes: auto and desirable. What combination forms an EtherChannel?
options: desirable+desirable, or desirable+auto; auto+auto does NOT form a bundle
questions.explanation: PAgP negotiation: "desirable" actively sends PAgP PDUs. "auto" passively waits. For an EtherChannel: desirable+desirable = yes (both active). desirable+auto = yes (desirable initiates, auto responds). auto+auto = NO (neither sends PDUs). Compare LACP: active+active=yes, active+passive=yes, passive+passive=NO.
hints: PAgP desirable = active; auto = passive
auto+auto = no bundle (neither initiates)

questions.question: How does a switch handle a frame destined for a MAC address that IS in its CAM table?
options: Floods it to verify the destination is still reachable
Forwards it unicast to the single port associated with that MAC address
Sends it to the CPU for routing
Broadcasts it then discards redundant copies
questions.explanation: Known unicast forwarding: the switch looks up the destination MAC in the CAM (content-addressable memory / MAC table). If found, the frame is forwarded out ONLY the associated port (unicast — not flooded). If not found, it is flooded. This is the fundamental switching operation.
hints: Known unicast = single port forwarding (no flood)
Unknown unicast = flooded within VLAN

questions.question: What does "show mac address-table aging-time" display and what is the default?
options: The maximum number of MACs; default 1024
The time in seconds after which an inactive MAC entry is flushed; default 300 seconds (5 minutes)
The time between BPDU transmissions; default 2 seconds
The MAC learning rate limit; default 100 per second
questions.explanation: MAC address table aging: each entry has a timer reset when traffic is seen from that MAC. If no traffic for the aging time (default 300s), the entry is deleted. This prevents the table from filling with stale entries from disconnected devices. During STP topology changes, aging is shortened to forward_delay (15s) to flush stale entries faster.
hints: MAC aging default = 300 seconds (5 minutes)
Entry deleted if no traffic seen for aging-time duration

questions.question: What is the function of the "spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary" command?
options: Declares the switch as root with priority 0
Sets the switch priority to 24576 (or lower than current root) to ensure it becomes root for VLAN 10
Manually assigns a static root bridge ignoring BID
Forces all switches to recalculate their root port
questions.explanation: "spanning-tree vlan 10 root primary" macro: Cisco IOS checks the current root bridge priority and sets this switch's priority to either 24576 (if the current root has default priority 32768) or current_root_priority - 4096 (to be lower). It does NOT simply set priority to 0. For an explicit value, use "spanning-tree vlan 10 priority 4096".
hints: "root primary" sets priority to 24576 or lower than current root
Explicit: "spanning-tree vlan N priority 4096" for precise control

questions.question: Two switches, both Cisco, connected via a trunk. Native VLAN mismatch: SW1=VLAN 1, SW2=VLAN 10. What error appears in syslog?
options: CDP: Native VLAN mismatch discovered on port
%CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH: Native VLAN mismatch discovered on Gi0/1
questions.explanation: Cisco CDP detects native VLAN mismatches on trunk links and generates a syslog message: %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH. CDP compares the native VLAN advertised by each side. This is a warning (severity 4) — the trunk continues to operate but VLAN hopping is possible and untagged traffic is misassigned.
hints: CDP detects native VLAN mismatches and logs %CDP-4-NATIVE_VLAN_MISMATCH
The trunk still operates but untagged traffic is misassigned

questions.question: Which STP timer controls how long a port stays in listening and learning states?
options: Forward delay timer (default 15s per state = 30s total listening+learning)
questions.explanation: STP timers: Hello (2s) = interval between BPDUs sent by root. Max-age (20s) = how long a switch stores a BPDU before considering a neighbor down. Forward delay (15s) = duration of both listening state and learning state. Total transition from blocking to forwarding (802.1D): max-age + 2×forward_delay = 20+30 = 50s in the worst case.
hints: Forward delay = 15s per state (listening + learning = 30s)
Total worst-case convergence: max-age (20s) + 2×forward_delay (30s) = 50s

questions.question: A port channel (EtherChannel) interface is configured with an IP address. What is the purpose?
options: EtherChannel cannot have an IP address
A routed port-channel (Layer 3 EtherChannel) — the logical interface carries routed traffic; physical member ports have "no switchport" and no individual IPs
It enables SVI routing for the EtherChannel VLAN
The IP is used for LACP management only
questions.explanation: Layer 3 EtherChannel: configure "no switchport" on each physical member port (removes L2 switching), then configure an IP address on the Port-channel interface. The physical members carry the aggregated L3 routed traffic. No VLANs or 802.1Q needed. Use for high-bandwidth Layer 3 links between routers or multilayer switches.
hints: L3 EtherChannel: "no switchport" on members + IP on port-channel interface
L2 EtherChannel: no "no switchport" needed; can be trunk or access

questions.question: What does "vlan dot1q tag native" global command do on a Cisco switch?
options: Forces the switch to always use VLAN 1 as native
Causes the switch to tag native VLAN traffic with 802.1Q instead of sending it untagged; used for consistency and security
Disables 802.1Q and forces ISL on all trunks
Enables double-tagging VLAN hopping for testing
questions.explanation: "vlan dot1q tag native" causes the switch to send native VLAN traffic WITH an 802.1Q tag (explicitly tagged with the native VLAN ID). This ensures both ends process it correctly and prevents VLAN hopping attacks based on double-tagging. Required on some newer platforms by default. Both sides must have this enabled or disabled consistently.
hints: Tags native VLAN traffic instead of sending it untagged
Prevents double-tagging VLAN hopping; both sides must match

questions.question: A port in "show spanning-tree vlan 20" shows role=Desg, State=FWD. What does this mean?
options: The port is designated (forwarding toward the non-root segment) and actively forwarding data
The port is in the process of becoming root
The port failed STP election and is forwarding anyway
questions.explanation: STP port roles: Root (best path toward root), Designated (best path away from root on a segment), Alternate/Backup (blocked). States: BLK=Blocking, LIS=Listening, LRN=Learning, FWD=Forwarding. Desg+FWD = this port is the designated port for its segment and actively forwarding frames — the normal operating state for designated ports.
hints: Desg = Designated port (best path away from root on this segment)
FWD = Forwarding (actively passing data frames)

questions.question: What is the purpose of PVLAN (Private VLAN) and name its port types?
options: Same as standard VLAN — PVLAN adds no new features
PVLAN isolates hosts within the same subnet from each other. Port types: Promiscuous (talks to all), Isolated (only talks to promiscuous), Community (talks to same-community + promiscuous)
PVLAN creates a separate routing domain per VLAN
PVLAN is used for multi-tenant firewalls only
questions.explanation: Private VLAN (PVLAN) allows host isolation within the same IP subnet. Use case: hotel/ISP where customers share a subnet but should not communicate directly. Port types: Promiscuous (connected to router/gateway — can talk to all). Isolated (most restricted — only communicates with promiscuous). Community (communicates with same-community members + promiscuous).
hints: PVLAN = intra-subnet isolation
Promiscuous=gateway; Isolated=no peer comm; Community=same group only

questions.question: UDLD (Unidirectional Link Detection) is enabled. A fiber cut makes the link unidirectional (TX works, RX fails). What does UDLD do?
options: Detects the unidirectional failure and err-disables the port; prevents STP loops caused by a port that can send but not receive BPDUs
Sends a syslog warning but keeps the port up
questions.explanation: UDLD monitors bidirectional link integrity by sending periodic UDLD frames. If echoed frames are not received back (unidirectional failure), UDLD err-disables the port. This prevents STP loops: a port that can send but not receive BPDUs looks up in STP (no BPDUs received → transitions to forwarding) while actually not hearing the topology.
hints: UDLD: detects one-way link failures (TX ok, RX broken)
Err-disables the port to prevent STP loops from unidirectional links

questions.question: What happens to OSPF adjacencies if the "router-id" is changed on a running router?
options: Adjacencies update automatically with the new RID
The OSPF process must be cleared ("clear ip ospf process") for the new RID to take effect; until then, the old RID remains in use
Adjacencies reset immediately upon RID change
RID changes are not permitted on a running OSPF process
questions.explanation: OSPF RID is set at process startup. Changing "router-id" in config takes effect only after the OSPF process restarts ("clear ip ospf process" or reload). Until restarted, the old RID is used. After clearing, all adjacencies drop and reform with the new RID — this causes a brief traffic disruption.
hints: RID change requires OSPF process restart to take effect
clear ip ospf process = drops all adjacencies, reforms with new RID

questions.question: OSPF stub area: which LSA types are blocked from entering a stub area?
options: Type 5 (AS External) LSAs; the ABR injects a default route (Type 3) instead
questions.explanation: Stub area: blocks Type 5 (AS External) LSAs from ASBRs. The ABR injects a single Type 3 default route (0.0.0.0/0) into the stub area. Totally stubby area (Cisco extension): also blocks Type 3 inter-area LSAs — only Type 1 and Type 2 intra-area LSAs allowed; single default route for everything external. Reduces LSDB size significantly.
hints: Stub: no Type 5 (external) LSAs; ABR injects default route
Totally stubby: no Type 3 or Type 5; only intra-area + default

questions.question: Two OSPF areas: Area 0 (backbone) and Area 1. Which router type connects them and what LSA does it use?
options: ABR (Area Border Router) using Type 3 (Summary) LSAs to advertise inter-area routes
DR using Type 2 LSAs to connect areas
No special router needed — OSPF areas connect directly
questions.explanation: ABR (Area Border Router): router with interfaces in multiple OSPF areas, including Area 0. Generates Type 3 (Summary) LSAs to advertise routes from one area into another. ABR maintains a separate LSDB per area. Rules: all non-backbone areas must connect to Area 0 via an ABR.
hints: ABR = connects non-backbone area to Area 0
Type 3 = Summary LSA, generated by ABR for inter-area routes

questions.question: "ip route 192.168.1.0 255.255.255.0 Gi0/0" — what problem may arise with this recursive static route on a multi-access (Ethernet) interface?
options: It works correctly on Ethernet; no problem

The route requires the router to ARP for every destination in 192.168.1.0/24 individually since the next-hop is an interface not an IP — results in proxy ARP flooding or incomplete ARP entries on large subnets
Static routes cannot use interfaces — only IP addresses
It only works with directly connected networks
questions.explanation: Static routes pointing to an exit interface (no next-hop IP) on Ethernet: the router treats every destination in 192.168.1.0/24 as directly connected and ARPs for each destination IP. On large subnets this is inefficient — better to specify a next-hop IP: "ip route 192.168.1.0 255.255.255.0 10.0.0.1". Point-to-point interfaces (serial) are fine with interface-only routes.
hints: Interface-only static on Ethernet = ARP for each destination IP (proxy ARP issue)
Always use next-hop IP on Ethernet: "ip route x.x.x.x mask next-hop-ip"

questions.question: EIGRP has a concept of "stuck-in-active" (SIA). When does this occur?
options: When a route is always the best and never needs to be recalculated
When a query is sent for a lost route but no reply is received within the active timer (default 3 minutes); the neighbor relationship is then reset
When a route has equal metrics to two destinations
When EIGRP hello packets are delayed
questions.explanation: SIA (Stuck-In-Active): when a route's successor and all feasible successors fail, EIGRP sends QUERY messages to all neighbors. If a neighbor does not reply within the active timer (3 minutes default), the neighbor is declared SIA-stuck and the adjacency is reset. Common cause: slow WAN links or a neighbor that is also in active state and waiting for ITS neighbors.
hints: SIA: QUERY sent, no REPLY within active timer (3 min)
Result: neighbor adjacency reset; indicates EIGRP scalability issue

questions.question: What does "maximum-paths 4" in OSPF configuration do?
options: Allows OSPF to install up to 4 equal-cost routes to the same destination in the routing table (ECMP)
Sets the maximum hop count to 4
questions.explanation: "maximum-paths" sets how many equal-cost paths OSPF installs in the routing table for a destination. Default is 4 on most platforms (max 16 or 32). All paths must have equal cost (same OSPF metric). CEF then load-balances traffic across the paths using per-destination or per-packet hashing.
hints: maximum-paths = ECMP (equal-cost multi-path) entries per destination
All paths must have equal OSPF metric to be included

questions.question: What is the purpose of EIGRP passive-interface?
options: Disables EIGRP completely on the interface
Prevents EIGRP hello packets from being sent/received on the interface while still advertising the interface's network into EIGRP
Puts the interface in standby mode for failover
Blocks all routing updates but allows hello packets
questions.explanation: "passive-interface" in EIGRP (and OSPF/RIP) stops hello packets on the specified interface — no neighbor adjacency forms. The network attached to that interface is still advertised by EIGRP to other neighbors. Useful for: loopback interfaces (no neighbor needed), user-facing interfaces (prevent EIGRP PDU exposure to end users), stub WAN links.
hints: passive-interface: no hellos, no adjacency — but network still advertised
Use on loopbacks and user-facing interfaces

questions.question: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) uses which transport protocol and port?
options: TCP 179 — BGP uses TCP for reliable session establishment
EIGRP uses RTP; BGP uses UDP
questions.explanation: BGP uses TCP port 179. BGP relies on TCP's reliable delivery for its UPDATE messages — no need for its own reliability mechanism. BGP neighbors (peers) are configured manually (not discovered automatically). eBGP peers are typically directly connected; iBGP peers can be anywhere within the AS (often via loopback-to-loopback sessions).
hints: BGP = TCP 179
Neighbors configured manually; TCP provides reliability

questions.question: What is the difference between eBGP and iBGP?
options: eBGP = exterior (between different ASes); iBGP = interior (within the same AS). eBGP default TTL=1 (direct connection required); iBGP default TTL=255
eBGP uses TCP; iBGP uses UDP
eBGP is for IPv4; iBGP is for IPv6
eBGP requires IGP; iBGP is the IGP
questions.explanation: eBGP (external BGP): between routers in different Autonomous Systems. TTL=1 by default — peers must be directly connected (or use "ebgp-multihop" for indirect). iBGP (internal BGP): between routers in the same AS. TTL=255. iBGP requires full mesh or route reflectors (iBGP split horizon: routes learned via iBGP not advertised to other iBGP peers).
hints: eBGP = different AS; iBGP = same AS
eBGP TTL=1 (direct); iBGP TTL=255 (any path within AS)

questions.question: OSPF area 0 is the backbone. What happens if a non-zero area is not connected to Area 0?
options: The area works normally — Area 0 is optional
Routes from that area cannot reach other areas — inter-area routing fails. Solution: virtual link through a transit area or redesign
The area becomes a stub area automatically
questions.explanation: OSPF rule: all non-backbone areas MUST connect to Area 0 directly. If Area 2 connects to Area 1 (not Area 0) and Area 1 connects to Area 0, Area 2 routes cannot be redistributed properly into Area 0. Fix: virtual link through Area 1 (if Area 1 is not stub) to logically connect Area 2 to Area 0, or redesign the area topology.
hints: All OSPF areas must connect to Area 0
Disconnected area: use virtual link as temporary fix, or redesign

questions.question: "show ip route" shows "L 10.1.1.1/32" on a router interface. What does the "L" prefix mean?
options: L = Local route — automatically created by IOS for the exact IP address of a configured interface (host route /32); used to receive packets addressed to the router itself
L = learned via OSPF local area
questions.explanation: IOS automatically creates a /32 "L" (Local) route for each interface IP address. This ensures packets addressed to the router itself (management, OSPF hellos, SSH sessions) are handled locally without using the routing table. "C" routes are the connected subnet. "L" routes are the specific host address.
hints: L = Local: /32 host route for the router's own interface IP
C = Connected subnet; L = Exact interface IP address

questions.question: What is split horizon and how does it prevent routing loops in RIP?
options: Prevents routes from being advertised back out the interface they were learned on; if R1 learns route to 10.0.0.0 from R2 via Gi0/1, R1 will not advertise 10.0.0.0 back to R2 on Gi0/1
Splits large routing tables into multiple update packets
Limits the number of hops a route can traverse to 15
Sends routes with metric 16 to prevent loops
questions.explanation: Split horizon: do not advertise a route back out the interface it was received on. Prevents: if R2's link to 10.0.0.0 fails, R1 would otherwise re-advertise 10.0.0.0 back to R2 (thinking it has an alternate path), causing R2 to think 10.0.0.0 is still reachable via R1 — a routing loop. Split horizon with poison reverse sends metric 16 (infinity) back to explicitly announce the route is unreachable.
hints: Split horizon: don't advertise routes back the way they came
Poison reverse: advertise the route back with metric 16 (explicit unreachable)

questions.question: A router has OSPF and EIGRP both running and both learn 192.168.50.0/24. Which installs in the routing table?
options: EIGRP — lower default AD (90) wins over OSPF (110)
questions.explanation: When different protocols learn the same route, the one with lower Administrative Distance wins and is installed in the routing table. EIGRP internal AD=90. OSPF AD=110. 90 < 110, so EIGRP's route wins. The OSPF route sits in the protocol's topology table but is not installed in the IP routing table.
hints: EIGRP internal AD=90; OSPF AD=110
Lower AD = installed in routing table; higher AD route held in reserve

questions.question: What is the purpose of OSPF "area X range" command on an ABR?
options: Defines the IP range for OSPF area membership
Summarizes multiple intra-area routes into a single Type 3 LSA for advertisement into other areas (manual summarization at the ABR)
Limits the number of routes in an area
Creates a summary route injected into area X
questions.explanation: "area X range A.B.C.D mask" on an ABR: summarizes all routes within Area X that fall within the specified range into a single Type 3 Summary LSA advertised to other areas. Reduces LSDB size and improves scalability. The summary covers the range even if individual routes do not all exist — creates an aggregated advertisement.
hints: area X range = ABR manual summarization → one Type 3 LSA per summary
Reduces inter-area LSA count and LSDB size

questions.question: A router's routing table has: 10.0.0.0/8 via OSPF, 10.1.0.0/16 via EIGRP, 10.1.1.0/24 via RIP. A packet arrives for 10.1.1.50. Which route is used?
options: 10.1.1.0/24 — longest prefix match wins regardless of AD
Tied — all three contain the destination
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match always wins in IP routing, regardless of AD or metric. 10.1.1.0/24 (/24 = 24 bits match) is more specific than 10.1.0.0/16 (/16 = 16 bits) and 10.0.0.0/8 (/8 = 8 bits). The most specific matching route is always used first.
hints: Longest prefix match: most specific route wins — AD and metric are irrelevant
/24 > /16 > /8 in specificity

questions.question: What is the wildcard mask equivalent of the subnet mask 255.255.255.240?
questions.explanation: Wildcard mask = inverse of subnet mask. 255.255.255.240 → invert each byte: 0.0.0.15. This is used in ACLs and OSPF network statements to specify address ranges. 0.0.0.15 matches the last 4 bits of the address (16 addresses per block = /28).
hints: Wildcard = 255.255.255.255 - subnet mask

questions.question: ACL 150: "permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.255.255 host 203.0.113.10 eq 443". What traffic does this permit?
options: Any TCP traffic from 203.0.113.10 to 10.0.x.x on port 443
TCP traffic from any host in 10.0.0.0–10.0.255.255 (10.0.x.x) destined to the single host 203.0.113.10 on destination port 443 (HTTPS)
TCP traffic from 10.0.x.x on source port 443 to anywhere
questions.explanation: "permit tcp [source] [src-wildcard] [destination] [dst-wildcard] eq [port]": source=10.0.0.0/0.0.255.255 (10.0.0.0–10.0.255.255), destination="host 203.0.113.10" (/32), eq 443 = destination port 443 (HTTPS). Protocol = TCP. Permits: any 10.0.x.x host → 203.0.113.10 on TCP 443.
hints: src=10.0.x.x wildcard 0.0.255.255; dst=host 203.0.113.10; port 443=dest port
eq on the destination side = destination port filter

questions.question: PAT (NAT overload) is configured but all inside hosts fail to reach the internet. "show ip nat translations" shows no entries. What should you check first?
options: Whether the inside hosts have IPv6 addresses
Whether "ip nat inside" and "ip nat outside" are applied to the correct interfaces, and whether the ACL for NAT overload matches the inside hosts
Whether OSPF is redistributing the NAT routes
Whether the outside interface has a default route
questions.explanation: PAT troubleshooting: (1) Check "ip nat inside" on LAN interface and "ip nat outside" on WAN interface — a common misconfiguration. (2) Verify the ACL (or named access-list) used in "ip nat inside source list X interface Y overload" actually matches inside host addresses. (3) Ensure there is a default route pointing to the outside interface.
hints: ip nat inside/outside must be on correct interfaces
ACL must match inside hosts; default route needed for outside routing

questions.question: GLBP (Gateway Load Balancing Protocol) differs from HSRP in what fundamental way?
options: GLBP is Cisco-proprietary; HSRP is open
GLBP allows ALL gateway routers to forward traffic simultaneously (true load balancing); HSRP has one active and one standby — only one forwards at a time
GLBP uses UDP; HSRP uses TCP
questions.explanation: HSRP: one active router forwards all traffic; standby is idle. GLBP: all routers in the group can be active virtual forwarders (AVFs) simultaneously. The AVG (Active Virtual Gateway) assigns different virtual MACs to different AVFs — hosts ARP and receive different MACs, distributing load. True active-active load balancing unlike HSRP/VRRP's active-standby model.
hints: GLBP: multiple AVFs active simultaneously (load balancing)
HSRP/VRRP: one active, rest standby (no load balancing)

questions.question: An ACL has "deny tcp any any eq 23" but not "permit ip any any". What happens to HTTPS traffic?
options: HTTPS (port 443) is denied — all TCP is blocked
HTTPS is denied — the implicit deny at the end drops all unmatched traffic
HTTPS is permitted — the ACL only denies Telnet (23); implicit deny catches everything else including HTTPS
HTTPS is permitted by the TCP permit statement
questions.explanation: ACL processing: "deny tcp any any eq 23" denies Telnet. HTTPS (TCP 443) does not match this ACE. The next ACE is the implicit "deny ip any any" at the end — which matches HTTPS and denies it. Without an explicit "permit ip any any" or "permit tcp any any", all non-Telnet traffic is also denied by the implicit deny.
hints: Implicit deny at end blocks everything not explicitly permitted
Must add "permit ip any any" if you only want to deny specific traffic

questions.question: What is the purpose of NTP authentication and how is it configured?
options: Encrypts NTP traffic end-to-end using AES
Ensures NTP clients only synchronize with trusted (authenticated) NTP servers using MD5 or SHA-1 HMAC; configured with "ntp authentication-key" + "ntp authenticate" + "ntp trusted-key"
NTP authentication controls which users can change time settings
Enables NTP to use HTTPS for time synchronization
questions.explanation: NTP authentication prevents clients from syncing to rogue NTP servers (NTP spoofing attacks can shift time, breaking certificate validity, log correlation, etc.). Uses symmetric key (MD5 or SHA-1 HMAC). Config: (1) ntp authentication-key <id> md5 <key>, (2) ntp authenticate (enables auth check), (3) ntp trusted-key <id> (which keys to trust), (4) ntp server <ip> key <id> (on client).
hints: NTP auth prevents sync to rogue servers
Keys: ntp authentication-key → ntp authenticate → ntp trusted-key → ntp server ... key

questions.question: HSRP preempt delay is configured as "standby 1 preempt delay minimum 60". What is the effect?
options: HSRP waits 60 ms before taking over
After recovering, the higher-priority router waits 60 seconds before preempting the current active router; allows routing protocols time to reconverge before taking over
Preemption is permanently disabled for 60 seconds after boot
The standby router becomes active after 60 failed hello packets
questions.explanation: Preempt delay minimum N: when a router with higher priority comes back online, it waits N seconds before preempting the current active. This prevents the router from immediately taking over before its routing table and OSPF/EIGRP adjacencies reconverge — avoiding a brief traffic black hole if it takes over before it has full routing information.
hints: Preempt delay = wait for IGP reconvergence before taking active role
Prevents black hole: router takes over before routing table is complete

questions.question: What is the difference between SNMP trap and SNMP inform?
options: Traps are for errors; informs are for information only
Trap = unacknowledged UDP notification (fire and forget); Inform = acknowledged (manager must reply; retransmitted if no ACK)
Informs use TCP; traps use UDP; both are equivalent
Traps go to the manager; informs go to agents
questions.explanation: SNMP trap (SNMPv1/v2c/v3): UDP, no acknowledgment. If the packet is lost, the agent does not know. SNMP inform (SNMPv2c/v3): also UDP but includes a sequence number; the manager sends an acknowledgment. If the agent does not receive an ACK within a timeout, it retransmits. Informs are more reliable for critical event notifications.
hints: Trap = UDP, no ACK (fire and forget)
Inform = UDP with ACK and retransmit (more reliable)

questions.question: An extended ACL matches "ip any any" (permit) as the first ACE. What happens to a DNS query (UDP 53)?
options: DNS is denied — ACL 1 only matches TCP
DNS is permitted — "ip any any" matches all IP traffic including UDP
DNS is blocked — UDP is not an IP protocol
Permit ip any any also disables the implicit deny
questions.explanation: "permit ip any any" matches ALL IP traffic regardless of transport protocol (TCP, UDP, ICMP, EIGRP, OSPF, etc.). "ip" in an extended ACL means IP protocol, which encompasses all protocols carried in IP. This is equivalent to "permit everything" — no filtering occurs.
hints: "ip" in ACE = all IP protocols (TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc.)
permit ip any any = allow all IP traffic — no filtering

questions.question: What is "conditional NAT" (route-map-based NAT) used for?
options: NAT that only translates when a specific route exists in the routing table
Using a route-map to select which traffic is NAT-translated based on multiple criteria (source IP, destination IP, source port, protocol) — more granular than ACL-based NAT selection
NAT for IPv6 to IPv4 translation
Dynamic NAT that conditionally applies different pools
questions.explanation: Route-map NAT uses a route-map (which can match ACLs, IP addresses, ports, and perform set operations) to select precisely which traffic undergoes NAT translation. More flexible than simple ACL matching: for example, translate traffic from 10.0.0.0/24 going to 8.8.8.8 but NOT traffic going to 192.168.0.0/16.
hints: Route-map NAT = precise traffic selection using multiple match criteria
More granular than ACL-only NAT selection

questions.question: What is the purpose of "ip dhcp excluded-address 10.0.0.1 10.0.0.10" in DHCP server configuration?
options: Blocks those IPs from being pinged
Reserves those IPs so the DHCP server does not assign them to clients; used for statically-assigned devices (routers, servers, printers)
Prevents hosts with those IPs from connecting
questions.explanation: "ip dhcp excluded-address" reserves IP addresses that should NOT be dynamically assigned. These are typically addresses already statically configured on network devices (router interfaces, servers, printers, access points). The DHCP server skips these addresses when assigning from the pool.
hints: excluded-address = reserved, not assigned by DHCP
Use for: router interfaces, servers, printers, any statically-configured device

questions.question: A numbered ACL (ACL 101) needs a new entry inserted between existing lines. What is the only way to do this in IOS?
options: Delete the entire ACL and recreate it with the new entry in the correct position (or convert to named ACL with sequence numbers)
Standard ACLs support in-place insertion; extended ACLs do not
questions.explanation: Numbered ACLs do not support sequence-number-based insertion or selective deletion of individual ACEs (pre-IOS 12.4). To insert a line: (1) Copy ACL to text, modify it, paste it back — requires deleting and recreating. Named ACLs: "ip access-list extended NAME" supports sequence numbers, allowing insertion at any sequence number and deletion of specific entries. Convert numbered to named for easier management.
hints: Numbered ACL: no selective insertion — delete and recreate
Named ACL: sequence numbers allow insertion at any position

questions.question: HSRP uses virtual IP and virtual MAC. What is the virtual MAC format for HSRP group 5, version 1?
questions.explanation: HSRPv1 virtual MAC format: 0000.0C07.ACxx where xx = group number in hex. Group 5 = hex 05. Virtual MAC = 0000.0C07.AC05. HSRPv2: 0000.0C9F.Fxxx (12-bit group). VRRP: 0000.5E00.01xx.
hints: HSRPv1 MAC: 0000.0C07.AC + group (hex)
Group 5 hex = 05 → 0000.0C07.AC05

questions.question: "show ip dhcp conflict" lists 192.168.1.50. What does this mean and how did it get there?
options: The DHCP server failed to assign 192.168.1.50
The DHCP server detected 192.168.1.50 is already in use (via ping or gratuitous ARP detection before assignment); it will not assign this IP to clients until the conflict is cleared
The host at .50 violated DHCP snooping policy
The DHCP pool is exhausted at 192.168.1.50
questions.explanation: DHCP conflict detection: before assigning an IP, the Cisco DHCP server pings the address (default 2 pings). If a response is received, the address is already in use — the server marks it as a conflict and skips it. The admin must run "clear ip dhcp conflict *" (or specific IP) to return conflicted addresses to the available pool.
hints: DHCP conflict: server pinged the IP, got a reply — already in use
Clear with: clear ip dhcp conflict <ip> or clear ip dhcp conflict *

questions.question: What QoS mechanism uses CBWFQ (Class-Based Weighted Fair Queuing)?
options: Classifies traffic and assigns minimum bandwidth guarantees to each class; excess bandwidth shared proportionally
Randomly drops packets from all queues to prevent congestion
Prioritizes voice traffic only; all other traffic is best-effort
Marks traffic with DSCP values but does not queue differently
questions.explanation: CBWFQ: traffic is classified into user-defined classes. Each class is assigned a minimum bandwidth guarantee (in bps or percentage). If a class has traffic, it receives at least its guaranteed bandwidth. Excess bandwidth (beyond all guarantees) is shared proportionally. LLQ (Low Latency Queuing) adds a strict priority queue on top of CBWFQ for voice traffic (DSCP EF).
hints: CBWFQ: minimum bandwidth guarantee per class
LLQ = CBWFQ + strict priority queue for voice (prevents jitter)

questions.question: What does "ip helper-address 10.0.0.5" on a router interface do?
options: Sets the DHCP server address for that interface
Enables DHCP relay — converts DHCP broadcast DISCOVERs from the connected subnet into unicast and forwards them to the DHCP server at 10.0.0.5
questions.explanation: "ip helper-address" enables UDP broadcast forwarding (DHCP relay). When a DHCP DISCOVER (broadcast) arrives on the interface, the router converts it to a unicast packet destined for the helper address (DHCP server) and forwards it. The DHCP server responds to the router's interface IP (gateway IP = giaddr field). By default also relays: TFTP, DNS, NetBIOS, and other UDP broadcast services.
hints: ip helper-address = DHCP relay (broadcast → unicast forwarding)
giaddr field in DHCP tells server which subnet the client is on

questions.question: Cisco IOS ACL "remark" command is used for what purpose?
options: Adds a comment/description to an ACL for documentation; does not affect packet filtering
questions.explanation: "ip access-list extended NAME" / "remark This permits web traffic": adds a text comment into the ACL. "remark" lines appear in the running config and "show ip access-lists" output but are NOT ACEs — they never match or filter packets. Used for documentation: explaining the purpose of ACEs for other administrators.
hints: "remark" = documentation comment in ACL, not a filter rule
Appears in show output but never matches/filters traffic

questions.question: What is the purpose of the "DHCP option 43" and give a common use case?
options: Option 43 sets the DHCP lease time
Vendor-specific information; common use: Cisco access points use option 43 to receive the WLC (Wireless LAN Controller) IP address from DHCP
Option 43 provides the DNS domain name
questions.explanation: DHCP option 43 (Vendor-Specific Information) carries vendor-specific data. Cisco lightweight APs (LAPs) use option 43 to discover the WLC IP address via DHCP — the AP sends a DHCPDISCOVER, the DHCP server includes the WLC IP in option 43, the AP connects to the WLC without manual configuration. Other uses: VoIP phones discovering call manager via option 150.
hints: Option 43 = vendor-specific data in DHCP offer
Cisco APs: option 43 contains WLC IP for zero-touch provisioning

questions.question: A host has IP 172.16.45.200/21. Which statement is correct?
hints: Block size = 256-248=8
Find which multiple of 8 fits below 45

questions.question: How many usable host addresses exist in a /28 subnet?
questions.explanation: 2^4 - 2 = 14 usable hosts. /28 leaves 4 host bits.
hints: Host bits = 32-28=4
Subtract 2 for network and broadcast

questions.question: A packet arrives at a router. The destination IP matches two routing table entries: 10.1.0.0/16 via Serial0 and 10.1.4.0/24 via Gi0. Which path does the router use?
options: The entry with lowest metric wins
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match always wins regardless of metric or interface type. /24 is more specific than /16.
hints: Routers always prefer the most specific (longest) match

questions.question: Which field in an IPv4 header is decremented by each router the packet traverses?
questions.explanation: TTL (Time to Live) is decremented by 1 at each hop. When it reaches 0 the packet is discarded and an ICMP Time Exceeded message is sent back.
hints: This prevents routing loops

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the behavior of TCP's sliding window mechanism?
options: The receiver advertises how many bytes it can accept; the sender limits in-flight data to that amount
The sender transmits one segment and waits for an ACK before sending the next
Both sides negotiate window size only during the 3-way handshake and it never changes
The window size is fixed at 65,535 bytes for all connections
questions.explanation: TCP's sliding window is dynamic. The receiver advertises its window in each ACK, allowing the sender to keep multiple segments in flight without waiting for individual ACKs.
hints: Window size is in every TCP header, not just SYN

questions.question: Host A (10.1.1.1/24) sends a packet to 10.1.2.1. Its default gateway is 10.1.1.254. What does Host A place in the destination MAC address of the Ethernet frame?
questions.explanation: The destination IP is in a different subnet. Host A cannot reach it directly, so it sends the frame to the default gateway's MAC. The IP destination remains 10.1.2.1.
hints: Layer 2 vs Layer 3 addressing — only Layer 3 carries end-to-end destination

questions.question: An Ethernet frame has a Type field value of 0x0800. What does this indicate?
options: The payload is an IPv4 packet
The payload is an ARP packet
The payload is an IPv6 packet
questions.explanation: EtherType 0x0800 = IPv4. 0x0806 = ARP, 0x86DD = IPv6, 0x8100 = 802.1Q.
hints: Memorize the common EtherType values

questions.question: What is the maximum transmission unit (MTU) of a standard Ethernet frame payload?
questions.explanation: Standard Ethernet MTU (payload) is 1500 bytes. 1518 is the full frame (1500+18 header/trailer). 1522 adds 802.1Q tag. 9000 is a jumbo frame.
hints: MTU refers to the payload, not the entire frame

questions.question: Which mechanism does a host use to determine if a destination IP is on the same subnet without knowing the destination's MAC address?
options: ARP — broadcasts a request; if destination is local it replies directly
ICMP redirect from the default gateway
Proxy ARP response from the router
questions.explanation: ARP is used to resolve IPs to MACs on the same subnet. If the destination is in the same subnet the host ARPs for its MAC directly. If remote it ARPs for the gateway.
hints: ARP only works within a subnet

questions.question: Which IPv4 address class originally defined the range 128.0.0.0 to 191.255.255.255?
hints: First octet 128-191 = Class B

questions.question: What is the purpose of the IPv4 DF (Don't Fragment) bit?
options: Tells routers not to fragment the packet; if fragmentation is needed the packet is dropped and an ICMP Unreachable is sent
Prevents the packet from being duplicated across multiple paths
Instructs routers to use the express forwarding path
Disables TTL decrement for that packet
questions.explanation: DF bit prevents fragmentation. If the packet is too large for a link MTU, the router drops it and sends ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (fragmentation needed, DF set). Path MTU Discovery uses this.
hints: DF = Don't Fragment; used in PMTU discovery

questions.question: A UDP datagram is sent to port 53. The receiving host has no application listening on port 53. What happens?
options: The host sends an ICMP Port Unreachable message
The host sends a TCP RST
The datagram is queued until an application opens port 53
questions.explanation: When UDP delivers to a closed port, the kernel sends ICMP Type 3 Code 3 (Port Unreachable). TCP would send a RST, but this is UDP.
hints: ICMP Port Unreachable is for UDP; RST is for TCP

questions.question: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for reliable delivery, segmentation, and flow control?
questions.explanation: The Transport layer (L4) provides reliable delivery (TCP), segmentation, and flow control. Network (L3) handles routing; Data Link (L2) handles framing.
hints: TCP and UDP live at Layer 4

questions.question: What is the key difference between a collision domain and a broadcast domain?
options: A collision domain is bounded by switches/bridges at L2; a broadcast domain is bounded by routers at L3
A collision domain is bounded by routers; a broadcast domain is bounded by switches
They are the same concept with different names
Switches extend both collision and broadcast domains
questions.explanation: Each switch port is its own collision domain (full-duplex eliminates collisions). A broadcast domain is bounded by routers or L3 devices — all ports in a VLAN share a broadcast domain.
hints: Switches break collision domains; routers break broadcast domains

questions.question: You have the block 192.168.10.0/24. You need 6 subnets each supporting at least 25 hosts. Which mask works?
questions.explanation: /27 = 32 addresses, 30 usable hosts, 8 subnets from a /24. Meets both requirements (≥25 hosts, ≥6 subnets). /28 only has 14 usable; /26 only gives 4 subnets.
hints: Check both host count and subnet count simultaneously

questions.question: An IP packet has the IP precedence bits set to 5. In DSCP terms, what is the equivalent PHB?
questions.explanation: IP Precedence 5 maps to CS5. CS values use the top 3 bits of the DSCP field: CS5 = 101000 binary = 40 decimal.
hints: CS (Class Selector) PHBs maintain backward compatibility with IP Precedence

questions.question: What is the purpose of the TCP FIN flag compared to RST?
options: FIN initiates a graceful four-way teardown allowing in-flight data to finish; RST abruptly terminates without draining
FIN abruptly resets the connection; RST allows graceful close
They perform identical functions; RST is the older term
FIN is used only by servers; RST only by clients
questions.explanation: FIN = graceful close (half-close, waits for remaining data). RST = abrupt termination, discards buffered data, used for errors or port-closed responses.
hints: FIN drains; RST drops

questions.question: Which two ICMP message types are used by the traceroute utility to map network paths? (Choose two)
questions.explanation: Traceroute sends probes with incrementing TTL values. Intermediate routers return ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11) when TTL hits 0. The final hop returns ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3 Code 3 for UDP) or Echo Reply for ICMP-based traceroute. Type 11 and Type 3 are the key responses.
hints: TTL expiry → Time Exceeded; destination reached → Port Unreachable or Echo Reply

questions.question: You perform a `show ip arp` and see an incomplete entry. What does this mean?
options: An ARP request was sent but no reply was received within the timeout period
The entry has expired and will be refreshed on next use
The MAC address is statically configured but the IP is unknown
The entry is being replicated to a standby router
questions.explanation: An 'Incomplete' ARP entry means the router sent an ARP request for that IP but received no reply — the host may be down or unreachable.
hints: Incomplete = sent ARP, no MAC received back

questions.question: Which mechanism allows a single IPv4 address to represent an entire organization on the public internet?
questions.explanation: NAT translates private RFC 1918 addresses to a public IP. PAT (a form of NAT) multiplexes many private addresses to one public IP using port numbers.
hints: Think about how home routers work

questions.question: What is the primary difference between TCP and UDP regarding connection establishment?
options: TCP performs a 3-way handshake before data transfer; UDP sends data immediately without setup
TCP sends data immediately; UDP requires 3-way handshake
Both require handshake but UDP uses a 2-way handshake
Neither requires handshake in modern IP stacks
questions.explanation: TCP is connection-oriented — SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK before data. UDP is connectionless — no handshake, data is sent immediately at the cost of reliability.
hints: TCP = reliable + ordered; UDP = fast + unordered

questions.question: Which is the correct IPv4 private address range for the 172.x.x.x block?
questions.explanation: RFC 1918 defines 172.16.0.0/12 = 172.16.0.0 through 172.31.255.255. This is a /12 block (12 fixed bits).
hints: 172 private = /12 block, not /8 or /16

questions.question: A switch receives a frame with an unknown destination MAC. What action does it take?
options: Floods the frame out all ports except the one it arrived on
Drops the frame and sends an error to the source
Sends the frame to the default gateway
Forwards the frame only to the root bridge
questions.explanation: Unknown unicast flooding — when the destination MAC is not in the MAC address table, the switch floods the frame out all ports in the same VLAN except the ingress port.
hints: Unknown unicast = flood; known unicast = forward

questions.question: What is the result of performing a logical AND of 10.50.200.130 with the mask 255.255.255.192?
hints: AND each octet: 130 AND 192 = ?

questions.question: Which statement about CSMA/CD is true in a modern switched network?
options: CSMA/CD is effectively unused because full-duplex links eliminate collisions
CSMA/CD is used on all switch ports to detect collisions
CSMA/CD only applies to wireless networks
Modern switches use CSMA/CA instead of CSMA/CD
questions.explanation: Full-duplex Ethernet eliminates collisions entirely. CSMA/CD is defined in the standard but not exercised in modern switched, full-duplex environments.
hints: Full-duplex = separate TX/RX paths = no collisions

questions.question: A switch port configured with `switchport trunk native vlan 99` is connected to another switch with `switchport trunk native vlan 1`. What is the impact?
options: Native VLAN mismatch — frames tagged VLAN 1 on one side are treated as native (untagged) on the other, causing traffic to leak across VLANs
No impact; switches negotiate the native VLAN automatically via DTP
Traffic is load-balanced between the two native VLANs
The trunk is shut down by STP because of the mismatch
questions.explanation: Native VLAN mismatch means untagged frames sent by one switch are assigned to a different VLAN by the other. This can cause serious security issues (VLAN hopping). CDP warns about native VLAN mismatches.
hints: Native VLAN carries untagged traffic — mismatch = wrong VLAN assignment

questions.question: You have a trunk link and need VLAN 10 traffic to traverse it but VLAN 20 should be blocked. Which command achieves this?
questions.explanation: `switchport trunk allowed vlan 10` sets the allowed VLAN list to ONLY VLAN 10 on that trunk. `allowed vlan except` keeps existing list minus specified VLANs.
hints: 'allowed vlan X' replaces the list; 'allowed vlan add X' appends

questions.question: STP elects a root bridge. Which tiebreaker is used when two switches have the same bridge priority?
questions.explanation: Bridge ID = priority (32768 default) + MAC address. If priorities are equal, the switch with the LOWEST MAC address wins root bridge election.
hints: Lower Bridge ID wins; MAC is the tiebreaker

questions.question: A port in Rapid PVST+ goes through which states? (Select the correct sequence)
questions.explanation: RSTP (802.1w) consolidates states into Discarding, Learning, Forwarding. Traditional STP uses Blocking, Listening, Learning, Forwarding. Rapid PVST+ uses RSTP states.
hints: RSTP = 3 states: Discarding, Learning, Forwarding

questions.question: What distinguishes a Designated Port from a Root Port in STP?
options: Root Port is the best port toward the root bridge on a non-root switch; Designated Port is the best port on each network segment toward the root
Root Port is on the root bridge; Designated Port is on non-root switches
Both port types forward traffic; only the names differ by vendor
Designated Port is always in blocking state; Root Port forwards
questions.explanation: Every non-root switch has exactly one Root Port (best path toward root). Each segment has exactly one Designated Port (the port on the segment closest to root, which forwards).
hints: Root Port: toward root. Designated Port: on each segment, away from root.

questions.question: Which VTP mode allows a switch to create, modify, and delete VLANs but does NOT propagate VTP advertisements?
questions.explanation: VTP Transparent switches can create/modify/delete local VLANs but don't originate or accept VTP updates. They do forward VTP advertisements to other switches.
hints: Transparent = local VLANs only; forwards but ignores VTP ads

questions.question: An access port is configured with `switchport port-security maximum 2`. A third device connects. What happens by default?
options: The port is placed into err-disabled state (shutdown violation mode)
The third device's MAC is added, replacing the oldest entry
The port sends a trap but continues forwarding
The port drops traffic from the third device but stays up
questions.explanation: Default violation mode is shutdown — the port immediately goes to err-disabled. It must be manually re-enabled (or error-recovery configured) after a violation.
hints: Default violation mode = shutdown → err-disabled

questions.question: What is the purpose of BPDU Guard?
options: Immediately err-disables a PortFast-enabled port if a BPDU is received, preventing unauthorized switches from connecting
Blocks BPDUs from being sent on trunk ports to prevent topology changes
Prevents the root bridge from sending BPDUs to edge devices
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard protects edge (PortFast) ports. If any BPDU arrives on those ports (indicating a switch was connected), the port is err-disabled immediately.
hints: BPDU Guard = enforcement for access/PortFast ports

questions.question: You need to configure Layer 3 EtherChannel between two switches. Which interface must you configure the IP address on?
questions.explanation: For L3 EtherChannel, assign the IP to the Port-channel (logical) interface, not the physical member links. Members are added with `channel-group` commands.
hints: Port-channel is the logical bundle interface

questions.question: Which LACP mode combination results in an EtherChannel forming?
questions.explanation: LACP Active=sends LACP PDUs. Passive=responds only. Active-Active or Active-Passive form a channel. Passive-Passive = no channel (neither initiates). 'On' forces the channel without LACP negotiation and will NOT work with LACP modes.
hints: At least one side must be Active for LACP to negotiate

questions.question: A switch port is in the 'err-disabled' state. Which command re-enables it without reloading the switch?
questions.explanation: Enter the interface and issue `shutdown` followed by `no shutdown` to manually clear err-disabled. Alternatively configure `errdisable recovery cause` for automatic recovery.
hints: Manual reset: shutdown + no shutdown on the interface

questions.question: What does the `spanning-tree portfast` command do when applied to a switch port?
options: The port transitions directly from discarding to forwarding, skipping learning, without waiting for STP convergence
The port becomes the root port permanently
The port sends BPDUs at an accelerated rate
The port is excluded from STP entirely
questions.explanation: PortFast makes the port go directly to forwarding state (skipping listening/learning or discarding/learning), used for end-device ports to avoid 30-second STP delay.
hints: PortFast is for access ports connected to end devices, not switches

questions.question: A trunk port shows VLAN 30 as 'not allowed on trunk' in `show interfaces trunk`. What is the most likely cause?
options: VLAN 30 was removed from the allowed VLAN list with `switchport trunk allowed vlan remove 30`
VLAN 30 does not exist in the VLAN database
questions.explanation: 'Not allowed on trunk' means the VLAN was explicitly removed from the trunk's allowed VLAN list. If the VLAN simply didn't exist, it would show as 'not active.'
hints: 'Not allowed' vs 'not active' — these are distinct states in show interfaces trunk

questions.question: Which standard defines Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP)?
questions.explanation: 802.1w = RSTP. 802.1D = original STP. 802.1s = MSTP (Multiple Spanning Tree). 802.1Q = VLAN trunking.
hints: w = rapid, D = original, s = multiple

questions.question: What is the function of the VTP revision number?
options: Higher revision number = more recent database; switches update their VLAN database when they receive a VTP ad with a higher revision than their own
Lower revision number = more trusted; switches accept ads from switches with lower revision
Revision number tracks the number of VTP client switches
Revision number identifies the VTP domain name uniquely
questions.explanation: VTP uses revision numbers to determine if an update is newer. A switch with a higher revision overwrites lower-revision switches — a classic VTP gotcha when adding a misconfigured switch.
hints: High revision number = newest database; can wipe VLANs if you add a switch carelessly

questions.question: Which command verifies that an EtherChannel is operational and shows the member interfaces?
questions.explanation: `show etherchannel summary` displays the port-channel groups, protocol, and member port states with flags (P=in-bundle, D=down, etc.).
hints: Look for SU (L3 in use) or RU (L2) flags

questions.question: A switch has four switches in the network and must elect a root bridge for VLAN 10. Switch A has priority 24576, Switch B 32768, Switch C 32768, Switch D 4096. Which becomes root?
questions.explanation: Root bridge election: lowest Bridge ID wins. Bridge ID = priority + VLAN + MAC. Switch D has priority 4096 — the lowest among all four switches, so it wins regardless of MAC.
hints: Lowest priority wins; MAC only breaks ties among equal priorities

questions.question: What is a VLAN hopping attack, and which two techniques are used? (Select the best answer)
options: Attacker gains access to traffic on a different VLAN using switch spoofing or double tagging
Attacker overflows the MAC table to force unknown unicast flooding
Attacker injects rogue DHCP offers to intercept traffic
Attacker hijacks VTP server to delete VLANs
questions.explanation: VLAN hopping: (1) Switch spoofing — attacker negotiates a trunk link to receive all VLAN traffic. (2) Double tagging — attacker adds two 802.1Q headers; outer stripped by first switch, inner carries attacker to victim VLAN.
hints: Two methods: trunk negotiation and double-tagging

questions.question: Which STP port type does NOT forward traffic but receives BPDUs and can immediately transition to forwarding if the Designated or Root port fails?
questions.explanation: RSTP Alternate port: in discarding state, provides a backup path to the root. Immediately transitions to forwarding if the current Root Port fails (no 30-second wait).
hints: Alternate = upstream backup; Backup = same-segment backup

questions.question: Which PAgP mode combination will form an EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: PAgP: Desirable=actively negotiates, Auto=responds only. Desirable-Desirable or Desirable-Auto form channels. Auto-Auto = no channel. 'On' is not a PAgP mode — it's mode 'on' which bypasses negotiation.
hints: PAgP: at least one side must be Desirable

questions.question: When `show vlan brief` shows VLAN 50 as active but it does NOT appear in `show interfaces trunk` on the uplink, what is the problem?
options: VLAN 50 was removed from the trunk's allowed VLAN list
VLAN 50 is not in the VLAN database
The trunk is in access mode
VLAN 50 is the management VLAN and is always excluded from trunks
questions.explanation: If the VLAN exists (`show vlan brief` shows active) but doesn't appear on the trunk, it was removed from the trunk allowed list with `switchport trunk allowed vlan remove`.
hints: VLAN active but not on trunk = manually removed from allowed list

questions.question: Sticky MAC learning is enabled on a port. What happens to the learned MAC addresses when the switch reloads if the running config was NOT saved?
options: The sticky MACs are lost; the port relearns them after reload
The sticky MACs are permanent — they survive reloads without saving
The port enters err-disabled state at reload
The switch automatically saves sticky MACs to startup config every 5 minutes
questions.explanation: Sticky MACs appear in the running config. If you don't `write memory`, they are lost on reload. After reload the port relearns new sticky MACs.
hints: Always `wr mem` after sticky learning if you want persistence

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Spanning Tree BPDU Root Path Cost?
options: The cumulative cost of all links from a switch to the root bridge, used to elect Root Ports and Designated Ports
The cost of the local port only, used to determine whether to block or forward
The hop count from the root bridge to the current switch
The administrative cost configured manually to influence VLAN traffic
questions.explanation: Root Path Cost is the sum of port costs along the path from a switch to the root bridge. Switches compare root path costs to elect Root Ports (per switch) and Designated Ports (per segment).
hints: Cumulative cost, not just one hop

questions.question: You configure `switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q` but the command is rejected. What is the likely reason?
options: The switch only supports 802.1Q and the command is unnecessary; dot1q is the only option
The interface is already in access mode
The switch is in client mode and cannot change trunk encapsulation
questions.explanation: On modern Catalyst switches, ISL is not supported, so the only option is 802.1Q (dot1q). The `encapsulation` command is not needed or available on switches that only support dot1q.
hints: ISL is legacy Cisco; most modern switches are dot1q only

questions.question: Which command shows whether a port is currently operating as a trunk and which VLANs are forwarding?
questions.explanation: `show interfaces trunk` lists all trunk ports with their mode, native VLAN, allowed VLANs, and active/forwarding VLANs. `show vlan brief` shows VLAN-to-port membership for access ports.
hints: Trunk status = show interfaces trunk

questions.question: A DHCP server is on a different subnet than the clients. Which command on the router enables DHCP relay?
options: ip helper-address <server-ip> on the client-facing interface
service dhcp on the DHCP server interface
questions.explanation: `ip helper-address` on the client-facing (ingress) interface converts the client's broadcast DHCP Discover to a unicast directed to the DHCP server's IP.
hints: ip helper-address goes on the router interface closest to the clients

questions.question: Which HSRP state does a router enter when it is the active router sending keepalives?
questions.explanation: HSRP states: Initial → Learn → Listen → Speak → Standby → Active. The Active router owns the virtual IP/MAC and responds to ARP. Standby is next in line.
hints: Active router = currently forwarding traffic for the virtual IP

questions.question: What is the default HSRP hello timer and hold timer?
questions.explanation: Default HSRP timers: hello=3s, hold=10s. If the Standby router does not receive a hello from the Active within 10s, it assumes the Active has failed and transitions.
hints: Default: 3/10 — not the same as OSPF timers

questions.question: A NAT pool is exhausted (all public IPs allocated). What happens to new outbound connections from inside hosts?
options: New connections are dropped until a NAT translation times out
The router sends ICMP Source Quench to slow the traffic
questions.explanation: When a NAT pool is exhausted, new translation requests fail and packets are dropped. There is no automatic fallback to PAT unless explicitly configured with `overload`.
hints: PAT (overload) must be explicitly configured; it is not automatic failover

questions.question: Which NTP stratum level indicates a device that has lost contact with its reference clock and is no longer trustworthy?
questions.explanation: Stratum 16 means the NTP device is unsynchronized (cannot reach a time source). Stratum 0 is the atomic/GPS reference itself. Stratum 1 is directly connected to Stratum 0.
hints: 0=atomic, 1=directly synced, 16=unsynchronized

questions.question: An SNMP trap is sent from a managed device. Which port and protocol does it use?
questions.explanation: SNMP agents listen on UDP 161 (for Get/Set requests from NMS). SNMP traps are sent TO the NMS on UDP 162.
hints: 161=agent (receives requests), 162=NMS (receives traps)

questions.question: What is the key difference between SNMPv2c and SNMPv3?
options: SNMPv3 adds authentication and encryption; SNMPv2c uses community strings only
SNMPv2c uses MD5 authentication; SNMPv3 uses plain-text community strings
SNMPv3 removed trap support; SNMPv2c supports traps
They are functionally identical; SNMPv3 only changes OID numbering
questions.explanation: SNMPv3 adds user-based security model (USM) with authentication (MD5/SHA) and encryption (DES/AES). SNMPv2c relies on community strings sent in plaintext.
hints: v3 = security; v2c = plaintext community strings

questions.question: You configure `ip nat inside source list 10 interface gi0/1 overload`. What does `overload` add?
options: Enables PAT — multiple inside addresses share the interface's IP using different source ports
Allows more NAT translations than the default limit
Adds a backup NAT pool when the primary is full
Enables NAT for all IP protocols, not just TCP/UDP
questions.explanation: `overload` enables PAT (Port Address Translation), allowing many-to-one translation by differentiating sessions with unique source port numbers.
hints: overload = PAT = many hosts behind one public IP

questions.question: A router has `ip nat inside source static 192.168.1.10 203.0.113.50`. What is the effect?
options: Static one-to-one NAT: 192.168.1.10 always translates to 203.0.113.50 for both inbound and outbound
Dynamic NAT: 192.168.1.10 uses 203.0.113.50 only when the pool is available
PAT: all inside hosts share 203.0.113.50 with port translation
Static route: traffic for 203.0.113.50 is sent to 192.168.1.10
questions.explanation: Static NAT creates a permanent bidirectional one-to-one mapping. Traffic from 192.168.1.10 is translated to 203.0.113.50 outbound; traffic destined for 203.0.113.50 is translated inbound.
hints: Static NAT = permanent, bidirectional, one-to-one

questions.question: Which command shows currently active NAT translations on a router?
questions.explanation: `show ip nat translations` displays the NAT translation table with inside local/global and outside local/global addresses.
hints: Standard verification command for NAT

questions.question: What does the DHCP DORA process stand for, and in which order do the messages occur?
options: Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge — client broadcasts Discover, server Offers, client Requests, server Acknowledges
Discover, Order, Route, Assign — router assigns static IP based on MAC
Deliver, Offer, Register, Ack — server registers with DHCP relay before offering
Detect, Offer, Resolve, Assign — ARP is used to detect conflicts
questions.explanation: DORA: Discover (client broadcast) → Offer (server unicast/broadcast) → Request (client broadcast selects an offer) → ACK (server confirms the lease).
hints: All four messages: D=broadcast, O=server to client, R=broadcast, A=server to client

questions.question: GLBP differs from HSRP primarily because:
options: GLBP provides active-active load balancing across multiple gateways; HSRP only has one active forwarder at a time
GLBP is a Cisco proprietary protocol; HSRP is an open standard
GLBP only supports IPv6; HSRP supports both IPv4 and IPv6
GLBP uses a virtual MAC of 0000.0c07.ac__ like HSRP
questions.explanation: GLBP has one Active Virtual Gateway (AVG) that assigns different virtual MACs to multiple Active Virtual Forwarders (AVFs), achieving actual load balancing. HSRP has only one active gateway.
hints: GLBP = load balancing; HSRP = hot standby (one active at a time)

questions.question: An administrator configures `ntp authenticate` and `ntp trusted-key 1`. A peer without authentication attempts to synchronize. What happens?
options: The router rejects unauthenticated NTP updates
The router accepts the update but logs a warning
The router uses the unauthenticated peer as a backup only
The router promotes the unauthenticated peer to stratum 1
questions.explanation: With NTP authentication enabled, the router only synchronizes to peers that supply the correct authentication key. Unauthenticated peers are rejected.
hints: ntp authenticate + trusted-key = only authenticated sources accepted

questions.question: Which HSRP version introduced support for IPv6 and millisecond timers?
questions.explanation: HSRPv2 introduced IPv6 support, millisecond hello/hold timers, and expanded group numbers (0–4095 vs 0–255 in v1). HSRPv1 and v2 are not compatible and cannot interoperate.
hints: HSRPv2 = IPv6 + ms timers + more group numbers

questions.question: What is the syslog severity level for 'Error' conditions?
questions.explanation: Syslog levels: 0=Emergency, 1=Alert, 2=Critical, 3=Error, 4=Warning, 5=Notice, 6=Informational, 7=Debug. A good mnemonic: Every Awful Crisis Endangers Whole Network Infrastructure Daily.
hints: Memorize: 0=Emerg, 1=Alert, 2=Crit, 3=Error, 4=Warn

questions.question: Which command configures a router to send syslog messages to an external server at 10.1.1.100 at severity Warning and above?
options: logging host 10.1.1.100 then logging trap warnings
questions.explanation: `logging host <ip>` sets the destination. `logging trap <level>` sets the minimum severity level. `warnings` = level 4; messages at 0–4 are sent.
hints: Two commands: logging host + logging trap

questions.question: DNS uses which transport protocol for standard queries and which for zone transfers?
options: UDP port 53 for queries; TCP port 53 for zone transfers
TCP port 53 for queries; UDP port 53 for zone transfers
questions.explanation: DNS standard queries use UDP port 53 (small payload, fast). Zone transfers (AXFR) between DNS servers use TCP port 53 because they transfer large amounts of data reliably.
hints: Queries=UDP 53; Zone transfers=TCP 53

questions.question: A router configured as a DHCP server shows a conflict in `show ip dhcp conflict`. How did this conflict arise?
options: The DHCP server sent a Ping Probe before offering an address and received a reply, meaning the address is already in use
Two DHCP servers offered the same address simultaneously
The client's ARP probe failed to resolve
The lease time expired before the client renewed
questions.explanation: Before offering an address, a Cisco DHCP server pings the address. If it receives a reply, it marks it as a conflict and removes it from the pool. Conflicts can also be detected via client ARP probes.
hints: DHCP conflict = IP already in use detected by ping probe

questions.question: Which VRRP characteristic distinguishes it from HSRP?
options: VRRP is an open standard (RFC 5798); HSRP is Cisco proprietary
VRRP supports only one active router; HSRP supports multiple active routers
VRRP requires manual preemption configuration; HSRP preempts by default
questions.explanation: VRRP is defined in RFC 5798 and is vendor-neutral. HSRP is Cisco proprietary. VRRP uses 224.0.0.18; HSRP v1 uses 224.0.0.2, v2 uses 224.0.0.102. VRRP preempts by default; HSRP does not.
hints: VRRP = open standard; HSRP = Cisco proprietary

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `ip dhcp excluded-address` command?
options: Prevents the DHCP server from assigning those addresses to clients, reserving them for static assignment
Blocks DHCP traffic from those addresses entering the router
Excludes those addresses from NAT translation
Prevents ARP entries for those addresses from aging out
questions.explanation: `ip dhcp excluded-address` tells the server not to hand out those IPs from the pool. Used to reserve addresses for routers, servers, and other statically configured devices.
hints: Always exclude static IPs before defining the DHCP pool

questions.question: A standard ACL numbered 15 is applied inbound on Gi0/0 with `access-list 15 permit 10.1.0.0 0.0.255.255`. A packet arrives from 10.2.5.1. What happens?
options: The packet is dropped because it hits the implicit deny all at the end
The packet is forwarded because no explicit deny exists
The packet is forwarded because standard ACLs only match destination IP
The router sends ICMP Destination Unreachable to the source
questions.explanation: Every ACL has an implicit `deny any` at the end. 10.2.5.1 does not match 10.1.0.0/16, so it falls through to the implicit deny and is dropped.
hints: Implicit deny all = if nothing matches, drop

questions.question: Where should an extended ACL be placed to minimize wasted bandwidth?
options: As close to the source as possible
As close to the destination as possible
On the exit interface toward the internet
questions.explanation: Extended ACLs filter on source AND destination, so placing them near the source drops unwanted traffic early, before it wastes bandwidth crossing the network.
hints: Extended ACL near source; Standard ACL near destination

questions.question: An extended ACL entry is: `permit tcp 10.1.0.0 0.0.0.255 any eq 443`. What traffic does this permit?
options: TCP traffic from 10.1.0.0/24 destined to any IP on port 443 (HTTPS)
TCP traffic from any source to 10.1.0.0/24 on port 443
All IP traffic from 10.1.0.0/24 on port 443
UDP traffic from 10.1.0.0/24 to any on port 443
