options: 802.11a uses 5 GHz (up to 54 Mbps, more channels, less congested); 802.11b uses 2.4 GHz (up to 11 Mbps, fewer non-overlapping channels, more congestion)
802.11b uses 5 GHz and has 23 non-overlapping channels
They use the same frequency but different modulation
questions.explanation: 802.11a: 5 GHz, up to 54 Mbps, 23 non-overlapping 20 MHz channels (US). 802.11b: 2.4 GHz, up to 11 Mbps, only 3 non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11 in US).
hints: a=5GHz/54Mbps; b=2.4GHz/11Mbps; both released 1999

questions.question: What problem does Spanning Tree solve?
options: Prevents Layer 2 broadcast storms and MAC table instability caused by physical loops in switched networks
Prevents IP address conflicts on VLANs
Prevents duplicate frames from reaching hosts in redundant topologies
questions.explanation: Without STP, physical loops in a switched network would cause broadcast storms (broadcasts loop forever), MAC table instability (frames arrive from multiple ports), and duplicate unicast frames, crashing the network.
hints: STP prevents L2 loops → no broadcast storms → no MAC table thrashing

questions.question: A switch with default STP settings has a 1 Gbps uplink to the root bridge. What is the STP root path cost?
questions.explanation: Default (short) STP port cost for 1 Gbps = 4. Root path cost = sum of port costs along the path to the root. Single 1 Gbps link = cost 4.
hints: 1Gbps short cost=4; 100Mbps=19; 10Mbps=100

questions.question: What wireless roaming event occurs when a client moves between APs in the same SSID but managed by a WLC?
options: Intra-controller roaming — the WLC handles the handoff internally with no traffic interruption
Inter-controller roaming — requires mobility tunnel between WLCs
Both types are the same from the client's perspective
Roaming requires the client to re-associate and re-authenticate completely
questions.explanation: Intra-controller roaming: client moves between two APs managed by the same WLC. The WLC updates the client association internally. Client keeps its IP address — minimal disruption.
hints: Intra-controller = same WLC handles both APs = seamless handoff

questions.question: Which technology allows multiple physical switches to operate as a single logical switch, eliminating STP entirely between the members?
questions.explanation: Cisco StackWise (for Catalyst 9000), VSS (Catalyst 6000), and similar technologies logically merge switches into one. Since there's only one control plane, STP is not needed between stack members.
hints: StackWise/VSS = one logical switch = no STP between members

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 802.11 BSS (Basic Service Set) and what identifies it uniquely?
options: A BSS is a wireless network consisting of one AP and its associated clients; identified by the BSSID (AP's radio MAC address)
BSS identifies the SSID used by the network
BSS is the broadcast domain of the wireless network
BSS is defined by the IP subnet allocated to the wireless LAN
questions.explanation: BSS: single AP + associated clients. BSSID = the AP's radio MAC address (unique identifier). ESS (Extended Service Set) = multiple APs with the same SSID for roaming.
hints: BSS=one AP; BSSID=AP MAC; ESS=multiple APs same SSID for roaming

questions.question: Which 802.11 frame type is used for Beacon, Probe Request/Response, Association, and Authentication?
questions.explanation: 802.11 frame types: Management (beacons, probes, auth, association/reassociation), Control (RTS, CTS, ACK — medium access), Data (actual user data). Action frames are a subtype of Management.
hints: Management: beacons, probes, auth, assoc; Control: RTS/CTS/ACK; Data: payload

questions.question: A WLC shows an AP in 'Discovery' state. What does this mean?
options: The AP has joined the WLC control plane but is discovering its configuration — normal transition state before 'Registered'
The AP is in an error state and needs reboot
Discovery means the AP is operating in local switching mode
The AP is in standalone (autonomous) mode
questions.explanation: CAPWAP join process: AP boots → sends Discovery Request (broadcast/multicast/prior WLC) → receives Discovery Response → sends Join Request → Join Response → AP is Registered and downloads config.
hints: CAPWAP states: Discover → Join → Image Check → Config → Registered

questions.question: What is the purpose of WPA Enterprise vs WPA Personal?
options: WPA Enterprise uses 802.1X with a RADIUS server for per-user authentication; WPA Personal uses a shared passphrase (PSK) for all users
WPA Enterprise encrypts the passphrase; WPA Personal uses plaintext
WPA Enterprise is for home networks; WPA Personal for offices
They are identical except Enterprise supports AES and Personal uses TKIP
questions.explanation: WPA Personal (PSK): single shared passphrase, appropriate for homes/small offices. WPA Enterprise: each user authenticates with unique credentials via 802.1X/EAP to a RADIUS server — required for enterprise security.
hints: Enterprise=802.1X+RADIUS per-user auth; Personal=shared PSK

questions.question: Which 802.11 modulation technique used in 802.11n and 802.11ac allows higher data rates by sending multiple streams simultaneously?
questions.explanation: MIMO uses multiple antennas and spatial streams for parallel data transmission. 802.11n introduced 2×2 MIMO; 802.11ac uses up to 8×8 MU-MIMO. Significantly increases throughput.
hints: MIMO = multiple antennas = multiple parallel streams = higher throughput

questions.question: What command on a Cisco WLC would you use to verify which APs are currently associated and their status?
questions.explanation: `show ap summary` on a Cisco WLC displays all associated APs with name, IP, model, state (Registered), and uptime.
hints: WLC: show ap summary = all APs and their status

questions.question: In a FlexConnect deployment, what is the local switching mode?
options: Traffic is switched locally at the AP without traversing the WLC tunnel — reduces WAN bandwidth for remote sites
All traffic tunnels to the WLC for centralized policy enforcement
FlexConnect forces all traffic to the internet directly
Local switching requires a local WLC at each site
questions.explanation: FlexConnect (formerly HREAP): lightweight APs at remote sites can switch traffic locally (without sending it back to the central WLC), reducing WAN traffic. Management traffic still goes to WLC.
hints: FlexConnect local switching = data plane local; control still via WLC over WAN

questions.question: What is the Wi-Fi frequency band advantage of 5 GHz over 2.4 GHz?
options: More non-overlapping channels and less interference; shorter range trade-off is acceptable in dense environments
5 GHz penetrates walls better than 2.4 GHz
5 GHz is backward compatible with 802.11b devices
5 GHz has lower data rates but more reliable connections
questions.explanation: 5 GHz: 23+ non-overlapping channels, less crowded, supports higher data rates but shorter range due to higher frequency attenuation. 2.4 GHz: only 3 non-overlapping channels (1,6,11), longer range, more interference.
hints: 5GHz: more channels, higher rate, shorter range; 2.4GHz: fewer channels, longer range

questions.question: What does the `switchport port-security mac-address sticky` command do when frames arrive?
options: Dynamically learns MACs and adds them to the running config as static secure addresses — persists across reloads if saved
Adds the MAC address to the MAC address table only, with no security enforcement
Requires all MACs to be pre-configured before the command takes effect
Sticky only works with violation mode restrict
questions.explanation: Sticky MAC: as frames arrive, their source MACs are dynamically learned AND added to the running config as static port-security entries. Save running config to retain them across reboots.
hints: Sticky = dynamic learning + static config entry; save running config to persist

questions.question: A trunk port is configured `switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30`. VLANs 10 and 30 are in `show vlan brief` as active, but VLAN 20 is not in the database. What happens to VLAN 20 traffic on this trunk?
options: VLAN 20 is in the allowed list but not active — its traffic is NOT forwarded because the VLAN doesn't exist in the database
VLAN 20 frames are forwarded transparently as the trunk doesn't check the VLAN database
VLAN 20 is automatically created when added to the trunk allowed list
The trunk goes down because of the missing VLAN
questions.explanation: A VLAN must be: 1) in the allowed list AND 2) active in the VLAN database AND 3) not blocked by STP to appear in the 'active VLANs in spanning tree forwarding state' section of show interfaces trunk.
hints: VLAN forwarding: allowed + active + STP forwarding = all three required

questions.question: What is the OSPF Hello interval default on a broadcast (Ethernet) network?
questions.explanation: OSPF Hello intervals: Broadcast and point-to-point networks = 10 seconds (dead = 40s). NBMA networks = 30 seconds (dead = 120s).
hints: OSPF Hello: broadcast=10s, NBMA=30s; dead = 4× hello

questions.question: What is the purpose of the OSPF Type 2 (Network) LSA?
options: Generated by the DR on a multi-access segment, lists all routers attached to that segment, and carries the subnet mask
Generated by ABRs to summarize routes between areas
Describes external routes redistributed into OSPF
Generated by each router to describe its own links
questions.explanation: Type 2 Network LSA: generated by the Designated Router (DR) on broadcast/NBMA segments. Lists all routers on that segment and includes the subnet mask. Flooded within the area only.
hints: Type 2 = DR generates; describes the multi-access network segment

questions.question: Which EIGRP table is checked FIRST when the routing table is consulted for a destination?
options: Routing table (from the topology table's successors)
questions.explanation: EIGRP topology table stores all routes (successors and feasible successors). The routing table only contains the best (successor) routes. When forwarding, the routing table is consulted (which got its entries from the topology table's successors).
hints: EIGRP lookup order: routing table (successor) → topology table (if route goes active)

questions.question: A static route is configured: `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 null0`. What is the purpose of routing to Null0?
options: Creates a summary/aggregate route that drops traffic matching sub-routes if no more specific route exists — prevents routing loops and black-hole routing
Null0 sends ICMP Unreachable to senders when no path exists
Null0 is used for testing only
Routes to Null0 always have AD 255 and are never used
questions.explanation: Routing to Null0 (bit bucket): used with route summarization to prevent sub-optimal routing. If a more specific route exists, it matches; if not, the Null0 route drops the packet cleanly instead of looping.
hints: Null0 route = discard; used with summaries to prevent sub-summary leakage

questions.question: What is the difference between `show ip route` and `show ip route summary`?
options: `show ip route` displays the full routing table; `show ip route summary` shows only counts by protocol (how many routes of each type)
summary shows routes with AD and metric only
questions.explanation: `show ip route summary` is a compact view showing the number of routes per source (static, connected, OSPF, EIGRP, etc.) and total route count. Useful for capacity planning.
hints: show ip route summary = counts by protocol; not the actual routes

questions.question: What is OSPFv3's method for supporting both IPv4 and IPv6?
options: Address families — OSPFv3 AF allows running one OSPFv3 process with separate IPv4 and IPv6 address families
OSPFv3 only supports IPv6; OSPFv2 handles IPv4
OSPFv3 automatically dual-stacks both IPv4 and IPv6
OSPFv3 requires two separate processes for IPv4 and IPv6
questions.explanation: Modern OSPFv3 with Address Families (RFC 5838) allows a single OSPFv3 process to carry both IPv4 and IPv6 routing information. Previously, OSPFv3 was IPv6-only.
hints: OSPFv3 AF = one process handles both IPv4 and IPv6 families

questions.question: Which command enables OSPF on specific interfaces by matching their IP subnets?
options: ip ospf <process> area <id> on each interface
questions.explanation: Under `router ospf <process-id>`, the `network <subnet> <wildcard> area <id>` command matches interfaces whose IP falls in the range. Alternatively, `ip ospf <process> area <id>` directly on each interface.
hints: network command under router ospf = match interfaces by IP; or use ip ospf per-interface

questions.question: What does the `debug ip routing` command display?
options: Shows real-time routing table changes — route additions, deletions, and modifications
Shows all routing protocol packets received
Displays CPU usage for routing computations
questions.explanation: `debug ip routing` outputs messages whenever routes are installed or removed from the routing table. Useful for diagnosing intermittent route flapping. Use carefully — high output volume.
hints: debug ip routing = routing table changes (install/remove); high volume in busy networks

questions.question: Which BGP attribute is used as the primary path selection criterion for routes advertised to eBGP peers?
options: AS Path (shortest AS Path length wins)
questions.explanation: BGP path selection order (simplified): Weight > Local Preference > AS Path > Origin > MED > eBGP over iBGP > IGP metric. AS Path is the primary globally-significant attribute — shorter is preferred.
hints: BGP: Weight > Local Pref (iBGP) > AS Path > Origin > MED

questions.question: What is the purpose of a loopback interface in OSPF and BGP configuration?
options: Provides a stable, always-up Router ID and peering address — loopbacks never go down when physical links fail
Loopbacks are only used for testing and have no production purpose
Loopbacks are required for NAT operation
questions.explanation: Loopback interfaces are software-only and never go down (unless explicitly shut down). Used as OSPF/BGP Router IDs and BGP peer addresses for stability. Physical interface flaps don't affect loopback-based peering.
hints: Loopback = stable RID + BGP peer address; never goes down

questions.question: What does 'OSPF convergence' mean and what factors affect its speed?
options: All routers reach a consistent, loop-free view of the topology; affected by SPF timer (delay + hold), LSA flood propagation time, and dead interval detection
Convergence means the OSPF process restarts
Convergence speed depends only on interface bandwidth
OSPF convergence is always exactly 40 seconds
questions.explanation: OSPF convergence: all routers have the same LSDB and computed routing table. Factors: dead interval (failure detection = 40s default), LSA flood time, SPF delay/hold timers (incrementally tunable).
hints: Convergence time ≈ dead interval + LSA flood + SPF computation

questions.question: Which EIGRP packet type does NOT require an acknowledgment?
questions.explanation: EIGRP packet types: Hello and Ack are unreliable (no ack required). Update, Query, Reply, and Request use RTP (Reliable Transport Protocol) — they require acknowledgment.
hints: Hello + ACK = unreliable (no ack); Update/Query/Reply = reliable (ack required)

questions.question: What is the HSRP multicast address used by HSRPv1 for hello messages?
questions.explanation: HSRPv1 hellos sent to 224.0.0.2 (all routers multicast). HSRPv2 uses 224.0.0.102. VRRP uses 224.0.0.18. OSPF uses 224.0.0.5/6.

questions.question: Which DHCP message does a client use to decline an offered IP address?
questions.explanation: DHCP Decline: client found the offered IP already in use (via ARP probe) and rejects it. The server marks it as in-conflict. DHCP Release: client voluntarily releases its lease.
hints: Decline = address in use; Release = voluntarily giving up the lease

questions.question: An ACL is applied outbound on a router's exit interface. The router receives a packet destined for a host on that interface's subnet. The ACL denies the packet. Where is the packet dropped?
options: On the router's outbound interface (before the packet leaves the router)
On the switch connected to that interface
The packet is dropped at the ingress interface
questions.explanation: Outbound ACL: applied after routing decision, just before the packet exits the interface. The packet is dropped at the router's outbound interface, not forwarded to the connected network.
hints: Outbound ACL = applied after routing decision, at the exit interface

questions.question: What is the difference between DNS A records and AAAA records?
options: A records map hostnames to IPv4 addresses; AAAA records map hostnames to IPv6 addresses
A records are for internal use; AAAA for internet-facing hosts
A records support multiple IPs; AAAA only supports one
AAAA records are deprecated in IPv6
questions.explanation: DNS record types: A (Address) = hostname → 32-bit IPv4 address. AAAA = hostname → 128-bit IPv6 address (four A's = 4×32 bits). PTR = reverse lookup. MX = mail server.
hints: A=IPv4, AAAA=IPv6, PTR=reverse lookup, MX=mail

questions.question: When configuring a named ACL on IOS, which subcommand is used to remove a specific entry?
questions.explanation: In named ACL configuration mode: `no <sequence-number>` removes that specific entry. E.g., `no 20` removes entry 20. This is a key advantage of named ACLs — individual line deletion.
hints: Named ACL: no <seq#> deletes that entry; numbered ACLs can't do this

questions.question: What is the purpose of `ip helper-address` when multiple DHCP servers exist?
options: The ip helper-address forwards DHCP Discover broadcasts to one specified server IP — configure multiple ip helper-address statements to reach multiple servers
ip helper-address load-balances across multiple DHCP servers automatically
Only one DHCP server is supported per ip helper-address statement
ip helper-address sends to the broadcast 255.255.255.255 on the server side
questions.explanation: Multiple `ip helper-address` commands can be stacked on one interface. Each Discover is sent to all specified servers. Clients accept the first Offer received.
hints: Multiple ip helper-address = send to multiple DHCP servers; client takes first offer

questions.question: What is the purpose of the GLBP weighting mechanism?
options: Controls how traffic is distributed across AVF (Active Virtual Forwarder) routers — higher weight = more traffic routed through that gateway
Determines which router becomes the AVG (Active Virtual Gateway)
Prevents a router from becoming an AVF when its uplink is down
GLBP weighting configures the GLBP group priority
questions.explanation: GLBP weighting: each AVF can be assigned a weight (1-254). The AVG uses weights to determine the proportion of ARP replies that point to each AVF, controlling load distribution.
hints: GLBP weight = proportion of traffic through each AVF; higher = more traffic

questions.question: Which ACL entry matches all IP traffic and is equivalent to 'permit any any'?
questions.explanation: `permit ip any any` matches all IP traffic. The `ip` keyword matches any IP protocol. `any` is shorthand for `0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255`. This overrides the implicit deny.
hints: permit ip any any = allow everything (must be last explicit rule if used)

questions.question: A client receives a DHCP lease of 172.16.1.50/24 with a gateway of 172.16.1.1 but cannot reach 172.16.2.0/24. What is the likely problem?
options: The gateway (router) does not have a route to 172.16.2.0/24 or is not routing between subnets
The DHCP lease is for the wrong subnet
The client's subnet mask is incorrect
The client's ARP table is stale
questions.explanation: The client can reach the gateway (same subnet). If it cannot reach 172.16.2.0/24, the router is missing a route, has an ACL blocking it, or doesn't have ip routing enabled.
hints: Client → gateway OK; gateway → remote subnet fails = routing/ACL issue on router

questions.question: What is the function of the `ip nat translation max-entries` command?
options: Limits the maximum number of concurrent NAT translation entries to prevent resource exhaustion
Sets the maximum NAT timeout value
Limits the NAT pool to a maximum number of IP addresses
Restricts the number of inside hosts that can use NAT
questions.explanation: `ip nat translation max-entries` (global or per-host) prevents a single host or the overall system from creating excessive NAT entries, protecting router memory and CPU.
hints: max-entries = NAT table size limit; prevents resource exhaustion

questions.question: What does `show ip dhcp pool` display?
options: Pool name, subnet, total addresses, available addresses, and number of current leases
All current DHCP bindings with MAC addresses
The DHCP server's IP and lease time configured
questions.explanation: `show ip dhcp pool` shows each pool's configuration (name, subnet, range) and utilization statistics (current bindings, total IPs, available IPs). Useful for capacity monitoring.
hints: dhcp pool = pool config + utilization; dhcp binding = per-lease details

questions.question: Which technology allows ISPs to conserve IPv4 addresses by using a single public IP for many customers, with NAT performed at the ISP level?
questions.explanation: CGN (Carrier-Grade NAT / RFC 6888): ISPs deploy NAT on their infrastructure, allowing many customers to share one public IP. RFC 6598 defines 100.64.0.0/10 as the shared address space for CGN use.
hints: CGN = NAT at ISP scale; RFC 6598 = 100.64.0.0/10 shared address space

questions.question: What is the effect of applying the same ACL in both the inbound and outbound directions on the same interface?
options: Traffic is filtered twice — once entering the interface and once leaving; may unexpectedly block all or most traffic
The ACL is only applied once; IOS ignores duplicate applications
The inbound direction takes priority over outbound
ACLs cannot be applied bidirectionally on the same interface
questions.explanation: Applying the same ACL both in and out on an interface means all traffic is inspected against it twice. This can cause unexpected double-filtering. Best practice: apply ACLs in only one direction per interface.
hints: In + Out = double filtering; same ACL in both directions = may block valid return traffic

questions.question: Which SNMP message type is similar to a Trap but requires an acknowledgment from the NMS?
questions.explanation: SNMP Inform (SNMPv2c/v3): agent sends a notification to NMS and waits for a Response acknowledgment. If no ack, the agent retransmits. Traps are fire-and-forget (no ack).
hints: Inform = Trap with acknowledgment; retransmitted if NMS doesn't ack

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `ip nat pool` command?
options: Defines a pool of public IP addresses used for dynamic NAT translations
Sets the timeout for NAT translations
Enables PAT overload for the NAT pool
questions.explanation: `ip nat pool <name> <start-ip> <end-ip> netmask <mask>` (or prefix-length) defines the range of public IPs available for dynamic NAT. Referenced in `ip nat inside source list <acl> pool <name>`.
hints: ip nat pool = define public IP range for dynamic NAT

questions.question: An extended ACL is designed to permit only established TCP sessions. Which keyword achieves this?
questions.explanation: `established` matches TCP packets with ACK or RST bits set — packets belonging to already-established sessions. New inbound SYN packets don't have ACK set and are therefore denied.
hints: TCP established = ACK or RST bit set; blocks new inbound SYN, allows return traffic

questions.question: Which command configures SSH version 2 on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `ip ssh version 2` forces SSHv2 only. Combined with `ip ssh rsa keypair-name` or `crypto key generate rsa`, this secures the management interface against SSHv1 vulnerabilities.
hints: ip ssh version 2 = force SSHv2 only; SSHv1 is vulnerable

questions.question: What is the purpose of a DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network security?
options: An intermediate network segment hosting public-facing servers (web, email, DNS) that is separated from both the internet and the internal corporate network by firewalls
A network segment with no security controls
An encrypted WAN link between two offices
A VLAN reserved for management traffic only
questions.explanation: DMZ: servers that must be accessible from the internet (web servers, mail relays) are placed in a DMZ. The firewall allows internet → DMZ traffic but restricts DMZ → internal network traffic.
hints: DMZ = semi-trusted zone; internet can reach it; internal network is protected from it

questions.question: Which attack exploits the lack of authentication in ARP by poisoning the ARP cache of victims with false MAC-to-IP mappings?
questions.explanation: ARP has no authentication. An attacker sends gratuitous ARP replies claiming its MAC is associated with the gateway's IP. Victims update their ARP cache and send traffic to the attacker (MITM).
hints: ARP poisoning = fake ARP replies = MITM; mitigated by Dynamic ARP Inspection

questions.question: What is the primary function of a SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) system?
options: Aggregates and correlates log data from multiple sources to detect security incidents and provide centralized alerting and reporting
questions.explanation: SIEM collects logs from firewalls, IDS/IPS, routers, switches, servers, and endpoints. It correlates events across sources, detecting complex multi-stage attacks that individual systems might miss.
hints: SIEM = centralized log aggregation + correlation + alerting

questions.question: Which access model grants full access by default and requires explicit deny entries for restrictions?
questions.explanation: Blacklist/default-permit model: everything is allowed unless explicitly blocked. Whitelist/default-deny model: everything is blocked unless explicitly permitted (more secure). ACL implicit deny = whitelist approach.
hints: Whitelist = default deny (more secure); Blacklist = default permit (easier but riskier)

questions.question: What is the purpose of HTTPS certificate pinning?
options: The application only accepts a specific certificate or CA, rejecting even valid certificates from other CAs — prevents MITM attacks using rogue trusted certificates
Certificate pinning encrypts the HTTPS session with AES-256
Certificate pinning is required for FIPS compliance
questions.explanation: Certificate pinning: the app hardcodes the expected certificate or public key. Even if an attacker has a certificate signed by a trusted CA, the app rejects it if it doesn't match the pinned value.
hints: Pinning = app only accepts specific cert/key; defeats rogue CA MITM attacks

questions.question: Which Python data structure is most similar to a JSON object?
questions.explanation: JSON objects are key-value pairs (e.g., {"name": "R1", "ip": "10.1.1.1"}). Python dict is the equivalent: `{'name': 'R1', 'ip': '10.1.1.1'}`. JSON arrays map to Python lists.
hints: JSON object = Python dict; JSON array = Python list

questions.question: What is the purpose of Cisco's SWIM (Software Image Management) feature in DNA Center?
options: Automates IOS image distribution and upgrade across the network from a central repository, with pre-checks and rollback support
SWIM monitors network traffic for anomalies
SWIM provides wireless controller image updates only
questions.explanation: SWIM (DNA Center feature): centralized repository for IOS/IOS XE images. Automates checking compatibility, distributing images, scheduling activations, and rolling back if needed.
hints: SWIM = centralized IOS image management + automated upgrades via DNA Center

questions.question: In NETCONF, what is the purpose of the `<candidate>` datastore?
options: A staging area where configuration changes can be prepared and validated before being committed to the running config
The current active configuration of the device
A backup copy of the startup configuration
questions.explanation: NETCONF datastores: running (active config), candidate (staging area — edit here, then commit to running), startup (saved config). Not all devices support candidate; it enables atomic transactions.
hints: candidate = staging; commit applies candidate to running (atomic change)

questions.question: What does the `json.loads()` function do in Python?
options: Parses a JSON string and returns a Python object (dict/list)
Converts a Python object to a JSON string
Loads a JSON file from disk
questions.explanation: `json.loads(string)` = parse JSON string → Python object. `json.dumps(object)` = Python object → JSON string. `json.load(file)` = read from file object. `json.dump(obj, file)` = write to file.
hints: loads = string to Python; dumps = Python to string; load/dump = file I/O

questions.question: Which SD-WAN feature allows traffic to be sent over the best available WAN path based on real-time link quality measurements?
questions.explanation: AAR continuously measures WAN path quality (latency, jitter, loss) and automatically routes application traffic over paths meeting the SLA requirements defined in policy.
hints: AAR = application routing based on real-time WAN quality metrics

questions.question: What is the difference between Cisco IOS CLI scripting (TCL) and Ansible for network automation?
options: Ansible is agentless, vendor-neutral, uses YAML playbooks, and scales to thousands of devices; TCL is a built-in IOS scripting language for single-device local automation
TCL is more powerful and can manage multiple devices simultaneously
Ansible requires Cisco IOS-specific modules only
questions.explanation: TCL: built into IOS, good for single-device automation (local scripting). Ansible: external, agentless, multi-vendor, designed for fleet-wide automation with idempotent playbooks.
hints: TCL = local IOS scripting; Ansible = multi-device, multi-vendor, external

questions.question: What is the network address of 10.25.128.0/18?
hints: Block size for /18 = 64; 128 is 2×64 → network boundary

questions.question: How many host bits does a /29 mask leave for host addresses?
questions.explanation: /29 = 32-29=3 host bits. 2^3-2=6 usable hosts per subnet.
hints: Host bits = 32 minus prefix; /29 → 3 host bits

questions.question: Which IPv4 address is in the same /24 subnet as 192.168.5.1?
questions.explanation: 192.168.5.254 is in 192.168.5.0/24 (same third octet = 5). 192.168.5.0 is the network address, not a valid host.
hints: Same /24 = same first three octets

questions.question: Which transport layer feature prevents out-of-order TCP segments from being delivered to the application out-of-sequence?
questions.explanation: TCP sequence numbers allow the receiver to buffer out-of-order segments and deliver them to the application in the correct order once gaps are filled.
hints: TCP resequencing: buffer + sequence numbers → in-order delivery to app

questions.question: What is the binary value of the decimal number 172?
What is the last usable host address in the subnet 172.31.64.0/18?
hints: Broadcast = next block - 1 = 172.31.128.0 - 1 = 172.31.127.255; last host = broadcast-1

questions.question: A network diagram shows two routers connected via an Ethernet link. Which protocol would you use to detect the Layer 2 neighbor and its interface details without manual configuration?
questions.explanation: CDP (Cisco) or LLDP (IEEE 802.1AB) automatically discovers directly connected neighbors and exchanges device capability, interface, and IP information at Layer 2.
hints: CDP/LLDP = automatic neighbor discovery; no routing protocol needed

questions.question: What does the subnet mask 255.255.255.252 (/30) restrict a subnet to?
options: 2 usable host addresses (ideal for point-to-point links)
questions.explanation: /30 = 2 host bits. 2^2-2=2 usable. Perfect for point-to-point links between two routers.
hints: /30 = 2 usable hosts; standard for WAN point-to-point links

questions.question: Which subnets are created when 192.168.10.0/24 is subnetted into /26 subnets?
questions.explanation: /26 block size=64. From /24: .0, .64, .128, .192 — four subnets.
hints: Block size /26 = 64; four subnets starting at 0, 64, 128, 192

questions.question: Which Ethernet standard supports 10 Gbps over multimode fiber at distances up to 300 meters?
questions.explanation: 10GBASE-SR: 850nm laser, OM3/OM4 multimode fiber, up to 300m. LR=10km single-mode. ER=40km. T=copper Cat6A up to 100m.
hints: SR=short reach (multimode, 300m); LR=long reach (single-mode, 10km)

questions.question: What type of transmission sends data to ALL nodes in a network simultaneously?
questions.explanation: Broadcast: sent to all hosts in a domain (L2 broadcast to FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF or L3 to 255.255.255.255). Multicast: sent to a group. Anycast: to the nearest one of a group.
hints: Broadcast=all; Multicast=group; Unicast=one; Anycast=nearest of group

questions.question: What is the first valid host address in the subnet 10.1.0.0/16?
questions.explanation: 10.1.0.0 is the network address. First valid host = 10.1.0.1.
hints: First host = network + 1; 10.1.0.0 is network address

questions.question: What is the purpose of the IPv4 header checksum field?
options: Verifies the integrity of the IP header only (not the payload); recalculated at each hop as TTL changes
Verifies both header and payload integrity
Provides authentication of the packet source
Acts as a sequence number for fragmented packets
questions.explanation: IPv4 header checksum covers only the header fields. Since TTL changes at every router, the checksum must be recalculated at each hop. IPv6 removed the header checksum (upper layers handle integrity).
hints: IP checksum = header-only; recalculated each hop; IPv6 removed it

questions.question: Which TCP flag combination is used to reset an established connection immediately?
questions.explanation: TCP RST: abruptly terminates a connection. No data drain. Used for error conditions or closing connections to closed ports. FIN+ACK is used in the graceful four-way teardown.
hints: RST = abrupt reset; FIN = graceful close

questions.question: A host sends data at 1 Gbps. After passing through a router to a 100 Mbps WAN link, what happens to excess traffic?
options: The router's output queue buffers it; if the queue fills, packets are dropped (tail drop or RED)
The router automatically slows the source using TCP backpressure
The router fragments packets to reduce their size
Excess traffic is returned to the sender
questions.explanation: When traffic arrives faster than the outbound interface can transmit, the output queue buffers it. When the queue is full, new packets are dropped (tail drop). QoS policies manage which packets are dropped first.
hints: Queue full = packet drop (tail drop); QoS = smarter drop/scheduling

questions.question: What is the 6-byte Ethernet frame preamble and SFD used for?
options: Allow receiving NICs to synchronize their clock to the sender before the frame data begins; SFD marks the start of the actual frame
Store the source and destination MAC addresses
Provide error detection for the frame
questions.explanation: Preamble (7 bytes alternating 1/0): clock sync. SFD (Start Frame Delimiter, 1 byte 10101011): marks the end of preamble and start of actual frame. Not included in the 64-byte minimum frame size calculation.
hints: Preamble+SFD = 8 bytes for clock sync before frame data; not counted in frame size

questions.question: Which statement about IGMP is correct?
options: IGMP allows hosts to join IPv4 multicast groups; routers use IGMP to track which interfaces have group members
IGMP is used by routers to advertise multicast routes
IGMP is the IPv6 multicast management protocol
IGMP eliminates the need for PIM
questions.explanation: IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol): hosts send IGMP Join to their local router to request multicast traffic. Routers track memberships per interface using IGMP, then use PIM to build multicast trees.
hints: IGMP = host ↔ local router; PIM = router ↔ router for multicast routing

questions.question: What information does a DNS PTR record contain?
options: Maps an IP address back to a hostname (reverse DNS lookup)
Maps a hostname to an IPv4 address
Identifies a mail server for the domain
Provides load balancing by mapping one name to multiple IPs
questions.explanation: PTR (Pointer) record: IP → hostname reverse lookup. Stored in the in-addr.arpa zone (IPv4) or ip6.arpa zone (IPv6). Used by spam filters, log readability, and security tools.
hints: PTR = reverse DNS; IP → hostname

questions.question: What does a 'half-open' TCP connection mean?
options: A connection where the SYN has been received and SYN-ACK sent but the final ACK has not arrived yet
A connection with only one-way data flow
A connection using only half the TCP window
questions.explanation: Half-open (half-established): the server has replied with SYN-ACK and is waiting for the client's ACK to complete the handshake. SYN flood attacks exploit this by creating thousands of half-open connections.
hints: Half-open = SYN received, SYN-ACK sent, waiting for ACK; exploited by SYN flood

questions.question: What is the number of IPv4 addresses available in a /20 subnet?
questions.explanation: /20 = 12 host bits. 2^12 = 4096 total addresses (4094 usable).
hints: Total addresses = 2^(32-prefix) = 2^12 = 4096

questions.question: Which field in the TCP header contains the number of the next byte the sender expects to receive?
questions.explanation: ACK number: the next byte number the receiver expects. If the receiver has received bytes 0-999, the ACK number is 1000. The sender knows bytes through 999 are confirmed.
hints: ACK number = next expected byte; confirms receipt of all bytes before it

questions.question: A router has two static routes to the same destination with different ADs. Which statement is true?
options: Only the lower-AD route is installed in the routing table; the higher-AD route is a floating static used as backup
Both routes are installed and load-balanced
AD doesn't affect static route selection
questions.explanation: When two routes point to the same destination, lower AD wins. The higher-AD route is not installed. If the lower-AD route disappears (interface down), the floating static takes over.
hints: Lower AD = active route; higher AD = floating backup (inactive while primary exists)

questions.question: Which protocol provides Layer 2 loop prevention in a network with redundant switch links?
questions.explanation: STP/RSTP places redundant Layer 2 ports in blocking state to prevent loops. Only one active path exists between any two points. LACP bundles links (no loop), HSRP is gateway redundancy.
hints: STP = L2 loop prevention; blocks redundant paths

questions.question: What is the IP address range for the TEST-NET documentation block commonly used in examples?
questions.explanation: 192.0.2.0/24 is TEST-NET-1 (RFC 5737), used in documentation and examples. Also 198.51.100.0/24 and 203.0.113.0/24. Not routable on the internet.
hints: 192.0.2.0/24 = TEST-NET-1; appears in exam questions and RFC examples

questions.question: Which IPv6 address type is used for one-to-nearest delivery (routing to the closest interface with that address)?
questions.explanation: IPv6 Anycast: an address assigned to multiple interfaces; routing sends packets to the topologically nearest one. Used for load balancing and redundancy (e.g., DNS root servers, CDNs). IPv6 has no broadcast.
hints: Anycast = one-to-nearest; IPv6 unicast/multicast/anycast; no broadcast in IPv6

questions.question: What decimal value does 11111111 (8 binary ones) represent?
questions.explanation: 11111111 = 128+64+32+16+8+4+2+1 = 255. This is the value for a fully-masked octet in a subnet mask.

questions.question: A /16 subnet is split into /20 subnets. How many /20 subnets are created?
questions.explanation: /20 borrows 4 bits from /16 (20-16=4). 2^4=16 subnets.
hints: Borrowed bits = 20-16=4; subnets = 2^4 = 16

questions.question: What does 'connectionless' mean in the context of UDP?
options: No session is established before data is sent; each datagram is independent with no prior handshake
UDP cannot connect to remote hosts at all
UDP uses unreliable hardware connections only
UDP transmits data in a continuous stream without packetization
questions.explanation: Connectionless: UDP sends datagrams without first establishing a session (no SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK). Each datagram is processed independently by the receiver. Faster but unreliable.
hints: Connectionless = no handshake; each packet independent; no guaranteed delivery

questions.question: Which field in the Ethernet frame is used for error detection?
questions.explanation: FCS (4 bytes, CRC-32): the sender computes a CRC over the frame; the receiver recomputes and compares. If mismatch, the frame is dropped. FCS detects bit errors in transmission.
hints: FCS = CRC-based error detection; corrupt frame = dropped

questions.question: How many bits does an IPv4 address contain, and how is it typically written?
options: 32 bits, written as four octets in dotted decimal (e.g., 192.168.1.1)
questions.explanation: IPv4: 32 bits total, divided into four 8-bit octets written in dotted decimal notation (0-255 per octet). IPv6 is 128 bits.
hints: IPv4=32 bits=4 octets dotted decimal; IPv6=128 bits=8 hex groups

questions.question: A wireless client shows 'Limited Connectivity' after connecting to a WPA2 AP. What is the most likely cause?
options: The client authenticated successfully but DHCP failed — received no IP address
questions.explanation: 'Limited Connectivity' (Windows) or APIPA address means L2 association succeeded but DHCP failed. Causes: DHCP server unreachable, pool exhausted, VLAN misconfiguration, or ACL blocking DHCP.
hints: Limited connectivity = L2 OK, DHCP failed; troubleshoot DHCP path

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 802.11 RTS/CTS mechanism?
options: Reserves the wireless medium before transmitting large frames to prevent collisions, especially useful in hidden node scenarios
RTS/CTS provides encryption for management frames
questions.explanation: RTS/CTS: sender sends RTS, AP responds with CTS. All stations that hear either frame defer transmission for the duration. Solves the hidden node problem where two clients can't hear each other.
hints: RTS/CTS = medium reservation; solves hidden node problem

questions.question: What does 'channel bonding' or 'channel aggregation' do in 802.11n and 802.11ac?
options: Combines two adjacent 20 MHz channels into a 40 MHz (or 80/160 MHz) channel to double or quadruple bandwidth
Allows two APs to share the same channel without interference
Bonds client MACs together for higher throughput
Splits one channel into multiple lower-bandwidth channels
questions.explanation: Channel bonding: 802.11n supports 20/40 MHz; 802.11ac adds 80 MHz and 160 MHz channels. Wider channel = higher throughput (at cost of using more spectrum).
hints: 40 MHz = 2×20 MHz bonded; 80 MHz = 4×20 MHz; wider = more throughput

questions.question: Which command on a Cisco switch enables LLDP globally?
questions.explanation: `lldp run` enables LLDP globally. `lldp transmit` and `lldp receive` enable per interface. `no lldp run` disables globally. CDP and LLDP can run simultaneously.
hints: lldp run = enable LLDP globally; lldp transmit/receive = per-interface

questions.question: What is the maximum cable length for 100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet) over Cat5e UTP?
questions.explanation: 100BASE-TX (and all 10/100/1000 BASE-T Ethernet): 100 meter maximum segment length over UTP Cat5e/Cat6. This includes the cable and any patch panels.
hints: All copper BASE-T Ethernet = 100m max segment

questions.question: A port security violation is set to `protect`. What happens when the maximum MAC count is exceeded?
options: Frames from unknown MACs are silently dropped; the port stays up and no log/trap is generated
A syslog message is generated and the frame is dropped
The excess MAC is added to a 'quarantine' VLAN
questions.explanation: Port security violation modes: protect=drop silently (no log, no trap), restrict=drop+syslog+SNMP trap, shutdown=err-disable the port. Protect is the least disruptive.
hints: Protect=drop+silent; Restrict=drop+log; Shutdown=err-disable

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `spanning-tree vlan <id> priority 4096` command?
options: Lowers the bridge priority to 4096 (below default 32768), increasing the chance this switch wins the root bridge election for that VLAN
Raises the priority to make this switch less likely to be root
Sets the root path cost to 4096
Configures PortFast on all ports for VLAN <id>
questions.explanation: Lower bridge priority = better chance of winning root bridge election. Default = 32768. Valid values must be multiples of 4096: 0, 4096, 8192, ... 61440.
hints: Lower priority = more likely to be root; priority must be multiple of 4096

questions.question: Which 802.11 security improvement in WPA3 replaces the 4-way handshake PSK with a method that prevents offline dictionary attacks?
options: SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals / Dragonfly key exchange)
questions.explanation: WPA3-Personal uses SAE (SAE = Simultaneous Authentication of Equals, based on the Dragonfly handshake). SAE requires real-time interaction for each attempt, making offline dictionary attacks computationally infeasible.
hints: WPA3 SAE = Dragonfly; no offline crack; requires online interaction per attempt

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `switchport access vlan <id>` command?
options: Assigns the port to a specific VLAN when in access mode — all frames sent/received on this port belong to that VLAN
Changes the port from access to trunk mode
Sets the native VLAN on a trunk port
Allows only that VLAN on the trunk
questions.explanation: `switchport access vlan <id>` assigns an access port to a specific VLAN. Must also have `switchport mode access` (or the port must not be in trunk mode). Default VLAN for access ports is VLAN 1.
hints: switchport access vlan = VLAN assignment for access ports

questions.question: Which command shows the STP port role (Root/Designated/Alternate) and state (FWD/BLK) for all VLANs?
questions.explanation: `show spanning-tree` (without VLAN filter) shows STP summary for all VLANs. `show spanning-tree vlan <id>` gives detailed info for a specific VLAN.
hints: show spanning-tree = all VLANs summary; show spanning-tree vlan X = per-VLAN detail

questions.question: What is the 802.11 DIFS (Distributed Interframe Space) used for?
options: A mandatory backoff period before transmitting a data frame in CSMA/CA — ensures the medium is clear before competing for access
DIFS is used only for management frames
DIFS sets the maximum transmission time per frame
DIFS is the WPA key exchange interval
questions.explanation: CSMA/CA: a station waits until the medium is idle for DIFS, then adds a random backoff before transmitting. This reduces simultaneous transmissions.
hints: DIFS = wait time after medium becomes idle before attempting transmission

questions.question: A switch has 24 access ports in VLAN 10 and 2 uplink trunks to the distribution layer. What STP port role will each of the 24 access ports hold?
options: Designated port (forwarding) — access ports are always Designated because no alternative paths exist below them
Root port — all ports facing end devices are Root ports
Alternate port — end devices don't participate in STP
They vary depending on the host's STP priority
questions.explanation: STP roles are from the switch's perspective. Access ports connecting to end hosts (not other switches) have no competing bridge sending BPDUs. They become Designated ports (forwarding). Hosts send no BPDUs.
hints: Access ports to PCs = Designated (no competition, always forwarding)

questions.question: What happens when `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` is configured globally on a Cisco switch?
options: BPDU Guard is automatically enabled on all PortFast-enabled ports across the entire switch
BPDU Guard is enabled on all ports including trunk ports
Only manually configured BPDU Guard ports are affected
The command enables PortFast on all ports
questions.explanation: `spanning-tree portfast bpduguard default` enables BPDU Guard on every interface where PortFast is active. This is a best-practice global configuration to protect all access ports.
hints: bpduguard default = BPDU Guard on all PortFast ports globally

questions.question: Two switches are directly connected with a cable but `show cdp neighbors` shows no neighbor. What is the most likely cause?
options: CDP is disabled on one or both switches (or on the connecting interface)
The link is a trunk port
The switches are from different vendors
The switches are in different VLANs
questions.explanation: CDP must be enabled globally (`cdp run`) AND on the interface (`no cdp disable` or check `cdp enable`). If CDP is disabled on either switch or either interface, no neighbor is shown.
hints: No CDP neighbor: check 'show cdp' (global) and 'show cdp interface' (per-port)

questions.question: Which trunking mode will form a trunk only if the neighbor requests it, but won't initiate trunk negotiation itself?
questions.explanation: DTP modes: access=never trunk, dynamic auto=form trunk only if neighbor initiates (passive), dynamic desirable=actively try to form trunk, trunk=always trunk regardless.
hints: dynamic auto = passive (won't initiate); dynamic desirable = active (initiates)

questions.question: What does `show interfaces trunk` show in the 'VLANs in spanning tree forwarding state and not pruned' column?
options: The VLANs that are actually forwarding traffic on this trunk — in the allowed list, active in VLAN DB, and STP is in forwarding state
All VLANs in the allowed list
All active VLANs on the switch
questions.explanation: The final column shows VLANs that meet all three conditions: in the allowed list, exist in the VLAN database, AND are in STP forwarding state. This is the definitive list of VLANs actually passing traffic.
hints: show interfaces trunk final column = VLANs actually forwarding (all three conditions met)

questions.question: Which VLAN range is classified as 'extended VLAN' in Cisco's VLAN classification?
questions.explanation: Cisco VLAN ranges: Normal (1–1005), Extended (1006–4094). VLANs 1002-1005 are reserved for legacy protocols. Extended VLANs require VTP transparent mode or VTP v3.
hints: Normal VLANs: 1-1005; Extended: 1006-4094; VTP v1/v2 only supports normal range

questions.question: What is the purpose of a wireless probe request?
options: A client broadcasts Probe Requests to discover available SSIDs; APs respond with Probe Responses containing SSID and capabilities
Probe requests are sent by APs to measure interference
Probes are used to test WPA2 authentication
Probe frames carry payload data to test link quality
questions.explanation: Active scanning: client sends Probe Request to all channels. APs that receive it respond with Probe Response (SSID, supported rates, security). Allows faster network discovery than passive Beacon listening.
hints: Probe Request = active scanning by client; AP responds with Probe Response

questions.question: Which command shows the OSPF LSDB (Link State Database)?
questions.explanation: `show ip ospf database` displays the LSDB contents — all LSAs by type (Router, Network, Summary, etc.) that the router has received and stored.
hints: show ip ospf database = LSDB contents; show ip ospf neighbor = adjacency info

questions.question: What is the EIGRP variance feature used for?
options: Allows unequal-cost load balancing across paths with metrics within a multiplier of the successor's metric
Forces all traffic to the best path only
Sets the maximum metric for routes to be installed
Configures the K-value weights for EIGRP metric calculation
questions.explanation: EIGRP variance: `variance <multiplier>` allows feasible successors with FD up to <multiplier> × successor's FD to be installed in the routing table and share traffic proportionally.
hints: variance = unequal load balancing; metrics within variance × best FD are used

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 Null0` route commonly found in route summarization configurations?
options: Prevents routing loops by discarding traffic that matches the summary but has no more specific route
Creates a default route for internet traffic
Sends all traffic to the null interface for testing
Enables ECMP for the summary route
questions.explanation: A summary route with Null0 as the exit interface acts as a 'discard route' for sub-prefixes not specifically routed. Without it, routers might forward mismatched packets to the default gateway in a loop.
hints: Summary + Null0 = discard unmatched sub-routes; prevents routing loops

questions.question: OSPF uses which algorithm to calculate the shortest path through the network?
questions.explanation: OSPF is a link-state protocol that uses Dijkstra's SPF algorithm to compute the shortest path tree from the router's perspective using the LSDB. EIGRP uses DUAL. RIP uses Bellman-Ford.
hints: OSPF = link-state + Dijkstra SPF; EIGRP = DUAL; RIP = Bellman-Ford

questions.question: Which static route type doesn't require a recursive lookup and uses the exit interface directly?
options: Recursive static route with next-hop IP
questions.explanation: Directly attached static routes specify the exit interface instead of a next-hop IP. They don't require a recursive lookup to resolve the next-hop. Caveat on multi-access networks: they generate proxy ARP/neighbor entries for every destination.
hints: ip route X X GigabitEthernet0/1 = directly attached; no recursive lookup needed

questions.question: What is the purpose of the OSPF MaxAge value (3600 seconds)?
options: LSAs with age reaching MaxAge are considered expired and are flushed from the LSDB and refloded to notify neighbors
MaxAge is the hold-down timer after a neighbor failure
MaxAge defines the OSPF hello interval for stub areas
LSAs are refreshed every 3600 seconds regardless of topology changes
questions.explanation: OSPF LSA age: increments each second. At MaxAge (3600s), the LSA is considered stale, flushed from the LSDB, and re-flooded with MaxAge to tell neighbors to remove it. LSAs are refreshed every 1800s by the originator.
hints: MaxAge=3600s; LSAs refreshed every 1800s; at MaxAge = flush and re-flood

questions.question: Which EIGRP feature prevents routing loops without requiring periodic full routing table exchanges?
options: DUAL (Diffusing Update Algorithm) with the Feasibility Condition
questions.explanation: EIGRP's DUAL algorithm uses the Feasibility Condition (neighbor RD < local FD) to guarantee loop-free paths mathematically, without the need for periodic full table exchanges like RIP.
hints: DUAL + Feasibility Condition = loop-free guarantee without holddown timers

questions.question: What does `show ip eigrp topology all-links` display that `show ip eigrp topology` does not?
options: Shows ALL paths including those that don't meet the feasibility condition (not just successors and FSs)
Displays EIGRP hello interval and hold timers
questions.explanation: `show ip eigrp topology` shows only routes with successors and feasible successors. `all-links` adds paths that failed the feasibility condition — useful for troubleshooting why a route is active.
hints: all-links = shows paths that failed FC; useful when route goes active

questions.question: What is the purpose of OSPF authentication and what are the supported types?
options: Prevents unauthorized OSPF routers from injecting false LSAs; supports null (type 0), simple plaintext (type 1), and MD5 (type 2)
OSPF authentication encrypts all routing data
Authentication is required for all OSPF areas except Area 0
questions.explanation: OSPF auth: Type 0=no auth, Type 1=plaintext password (visible in packets), Type 2=MD5 hash. MD5 ensures only routers with the correct key form adjacencies and accept LSAs.
hints: OSPF auth: 0=none, 1=plaintext, 2=MD5; use MD5 in production

questions.question: Which IPv6 prefix is assigned to globally routable unicast addresses by IANA for general use?
questions.explanation: 2000::/3 is the globally routable unicast range (addresses starting with 001 in the first 3 bits, i.e., 2000:: through 3FFF::). FC00::/7 = unique local, FE80::/10 = link-local, FF00::/8 = multicast.
hints: Global unicast = 2000::/3 (starts with 2 or 3); unique local = FC/FD; link-local = FE80

questions.question: Which command configures the OSPF cost on a router interface to 10?
questions.explanation: `ip ospf cost <value>` configured under the interface manually overrides the auto-calculated OSPF cost. Used to influence path selection without changing bandwidth.
hints: ip ospf cost = manual override of the calculated OSPF interface cost

questions.question: What does the `show ip nat translations verbose` command add to the standard output?
options: Adds timing information (age, timeout) and flags for each translation entry
Displays the NAT configuration (pool and access-list)
Verbose shows translations for a specific inside host only
questions.explanation: `verbose` adds the remaining TTL (time to expiry), protocol, and flags to each NAT table entry — useful for diagnosing translation timeouts.
hints: verbose = adds age/timeout info to each NAT entry

questions.question: Which protocol is used by the syslog client to send messages to a syslog server?
questions.explanation: Traditional syslog uses UDP port 514. RFC 6587 defines TCP syslog for reliable delivery. Cisco IOS uses UDP 514 by default unless TCP syslog is explicitly configured.
hints: Syslog default: UDP 514; TCP 514 for reliable syslog

questions.question: What is SNMP's Management Information Base (MIB)?
options: A hierarchical database of OIDs (Object Identifiers) that defines what information can be read from or written to a managed device
A list of SNMP managers authorized to poll the device
questions.explanation: MIB: a tree-structured collection of OIDs organized under standard and vendor-specific branches. Each OID represents a manageable parameter (interface speed, CPU usage, etc.).
hints: MIB = OID tree; OID = unique identifier for each manageable parameter

questions.question: What is the purpose of `ip nat inside source list <acl> pool <name> overload` in a NAT configuration?
options: Translates inside addresses matching the ACL using addresses from the pool, with PAT overload when the pool is exhausted
The 'overload' keyword is not needed when using a pool
Creates static NAT for each address in the ACL
questions.explanation: When the pool runs out of addresses, `overload` enables PAT as a fallback, using the pool addresses with port numbers to support additional translations.
hints: pool + overload = use pool IPs first; PAT when pool is full

questions.question: A DHCP server has the pool: network 10.10.0.0 255.255.0.0, lease 2 days, default-router 10.10.0.1. A client gets 10.10.50.100. What is the client's lease renewal time (T1)?
questions.explanation: DHCP T1 = 50% of lease time. 2 days × 0.5 = 1 day. T2 = 87.5% = 42 hours.
hints: T1=50% of lease; T2=87.5% of lease

questions.question: Which command verifies that NTP synchronization is working on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `show ntp status`: shows sync state, stratum, reference. `show ntp associations`: shows configured server IPs and sync status (* = selected peer). `show clock detail`: shows current time and source.
hints: show ntp status + show ntp associations = NTP verification; * = synced peer

questions.question: What is the difference between `logging synchronous` and `logging buffered` on a Cisco device?
options: logging synchronous re-displays the CLI prompt after a syslog message interrupts typing; logging buffered stores messages in RAM
logging synchronous writes to a server; logging buffered writes to console
logging synchronous prevents logging; logging buffered enables it
questions.explanation: `logging synchronous` on a line (console/VTY): re-displays your command after a log message appears mid-type, preventing confusing output. `logging buffered` stores messages in RAM for `show logging`.
hints: logging synchronous = prevent log messages from corrupting your input line

questions.question: What is the purpose of the VRRP preempt feature (enabled by default)?
options: When the master fails and a new master is elected, preempt allows the original master to reclaim master status when it recovers (if it has higher priority)
Preempt prevents any failover from occurring
Preempt forces the standby to become active immediately on hello timeout
Preempt is required for VRRP to function
questions.explanation: VRRP preempt: enabled by default (unlike HSRP where it must be explicitly configured). The highest-priority router becomes master, and reclaims master status when it recovers.
hints: VRRP: preempt ON by default; HSRP: preempt OFF by default

questions.question: What happens to HSRP if both routers have the same priority and preempt is enabled on both?
options: The router with the highest IP address on the HSRP interface becomes active
The router with the lowest MAC becomes active
HSRP disables itself to prevent a loop
questions.explanation: HSRP tie-breaking: if priorities are equal, the router with the highest IP address on the HSRP group interface wins. (Note: MAC is the tiebreaker for STP, not HSRP.)
hints: HSRP tie: highest IP wins; STP tie: lowest MAC wins

questions.question: Which command enables SNMPv3 with authentication and encryption on a Cisco router?
options: snmp-server user <user> <group> v3 auth sha <authpw> priv aes 128 <privpw>
questions.explanation: `snmp-server user` configures SNMPv3 users with auth (SHA/MD5) and priv (AES/DES) parameters. The user must be associated with a group that has the appropriate security level.
hints: SNMPv3: snmp-server user + snmp-server group + snmp-server view

questions.question: What is the effect of the `service timestamps log datetime msec` command?
options: Adds date, time, and milliseconds to syslog messages — essential for precise event correlation
Sets the router's clock from syslog server timestamps
Synchronizes the clock via NTP on each log event
questions.explanation: `service timestamps log datetime msec` adds accurate timestamps (synchronized with NTP) to all syslog messages. Critical for correlating events across devices in incident investigations.
hints: service timestamps = add date/time to log messages; combine with NTP for accuracy

questions.question: What is a 'man-in-the-middle' (MITM) attack?
options: Attacker intercepts communication between two parties, reading and optionally modifying traffic without either party knowing
A denial of service attack on a single host
A physical attack on a network cable
An attack where the attacker floods the network with fake routing updates
questions.explanation: MITM: attacker positions themselves between two communicating parties, intercepting, reading, and potentially altering the traffic. Common in wireless environments or via ARP/DNS poisoning.
hints: MITM = intercept + potentially modify; attacker is invisible in the middle

questions.question: Which command on a Cisco switch verifies DAI (Dynamic ARP Inspection) is enabled and shows drop/forward counts?
questions.explanation: `show ip arp inspection` displays DAI status per VLAN (enabled/disabled), forwarded/dropped packet counts, and DHCP snooping association.
hints: show ip arp inspection = DAI status and counters per VLAN

questions.question: What is the purpose of `banner motd` on a Cisco device?
options: Displays a legal notice/warning message to anyone connecting before login — satisfies legal requirements for prosecution of unauthorized access
Banner MOTD sets the device hostname
MOTD is displayed only after successful authentication
MOTD provides help text for CLI commands
questions.explanation: Banner MOTD (Message of the Day): displayed before the login prompt. Should contain a legal warning (e.g., 'Authorized access only'). Without it, unauthorized access may not be prosecutable.
hints: banner motd = pre-login legal notice; required for legal action against intruders

questions.question: What is the difference between a worm and a virus?
options: Worm: self-propagates over network without host program; Virus: requires a host file and user execution to spread
Virus is always destructive; worm is harmless
Worm only affects Windows; virus is cross-platform
They are identical — 'worm' is the term used in Unix environments
questions.explanation: Virus: attaches to a file; needs user to execute the host file. Worm: self-replicating, spreads autonomously over network (exploits vulnerabilities) without user interaction.
hints: Worm = self-propagates (no user needed); Virus = needs host file + execution

questions.question: An ACL is required to permit HTTPS from any host to server 10.5.5.5. Which ACL entry is correct?
questions.explanation: `permit tcp any host 10.5.5.5 eq 443` — source=any, destination=10.5.5.5, protocol=TCP, destination port=443 (HTTPS). The `host` keyword equals a /32 with wildcard 0.0.0.0.
hints: Extended ACL: permit <protocol> <src> <dst> eq <port>; HTTPS=TCP 443

questions.question: What is the purpose of Cisco IOS Guest Shell?
options: A Linux container embedded in IOS XE that provides a Python execution environment and access to IOS APIs for on-box automation
A restricted CLI mode for read-only access
A virtualized firewall running within the router
A web-based management interface for IOS
questions.explanation: Guest Shell: a bash-ready Linux container in IOS XE. Run Python scripts on-box, access the IOS NETCONF/RESTCONF APIs locally, schedule EEM applets. No external server needed for simple automation.
hints: Guest Shell = Python environment embedded in IOS XE; on-box automation

questions.question: Which Python library is used to interact with Cisco IOS devices via SSH, providing a consistent interface for multiple vendors?
questions.explanation: Netmiko: built on top of paramiko, provides vendor-specific connection handlers for Cisco IOS, IOS XE, IOS XR, NX-OS, and many others. Simplifies `send_command()`, `send_config_set()` etc.
hints: netmiko = multi-vendor SSH abstraction; built on paramiko

questions.question: What does a 200 OK HTTP response code indicate when using a REST API?
options: The request succeeded; the response body contains the requested data
The request was accepted but not yet processed
questions.explanation: HTTP 200 OK = success for GET/PUT/PATCH. 201 Created = POST created new resource. 204 No Content = success with no response body. 401 = Unauthorized. 404 = Not Found.
hints: 200=OK, 201=Created, 204=No Content, 400=Bad Request, 401=Auth, 404=Not Found

questions.question: What is Terraform used for in network automation?
options: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool that declaratively defines and provisions infrastructure across cloud and on-premises providers
A Cisco proprietary tool for DNA Center automation
A Python testing framework for network device verification
questions.explanation: Terraform (HashiCorp): open-source IaC tool using HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language). Providers available for AWS, Azure, GCP, and network vendors (Cisco ACI, NSO, etc.). Plans and applies changes declaratively.
hints: Terraform = IaC for cloud and network; HCL config files; plan → apply workflow

questions.question: Which Ansible module would you use to send CLI commands to a Cisco IOS device and capture the output?
questions.explanation: `ios_command` sends show/exec commands to IOS devices and returns output. `ios_config` applies configuration changes. Both are part of the `cisco.ios` collection in Ansible Galaxy.
hints: ios_command = show commands (read); ios_config = config changes (write)

questions.question: What is the purpose of model-driven telemetry's 'dial-out' mode?
options: The network device initiates the connection to the collector and pushes data at configured intervals or on change
The collector polls the device at regular intervals
Dial-out is used only for SNMP traps
Dial-out requires the device to have a public IP
questions.explanation: MDT dial-out: device establishes the gRPC/gNMI connection to the collector and pushes data proactively. Dial-in: collector initiates the connection. Dial-out scales better for large deployments.
hints: Dial-out = device initiates + pushes; dial-in = collector initiates + subscribes

questions.question: What is Cisco Intersight primarily used for?
options: Cloud-based infrastructure management for Cisco UCS compute and HyperFlex converged infrastructure
questions.explanation: Cisco Intersight: SaaS-delivered management platform for UCS compute, HyperFlex, and integrating with cloud providers. Provides lifecycle management, telemetry, and automation via REST APIs.
hints: Intersight = cloud-managed compute/HyperFlex; REST API; SaaS delivery

questions.question: Which encoding format is required by default for RESTCONF in RFC 8040?
options: JSON and XML are both supported; the client specifies via Accept/Content-Type headers
questions.explanation: RFC 8040 RESTCONF supports both JSON and XML encoding. The client uses `Accept: application/yang-data+json` or `application/yang-data+xml` to request preferred format.
hints: RESTCONF supports JSON and XML; client specifies via Accept header

questions.question: What does 'event-driven automation' mean in network operations?
options: Automation triggers are based on network events (interface down, threshold exceeded, log message) rather than scheduled or manual execution
Automation runs at fixed intervals only
Event-driven means manual CLI triggers are automated
All network changes require operator approval events
questions.explanation: Event-driven automation: Cisco EEM (Embedded Event Manager) or external tools like StackStorm trigger playbooks/scripts when specific events occur (syslog, SNMP trap, threshold). Reduces mean time to repair.
hints: Event-driven = trigger on conditions (not schedule); EEM is Cisco's on-box solution

questions.question: What is the relationship between YANG modules and NETCONF/RESTCONF?
options: YANG defines the data model (schema) and NETCONF/RESTCONF are the protocols that carry YANG-modeled data between the manager and the device
YANG is a transport protocol; NETCONF/RESTCONF are data models
YANG replaces both NETCONF and RESTCONF
NETCONF defines the schema; YANG is the transport
questions.explanation: YANG = schema language (defines structure, types, constraints). NETCONF and RESTCONF = protocols that use YANG models to know what operations and data are valid on a given device.
hints: YANG = what data looks like (schema); NETCONF/RESTCONF = how to send it (protocol)

questions.scenario_text: A network engineer receives a complaint that TCP connections from PC-A (192.168.1.10) to a web server (10.0.0.5) are failing intermittently. Using Wireshark, she captures traffic and observes TCP SYN packets leaving the NIC but no SYN-ACK returning.

sub_questions.question: At which OSI layer does a TCP SYN segment exist before Ethernet encapsulation adds the frame header?
sub_questions.explanation: TCP is a Layer 4 (Transport) protocol. The segment exists at Layer 4 before Layer 2 encapsulation wraps it in an Ethernet frame.

sub_questions.question: What is the PDU name for data at Layer 2 of the OSI model?
sub_questions.explanation: PDU names: L1=Bit, L2=Frame, L3=Packet, L4=Segment, L5-7=Data.

sub_questions.question: A router processes PDUs up to OSI Layer 3 only, stripping the Layer 2 header to make forwarding decisions.
sub_questions.explanation: Routers operate at L3. They strip the incoming L2 frame, read the L3 IP header to determine the next hop, then re-encapsulate in a new L2 frame for the outgoing interface.

questions.scenario_text: A company runs two critical applications: VoIP calls and large file transfers. The network team is designing QoS policies and needs to understand the transport protocols each application uses to make appropriate queuing decisions.

sub_questions.question: VoIP applications typically use which transport protocol, and for what reason?
options: TCP — guaranteed delivery ensures voice quality
UDP — low overhead is preferred; retransmission delays would worsen call quality
TCP — the three-way handshake synchronises call endpoints
UDP — it provides built-in flow control for voice streams
sub_questions.explanation: VoIP uses UDP. Voice is delay-sensitive; a retransmitted late packet would cause more disruption than a missing one. UDP's lower overhead and no retransmission mechanism suit real-time media.

sub_questions.question: Which TCP mechanism prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver?
sub_questions.explanation: TCP flow control uses a receive window (advertised in every TCP header). The sender cannot send more unacknowledged data than the receiver's window allows.

sub_questions.question: UDP provides acknowledgement and retransmission of lost segments, just like TCP.
sub_questions.explanation: UDP is connectionless with no acknowledgement, retransmission, sequencing, or flow control. Applications that need reliability must implement it themselves above UDP.

questions.scenario_text: A technician is configuring a new subnet for 50 hosts in a branch office. She has been allocated the 192.168.10.0 address block and must choose the smallest subnet mask that accommodates all 50 hosts.

sub_questions.question: Which prefix length provides enough space for 50 hosts with the least address waste?
sub_questions.explanation: /26 gives 2^6 − 2 = 62 usable hosts, which covers 50. /27 only gives 30, which is insufficient. /25 works but wastes 76 addresses. /26 is the best fit.

sub_questions.question: For subnet 192.168.10.64/26, what is the broadcast address?
sub_questions.explanation: 192.168.10.64/26 spans .64–.127. The broadcast is the last address: 192.168.10.127.

sub_questions.question: The network address 192.168.10.64 can be assigned to a host interface on the /26 subnet.
sub_questions.explanation: 192.168.10.64 is the network (subnet) address for the /26 subnet. It cannot be assigned to a host. Usable host range is .65–.126.

questions.scenario_text: A Cisco Catalyst switch is operating normally. A new host connects to an access port and sends its first Ethernet frame destined for a server whose MAC address is not yet in the switch's MAC address table.

sub_questions.question: When the switch receives a frame with an unknown destination MAC address, what does it do?
options: Floods the frame out all ports except the ingress port
Sends an ICMP error back to the source
Forwards the frame only to the default gateway
sub_questions.explanation: When a switch has no MAC table entry for the destination, it floods the frame out every port in the same VLAN except the port it arrived on (unknown unicast flooding).

sub_questions.question: How does the switch learn the MAC address of the new host?
options: By examining the destination MAC of frames the host sends
By examining the source MAC of frames the host sends
By sending an ARP request to the host
By consulting a DHCP binding table
sub_questions.explanation: Switches learn MAC addresses by reading the source MAC field of incoming frames and associating it with the ingress port in the MAC address table.

sub_questions.question: What is the standard maximum payload (MTU) of an Ethernet II frame?
sub_questions.explanation: The Ethernet II MTU (maximum payload) is 1500 bytes. The maximum frame size including header and FCS is 1518 bytes (or 1522 with 802.1Q tag).

questions.scenario_text: A company is migrating to IPv6. The engineer assigns the address 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334/64 to a router interface and needs to communicate the address to the team in shortened form.

sub_questions.question: What is the fully compressed form of 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334?
sub_questions.explanation: Two consecutive all-zero groups (0000:0000) are compressed to ::. Leading zeros are removed from each group. The :: can appear only once. Result: 2001:db8:85a3::8a2e:370:7334.

sub_questions.question: If no static address is configured, EUI-64 generates an interface identifier from which source?
sub_questions.explanation: EUI-64 splits the 48-bit MAC into two 24-bit halves, inserts FF:FE in the middle, and flips the universal/local bit to create a 64-bit interface ID.

sub_questions.question: An IPv6 link-local address begins with FE80::/10 and is automatically generated on every IPv6-enabled interface.
sub_questions.explanation: All IPv6 interfaces automatically generate a link-local address in the FE80::/10 range. Link-local addresses are used for on-link communication, neighbor discovery, and routing protocol packets.

questions.scenario_text: A network engineer is configuring a Cisco Catalyst switch. PC-A is assigned to VLAN 10 (Finance) and PC-B is in VLAN 20 (HR). Both PCs connect to the same physical switch but must be isolated from each other at Layer 2.

sub_questions.question: Without a Layer 3 routing device, which statement about VLAN 10 and VLAN 20 is correct?
options: They can communicate through the switch's backplane
They cannot communicate — VLANs are separate broadcast domains at Layer 2
They can communicate using static routes on the switch
They can communicate if both ports are in STP forwarding state
sub_questions.explanation: VLANs segment a switch into separate Layer 2 broadcast domains. Without a router or Layer 3 switch to route between VLANs, inter-VLAN communication is impossible.

sub_questions.question: A port connecting an end device (PC) to VLAN 10 should be configured as which port type?
sub_questions.explanation: Access ports carry traffic for a single VLAN. End devices connect to access ports. Trunk ports carry tagged traffic for multiple VLANs between switches and routers.

sub_questions.question: The native VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk carries frames without a VLAN tag.
sub_questions.explanation: 802.1Q defines a native VLAN whose frames are sent untagged across a trunk. Both ends must agree on the native VLAN to avoid frames appearing in the wrong VLAN.

questions.scenario_text: Three switches (SW1, SW2, SW3) are connected in a triangle topology. SW1 has a bridge priority of 4096, SW2 and SW3 have default priorities (32768). All MACs differ. STP is running to prevent a Layer 2 loop.

sub_questions.question: Which switch becomes the root bridge?
options: SW1 — it has the lowest bridge priority
SW2 — it has a higher MAC address than SW3
SW3 — it uses the default priority
All three — they share the root role
sub_questions.explanation: The root bridge is elected by lowest bridge ID. Bridge ID = priority + MAC. SW1's priority (4096) is lower than the default (32768), so SW1 wins regardless of MAC.

sub_questions.question: On non-root switches, which STP port role sends/receives traffic toward the root bridge via the best path?
sub_questions.explanation: Each non-root switch has exactly one Root Port — the port with the lowest cost (best path) to the root bridge.

sub_questions.question: In Rapid PVST+ (802.1w), an edge port (PortFast) transitions directly to forwarding state without waiting 30 seconds.
sub_questions.explanation: PortFast / edge ports skip the Listening and Learning STP states, going directly to forwarding. This is safe only on ports connected to end devices (not other switches).
