questions.scenario_text: Two Cisco switches (SW-Core and SW-Dist) are connected with four parallel Gigabit Ethernet links. The team wants to bundle them into a single logical channel to increase throughput and provide link redundancy without STP blocking any links.

sub_questions.question: Which EtherChannel negotiation protocol is standards-based and works between Cisco and non-Cisco switches?
sub_questions.explanation: LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) is the standards-based protocol for EtherChannel negotiation. PAgP is Cisco-proprietary.

sub_questions.question: If all four 1-Gbps links are active in the Port-Channel, what is the maximum aggregate bandwidth?
options: 1 Gbps — STP limits to one path
sub_questions.explanation: EtherChannel aggregates physical link bandwidth. Four 1-Gbps links = 4 Gbps aggregate (each direction). Load balancing distributes flows across all active links.

sub_questions.question: The channel-group command is applied to individual physical interfaces, not to the Port-channel interface directly.
sub_questions.explanation: You configure 'channel-group N mode active/passive/on' on each physical member interface. The Port-channel interface is created automatically and inherits the configuration.

questions.scenario_text: A company is deploying 50 lightweight APs across three office floors. A Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) sits in the data centre. The APs must be centrally managed and all client traffic must flow through the WLC for policy enforcement.

sub_questions.question: Which protocol do lightweight APs use to form a tunnel to the WLC?
sub_questions.explanation: CAPWAP (Control And Provisioning of Wireless Access Points) is the current standard. It uses UDP port 5246 (control) and 5247 (data) to tunnel AP-controller communications.

sub_questions.question: In a split-MAC (centralised) CAPWAP deployment, where are 802.11 management frames (association, authentication) processed?
sub_questions.explanation: In split-MAC architecture, the WLC handles 802.11 management (association, authentication, security) while the AP handles real-time 802.11 frame transmission/reception.

sub_questions.question: A FlexConnect AP can continue to bridge client traffic locally to the wired network even if the CAPWAP tunnel to the WLC fails.
sub_questions.explanation: FlexConnect (formerly HREAP) allows APs to forward data locally when the WLC is unreachable. This is useful for branch offices with limited WAN bandwidth or WLC availability.

questions.scenario_text: An engineer configures inter-VLAN routing using Router-on-a-Stick. The physical interface G0/0 on the router connects to a trunk port on the switch. VLANs 10, 20, and 99 (native) are in use. The router must route between VLAN 10 and VLAN 20.

sub_questions.question: Which sub-interface command tags frames for VLAN 10 on subinterface G0/0.10?
sub_questions.explanation: 'encapsulation dot1q 10' configures the subinterface to tag outgoing frames with VLAN 10 and accept only VLAN 10-tagged frames inbound.

sub_questions.question: What happens to frames tagged with VLAN 99 (the native VLAN) as they cross the 802.1Q trunk?
sub_questions.explanation: The native VLAN is sent untagged on an 802.1Q trunk. The receiving side assumes untagged frames belong to the native VLAN. Mismatch causes frames to appear in the wrong VLAN.

sub_questions.question: The native VLAN must be identical on both ends of an 802.1Q trunk link to prevent traffic from appearing in the wrong VLAN.
sub_questions.explanation: If native VLANs differ (e.g., one end uses VLAN 1, the other VLAN 99), untagged frames are sent into one VLAN but received into another — a VLAN hopping / mismatch vulnerability.

questions.scenario_text: Two Cisco routers (R1: G0/0 = 10.1.1.1/24, R2: G0/0 = 10.1.1.2/24) are directly connected. OSPF Area 0 is configured on both interfaces, but no neighbor relationship forms. The engineer runs 'show ip ospf neighbor' and sees an empty table.

sub_questions.question: Which combination of mismatched parameters would prevent OSPF neighbors from forming?
options: Mismatched area ID, hello/dead interval, subnet mask, or MTU (if IP MTU check is enabled)
sub_questions.explanation: OSPF neighbors will not form if: area IDs differ, hello/dead intervals differ, subnet masks differ (on broadcast/point-to-point), stub area flags differ, or MTU mismatch (if enabled). Router ID mismatches do not prevent adjacency.

sub_questions.question: A router configured with OSPF priority 0 on an interface will never become which of the following?
sub_questions.explanation: OSPF priority 0 means the router is ineligible for DR/BDR election on that segment. It can still form neighbour relationships and become DROTHER.

sub_questions.question: OSPF calculates interface cost as: reference bandwidth ÷ interface bandwidth. A lower cost path is always preferred.
sub_questions.explanation: Default reference bandwidth is 100 Mbps. A FastEthernet (100 Mbps) interface has cost 1, GigabitEthernet (1000 Mbps) would also be cost 1 unless the reference bandwidth is increased. Lower cost = more preferred path.

questions.scenario_text: A branch router (R-Branch) connects to HQ via a point-to-point WAN link (10.0.0.0/30). R-HQ has interface Gi0/0 = 10.0.0.1/30. R-Branch has Gi0/0 = 10.0.0.2/30. The branch LAN is 192.168.100.0/24. HQ needs reachability to branch hosts.

sub_questions.question: Which static route on R-HQ correctly routes traffic to the 192.168.100.0/24 branch LAN?
sub_questions.explanation: The route is: destination network 192.168.100.0 /24, next hop 10.0.0.2 (R-Branch's WAN interface). Option D uses 10.0.0.1 which is R-HQ's own interface — that loops back.

sub_questions.question: A floating static route uses a higher administrative distance than the primary dynamic routing protocol so it activates only if the dynamic route is removed from the routing table.
sub_questions.explanation: A floating static route (e.g., AD=250) loses to OSPF (AD=110) under normal conditions. If OSPF loses the route (link failure), the floating static route becomes the active route.

sub_questions.question: What is the default administrative distance of a static route on Cisco IOS?
sub_questions.explanation: Static routes have a default AD of 1. Only directly connected routes (AD=0) are more trusted. AD=255 means the route is considered unreachable/unusable.

questions.scenario_text: A large campus network uses multi-area OSPF: Area 0 (backbone), Area 1, and Area 2. An ABR connects Area 1 to Area 0. The network team wants to limit LSA flooding to reduce memory and CPU overhead.

sub_questions.question: Which OSPF LSA type describes the links within a single area and is NOT flooded beyond that area's boundaries?
sub_questions.explanation: Type 1 Router LSAs are flooded only within the originating area. ABRs do not forward Type 1 LSAs; instead they generate Type 3 Summary LSAs to describe inter-area routes.

sub_questions.question: Which area type blocks Type 5 External LSAs but still receives Type 3 Summary LSAs from the ABR?
sub_questions.explanation: A stub area blocks Type 5 External LSAs. The ABR substitutes a default route (Type 3 LSA). A Totally Stubby area blocks both Type 3 and Type 5. NSSA allows limited external routes via Type 7 LSAs.

sub_questions.question: All non-backbone OSPF areas must connect directly to Area 0 for proper inter-area routing.
sub_questions.explanation: OSPF requires all inter-area routing to pass through Area 0. Non-backbone areas that cannot physically connect to Area 0 must use a virtual link through another area that does.

questions.scenario_text: A dual-stack router has IPv6 configured on two interfaces: G0/0 = 2001:db8:1::1/64 and G0/1 = 2001:db8:2::1/64. A host on G0/0 needs to reach a host on G0/1 but pings are failing.

sub_questions.question: Which global configuration command is required to enable IPv6 packet routing between interfaces on a Cisco router?
sub_questions.explanation: 'ipv6 unicast-routing' enables the router to forward IPv6 packets between interfaces. Without it, IPv6 behaves like a host — it accepts IPv6 traffic but does not route between interfaces.

sub_questions.question: Which command displays the IPv6 routing table on a Cisco router?
sub_questions.explanation: 'show ipv6 route' displays the IPv6 routing table including connected, static, and dynamic IPv6 routes.

sub_questions.question: OSPFv3 supports routing of IPv6 prefixes and uses the same Area 0 backbone requirement and router-ID concepts as OSPFv2.
sub_questions.explanation: OSPFv3 uses the same area hierarchy, DR/BDR election, and LSA flooding as OSPFv2. The router ID is still a 32-bit value (often taken from a loopback IPv4 address or configured manually).

questions.scenario_text: Router R1 has three routes to network 172.16.0.0/16 in its routing table: one learned via OSPF (AD=110), one via EIGRP (AD=90), and one configured as a static route (AD=1, next-hop 192.168.1.1).

sub_questions.question: Which route will be installed as the active route in the routing table?
sub_questions.explanation: Administrative distance selects the best source when multiple protocols know a route. Static (AD=1) < EIGRP (AD=90) < OSPF (AD=110). Only the static route is installed.

sub_questions.question: If the static route is removed, which remaining route is preferred?
options: Neither — the route is withdrawn until reconverged
sub_questions.explanation: With the static route gone, EIGRP (AD=90) beats OSPF (AD=110). EIGRP has the lower AD so its route is installed.

sub_questions.question: Administrative distance is used to choose between routes from different routing sources; metric is used to compare routes from the same source.
sub_questions.explanation: AD is the inter-protocol tiebreaker (which source to trust). Metric is the intra-protocol tiebreaker (which path within OSPF, EIGRP, etc. is best). A router only compares metrics among routes from the same protocol.

questions.scenario_text: A small-office router (R-Edge) connects 30 private hosts using 10.0.0.0/24 addressing to the internet through a single public IP address (203.0.113.1) assigned to its WAN interface.

sub_questions.question: Which NAT type allows all 30 private hosts to share one public IP address simultaneously?
sub_questions.explanation: PAT (Port Address Translation), also called NAT overload, maps multiple inside private addresses to a single outside public IP by using unique source port numbers to track sessions.

sub_questions.question: Which Cisco IOS command marks the inside interface for NAT translation?
sub_questions.explanation: 'ip nat inside' is applied to the LAN-facing interface. 'ip nat outside' is applied to the WAN/internet-facing interface. NAT translates traffic flowing between these two designated interfaces.

sub_questions.question: With PAT, the router differentiates multiple inside hosts sharing a single public IP by tracking unique TCP/UDP source port numbers for each session.
sub_questions.explanation: PAT keeps a translation table mapping {inside-private-IP:port} to {public-IP:unique-port}. Port numbers allow thousands of simultaneous sessions from many hosts on one public IP.

questions.scenario_text: A Cisco router is acting as a DHCP server for subnet 192.168.50.0/24. The default gateway is 192.168.50.1, DNS is 8.8.8.8. Addresses .1–.10 must be reserved for static assignments and excluded from the DHCP pool.

sub_questions.question: Which Cisco IOS command excludes addresses .1 through .10 from DHCP allocation?
sub_questions.explanation: The correct syntax is 'ip dhcp excluded-address <low> <high>' entered in global configuration mode, before or after creating the pool. It prevents those addresses from being leased.

sub_questions.question: Hosts on a different subnet need DHCP addresses from this router. Which feature must be configured on the router interface facing those hosts?
options: ip helper-address pointing to the DHCP server
sub_questions.explanation: 'ip helper-address <server-IP>' converts broadcast DHCP DISCOVER messages from the remote subnet into unicast packets forwarded to the DHCP server, enabling cross-subnet DHCP.

sub_questions.question: A DHCP DISCOVER message is sent as a broadcast (255.255.255.255) because the client has no IP address yet.
sub_questions.explanation: The DHCP exchange starts with DISCOVER (broadcast) → OFFER (unicast) → REQUEST (broadcast) → ACK (unicast). The client broadcasts DISCOVER because it has no IP address or server address yet.

questions.scenario_text: Two routers (R1 with HSRP priority 110, R2 with priority 100) are configured for HSRP group 1 on the 192.168.1.0/24 LAN. The virtual gateway IP is 192.168.1.254. R1 is currently the active router.

sub_questions.question: If R1's LAN interface fails, what happens to the virtual IP 192.168.1.254?
options: All hosts lose their default gateway until R1 recovers
R2 becomes the active router and takes over the virtual IP
R2 assumes the virtual IP using a new MAC address assigned to its physical interface
The virtual IP is deactivated and hosts must reconfigure
sub_questions.explanation: HSRP provides gateway redundancy. When the active router (R1) fails, the standby router (R2) transitions to active and begins responding to the virtual IP/MAC — transparent to hosts.

sub_questions.question: How does the HSRP standby router detect that the active router has failed?
options: The standby router stops receiving HSRP HELLO messages within the dead interval
Gratuitous ARP sent by R1 stops arriving
sub_questions.explanation: HSRP routers send HELLO messages every 3 seconds by default. If the standby router doesn't receive a HELLO within the hold time (10 seconds by default), it assumes the active is down and takes over.

sub_questions.question: HSRP is Cisco-proprietary; VRRP (Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol) is the standards-based equivalent defined in RFC 5798.
sub_questions.explanation: HSRP is proprietary to Cisco. VRRP (RFC 5798) achieves the same purpose and is supported by multiple vendors. GLBP is another Cisco-proprietary FHRP that adds load balancing.

questions.scenario_text: A company's 10-Mbps WAN link carries a mix of traffic: VoIP (2 Mbps, latency-sensitive), video conferencing (3 Mbps), and best-effort data (5 Mbps). The network team is implementing QoS to guarantee service levels for real-time traffic.

sub_questions.question: Which QoS queuing mechanism classifies traffic by DSCP markings and guarantees minimum bandwidth per class?
sub_questions.explanation: CBWFQ assigns minimum bandwidth guarantees to each traffic class. It often pairs with LLQ (Low Latency Queuing) to add a strict priority queue for real-time traffic like VoIP.

sub_questions.question: Which DSCP decimal value marks VoIP bearer (RTP) traffic for Expedited Forwarding (EF) treatment?
sub_questions.explanation: DSCP EF = binary 101110 = decimal 46. Expedited Forwarding is the per-hop behaviour for VoIP. It provides a low-loss, low-latency, low-jitter forwarding path.

sub_questions.question: Traffic shaping buffers and delays excess traffic to conform to a rate, whereas traffic policing drops or re-marks packets that exceed the configured rate immediately.
sub_questions.explanation: Shaping uses a buffer/queue — excess traffic waits. Policing enforces a hard limit — excess is dropped or re-marked (to a lower DSCP). Shaping causes delay; policing causes drops.

questions.scenario_text: A network engineer notices routers in the enterprise have clock skew of several hours, causing HTTPS certificate validation failures and AAA authentication errors. She decides to deploy NTP to synchronise all devices.

sub_questions.question: Which IOS command configures a router to synchronise its clock from NTP server 192.168.1.1?
sub_questions.explanation: The global configuration command 'ntp server <ip>' designates an NTP server for the device to synchronise with. No 'ip' prefix is needed — it is simply 'ntp server'.

sub_questions.question: What NTP stratum number is assigned to a device directly synchronised from a stratum 1 server?
sub_questions.explanation: Stratum 0 = reference clock (atomic/GPS). Stratum 1 = directly connected to stratum 0. Each hop increases stratum by 1. A router syncing from a stratum 1 server is stratum 2. Stratum 16 = unsynchronised/unreachable.

sub_questions.question: A DHCP server can distribute the DNS server IP address to clients using the 'dns-server' option in the DHCP pool configuration.
sub_questions.explanation: DHCP option 6 (DNS server) is configured with 'dns-server <ip>' inside a Cisco IOS DHCP pool. Clients receive the DNS server address along with their IP, mask, and default gateway.

questions.scenario_text: A security engineer needs to block all traffic from the 192.168.10.0/24 network from reaching a finance server at 10.0.0.100, while allowing all other traffic to pass. She decides to use a standard ACL.

sub_questions.question: Standard ACLs filter traffic based on which criterion only?
sub_questions.explanation: Standard IP ACLs (numbered 1–99, 1300–1999) filter only on source IP address. Extended ACLs (100–199, 2000–2699) can filter on source, destination, protocol, and ports.

sub_questions.question: Where should a standard ACL be placed for most efficient filtering (to minimise unwanted traffic traversal)?
options: As close to the source as possible
As close to the destination as possible
sub_questions.explanation: Standard ACLs can only match source IP, so placing them close to the source would block all traffic from that source — including traffic to other legitimate destinations. Place standard ACLs close to the destination.

sub_questions.question: Every Cisco ACL has an implicit 'deny any' statement at the end that drops any traffic not matched by an earlier permit.
sub_questions.explanation: The implicit deny-all at the end of every ACL means that if no ACE matches a packet, it is dropped. A common mistake is forgetting this — if an ACL only has deny entries, all other traffic is also dropped.

questions.scenario_text: A security policy requires that only HTTPS traffic (TCP port 443) from the 172.16.0.0/16 network be permitted to reach web server 10.1.1.10. All other traffic destined for that server must be denied.

sub_questions.question: Which extended ACL entry correctly permits only HTTPS from 172.16.0.0/16 to host 10.1.1.10?
sub_questions.explanation: Extended ACL syntax: permit/deny protocol source-IP wildcard destination-IP wildcard [operator port]. Wildcard for /16 = 0.0.255.255. 'host' = single IP (0.0.0.0 wildcard). Option D uses a subnet mask, not a wildcard mask — incorrect.

sub_questions.question: An extended ACL should be placed as close to the ______ as possible.
sub_questions.explanation: Extended ACLs can match on both source and destination, so placing them close to the source prevents unwanted traffic from crossing the network — conserving bandwidth.

sub_questions.question: An extended ACL can simultaneously filter on source IP, destination IP, Layer 4 protocol (TCP/UDP), and port number.
sub_questions.explanation: That is the key advantage of extended over standard ACLs. Extended ACLs provide granular control — e.g., 'permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 any eq 80' allows HTTP from a specific subnet to any destination.

questions.scenario_text: A switch port Fa0/5 connects to a user workstation. The security policy allows a maximum of two MAC addresses on this port; any third MAC address should cause the port to shut down immediately.

sub_questions.question: Which port security violation mode shuts the port down on detecting a security violation AND increments the violation counter?
sub_questions.explanation: Shutdown mode: port goes to err-disabled state, LED turns amber, violation counter increments, syslog message generated. Protect: drops frames silently, no counter. Restrict: drops frames + syslog + counter, port stays up.

sub_questions.question: Which IOS command, applied to interface Fa0/5, sets the maximum number of secure MAC addresses to 2?
sub_questions.explanation: The correct syntax is 'switchport port-security maximum <count>' at the interface configuration prompt. The interface must also have 'switchport port-security' enabled and be an access port.

sub_questions.question: The 'sticky' keyword causes a switch to dynamically learn and save secure MAC addresses to the running-config, making them survive reloads if saved.
sub_questions.explanation: 'switchport port-security mac-address sticky' dynamically learns MACs and writes them as 'switchport port-security mac-address sticky <mac>' in the running-config. A 'copy run start' makes them persistent across reboots.

questions.scenario_text: A company uses a centralised authentication server for all network device logins. The security team is evaluating TACACS+ versus RADIUS to decide which protocol suits their IOS router management requirements.

sub_questions.question: Which protocol encrypts only the password field in its authentication packets, while the other encrypts the entire payload?
options: RADIUS encrypts only the password; TACACS+ encrypts the full payload
TACACS+ encrypts only the password; RADIUS encrypts the full payload
sub_questions.explanation: RADIUS (RFC 2865) encrypts only the password using a shared secret + MD5. TACACS+ (Cisco) encrypts the entire body of every packet, offering stronger confidentiality for the full AAA exchange.

sub_questions.question: In the AAA framework, which function verifies that a user is who they claim to be (identity verification)?
sub_questions.explanation: Authentication = Who are you? (identity verification via username/password, certificates, tokens). Authorisation = What can you do? (permissions). Accounting = What did you do? (logging/auditing).

sub_questions.question: TACACS+ uses TCP port 49, while RADIUS uses UDP ports 1812 (authentication/authorisation) and 1813 (accounting).
sub_questions.explanation: TACACS+ uses TCP 49 — TCP's reliability suits device management. RADIUS originally used UDP 1645/1646; RFC 2865/2866 standardised UDP 1812/1813. The port distinction is a common exam topic.

questions.scenario_text: A company uses IPsec VPN between its headquarters (HQ) and a branch office over the public internet. The security team must choose between IPsec transport mode and tunnel mode, and understand the IKE negotiation phases.

sub_questions.question: Which IPsec mode encapsulates the entire original IP packet — header and payload — inside a new IP packet?
sub_questions.explanation: Tunnel mode wraps the entire original IP packet (including header) in a new IP packet with new source/destination IPs. This hides the original addresses, making it ideal for site-to-site VPNs. Transport mode only encapsulates the payload.

sub_questions.question: Which IKE phase negotiates the IPsec SAs (Security Associations) that protect the actual data traffic?
sub_questions.explanation: IKE Phase 1 establishes the ISAKMP SA — a secure, authenticated channel between peers. IKE Phase 2 (Quick mode) uses that channel to negotiate the IPsec SAs (AH/ESP) that actually protect user data.

sub_questions.question: IPsec ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) provides both data confidentiality (encryption) and data integrity (authentication).
sub_questions.explanation: ESP provides encryption (confidentiality), integrity (HMAC), anti-replay protection, and optional authentication. AH provides only integrity and authentication (no encryption). ESP is the more commonly used IPsec protocol.

questions.scenario_text: A network engineer uses a REST API exposed by a network controller to query device inventory. The API endpoint is: GET /api/v1/devices/{deviceId}/status. She uses a Python script with the 'requests' library to interact with it.

sub_questions.question: Which HTTP method is used to retrieve (read) data from a REST API without modifying server state?
sub_questions.explanation: REST CRUD mapping: GET=Read, POST=Create, PUT=Update/Replace, PATCH=Partial Update, DELETE=Remove. GET is idempotent and side-effect free; it retrieves the resource representation.

sub_questions.question: Which HTTP status code indicates the request was processed successfully?
sub_questions.explanation: 200 OK = success. 201 Created (POST success). 400 Bad Request. 401 Unauthorised. 403 Forbidden. 404 Not Found. 500 Server Error. Knowing 2xx=success, 4xx=client error, 5xx=server error is sufficient for CCNA.

sub_questions.question: REST APIs are stateless — each request must contain all necessary information; the server does not retain client session state between requests.
sub_questions.explanation: Statelessness is a core REST constraint. Each API call is independent. Credentials (API key, OAuth token) must be included in every request. This simplifies server scaling and caching.

questions.scenario_text: A network team needs to automate NTP configuration across 200 routers. They are evaluating Ansible versus a manual CLI approach and need to understand how Ansible operates in an agentless model.

sub_questions.question: Which Ansible component lists the managed devices and their logical groupings (e.g., routers, switches, by site)?
sub_questions.explanation: The Ansible inventory (hosts file or dynamic inventory script) defines managed hosts and groups. Playbooks reference these groups to target specific devices.

sub_questions.question: What is the key characteristic that differentiates Ansible from configuration management tools like Puppet and Chef?
options: Ansible requires an agent running on every managed device
Ansible is agentless — it connects via SSH (or API) to push configurations
Ansible requires a dedicated agent server per site
sub_questions.explanation: Ansible is agentless: no software needs to be pre-installed on managed nodes. It connects via SSH (network devices via netconf/ssh or network modules). Puppet and Chef use agents that pull configs from a central server.

sub_questions.question: Ansible playbooks are written in YAML (YAML Ain't Markup Language) format.
sub_questions.explanation: Ansible uses YAML for playbooks, inventory files, and variable files. YAML uses indentation-based structure with key-value pairs — human-readable and widely supported by automation tools.

questions.scenario_text: A network operator uses NETCONF over SSH to programmatically configure and query interface IP addresses on a Cisco IOS-XE router. YANG models define the structure of the configuration data.

sub_questions.question: On which TCP port does NETCONF operate by default over SSH?
sub_questions.explanation: NETCONF (RFC 6242) runs over SSH on port 830. Standard SSH is port 22. The dedicated port 830 avoids conflicts with interactive SSH sessions and allows firewall rules to be written specifically for NETCONF.

sub_questions.question: YANG is a data modelling language used to describe network configuration schemas. What does YANG stand for?
options: YANG is not actually an acronym
sub_questions.explanation: YANG = Yet Another Next-Generation. It defines the structure, syntax, and semantics of configuration and state data used by NETCONF and RESTCONF.

sub_questions.question: NETCONF encodes its data in XML, while RESTCONF uses JSON or XML transported over HTTP/HTTPS.
sub_questions.explanation: NETCONF (RFC 6241) always uses XML encoding. RESTCONF (RFC 8040) is HTTP-based and supports both JSON and XML. RESTCONF is simpler for REST-familiar developers; NETCONF offers more advanced transaction capabilities.

questions.scenario_text: A company is implementing Software-Defined Networking (SDN) with a centralised controller managing 50 switches. The controller separates the control plane from the data plane and exposes APIs to orchestration systems.

sub_questions.question: In SDN architecture, which plane is responsible for making decisions about where traffic should be forwarded?
sub_questions.explanation: The control plane decides HOW to forward (routing protocols, STP, etc.). The data plane (forwarding plane) actually moves packets based on those decisions. SDN centralises the control plane in a controller.

sub_questions.question: Which SDN API type allows the controller to communicate downward to network devices to programme their forwarding tables?
sub_questions.explanation: Southbound APIs (e.g., OpenFlow, NETCONF, RESTCONF) connect the controller to network devices. Northbound APIs connect the controller to applications and orchestration systems (e.g., REST API).

sub_questions.question: OpenFlow is an example of a southbound API protocol that an SDN controller uses to programme flow tables on forwarding devices.
sub_questions.explanation: OpenFlow was one of the first standardised southbound protocols. It allows an SDN controller to install flow entries directly into switch flow tables, enabling centralised traffic engineering.

questions.scenario_text: A network automation script exchanges data with a REST API that returns device information in JSON format. The team also receives some vendor data in XML and uses Ansible playbooks in YAML. The engineer must understand all three formats.

sub_questions.question: Which JSON structure uses key-value pairs enclosed in curly braces { }?
sub_questions.explanation: JSON has two container types: Object { } = key-value pairs (like a dictionary), Array [ ] = ordered list of values. A JSON response body is typically an object at the root level.

sub_questions.question: Which data serialisation format uses indentation and colons to define hierarchy and is natively used in Ansible playbooks?
sub_questions.explanation: YAML uses indentation (spaces) for hierarchy and 'key: value' pairs. It is the native format for Ansible playbooks and inventory files because it is human-readable and concise.

sub_questions.question: XML uses opening and closing tags (e.g., <hostname>R1</hostname>) to encode structured data, while JSON uses curly braces and square brackets.
sub_questions.explanation: XML is tag-based (verbose but self-describing). JSON is brace/bracket-based (more compact). Both are used in network automation APIs; NETCONF uses XML, REST APIs commonly use JSON.

questions.question: Which command activates the AAA framework on an IOS device?
questions.explanation: aaa new-model is required before any other aaa commands. It activates the AAA subsystem and overrides the default line-based authentication.

questions.question: What does AAA stand for?
questions.explanation: AAA = Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (what can you do?), Accounting (what did you do?).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the difference between TACACS+ and RADIUS in terms of AAA separation?
options: RADIUS separates authentication and authorization; TACACS+ combines them
TACACS+ separates authentication, authorization, and accounting into distinct services; RADIUS combines authentication and authorization
Both protocols separate all three AAA services identically
TACACS+ uses UDP and combines AAA; RADIUS uses TCP and separates them
questions.explanation: TACACS+ separates AAA into separate control packets, allowing granular per-command authorization. RADIUS combines authentication and authorization in a single Access-Accept response.

questions.question: Which RADIUS ports are used for authentication and accounting respectively?
options: Both B and C are valid
questions.explanation: RADIUS originally used UDP 1645 (auth) and 1646 (accounting). RFC 2865/2866 updated these to 1812 and 1813. Both sets are still used in practice.

questions.question: In TACACS+, which portion of the packet is encrypted?
options: Nothing — TACACS+ sends data in cleartext
questions.explanation: TACACS+ encrypts the entire packet body using a shared secret, making it more secure than RADIUS which only encrypts the password attribute.

questions.question: What is the implicit rule at the end of every Cisco ACL?
questions.explanation: Every ACL ends with an implicit 'deny any any'. If no ACE matches, traffic is dropped. Always add 'permit ip any any' at the end if you want unmatched traffic to pass.

questions.question: Standard IP ACLs filter traffic based on which criteria?
options: Source IP, destination IP, and protocol
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs (1-99, 1300-1999) filter on source IP address only. Use extended ACLs for more granular matching.

questions.question: Which wildcard mask would match exactly the host 10.1.1.5?
questions.explanation: A wildcard mask of 0.0.0.0 means all bits must match — equivalent to using the 'host' keyword. 'permit 10.1.1.5 0.0.0.0' matches only that single host.

questions.question: Which command applies an ACL named BLOCK-TELNET inbound on an interface?
questions.explanation: ip access-group <name or number> in|out applies an ACL to an interface. ip access-list is used to create/enter the ACL definition.

questions.question: An ACL on a router interface set to 'in' filters traffic that is:
options: Leaving the router on that interface toward the network
Entering the router on that interface from the network
Transiting through the router between two interfaces
Generated by the router itself (management traffic)
questions.explanation: 'in' on an interface filters traffic arriving on that interface before routing. 'out' filters traffic after the routing decision, as it exits that interface.

questions.question: Which extended ACL entry correctly denies Telnet from any source to host 10.0.0.1?
options: deny tcp any host 10.0.0.1 eq telnet
Both A and C are syntactically valid
questions.explanation: Both 'eq telnet' and 'eq 23' are valid IOS syntax. 'host 10.0.0.1' is shorthand for '10.0.0.1 0.0.0.0'. Both forms match correctly.

questions.question: Which type of malware self-replicates by attaching to legitimate programs?
questions.explanation: A virus attaches to a host program and requires user action to spread. A worm is self-propagating and does not need a host file. A Trojan disguises itself as legitimate software.

questions.question: What is a phishing attack?
options: Flooding a network with ICMP packets
Exploiting a buffer overflow in an application
Using deceptive emails/websites to trick users into revealing credentials
Injecting malicious SQL queries into a database
questions.explanation: Phishing uses social engineering — fake emails or websites that mimic trusted entities — to steal credentials, credit card numbers, or install malware.

questions.question: A DoS attack that uses multiple compromised systems to send traffic is called:
questions.explanation: A DDoS (Distributed DoS) uses a botnet — many compromised hosts (zombies) — to overwhelm a target simultaneously, making it much harder to mitigate than a single-source DoS.

questions.question: What is a man-in-the-middle (MitM) attack?
options: An attacker physically intercepts cables between two hosts
An attacker secretly intercepts and possibly alters communications between two parties who believe they are communicating directly
An attacker floods a switch to overflow the MAC table
An attacker sends forged ARP replies to redirect traffic
questions.explanation: MitM attacks intercept communications. ARP poisoning is one technique to achieve MitM on a LAN. Both parties think they are communicating directly but traffic flows through the attacker.

questions.question: Which attack exploits the TCP three-way handshake by sending SYN packets with spoofed source IPs, filling the server's half-open connection queue?
questions.explanation: SYN flood attacks send many SYN packets with fake source IPs. The server allocates state (SYN-RECEIVED) but never gets ACK back. The backlog fills, preventing legitimate connections. Mitigated with SYN cookies.

questions.question: An attacker sends a gratuitous ARP reply claiming their MAC address is associated with the default gateway's IP. What is this attack and what does it enable?
options: MAC Flooding — enables CAM table overflow
ARP Poisoning/Spoofing — enables MitM by redirecting LAN traffic through attacker
IP Spoofing — bypasses ACL source filters
questions.explanation: ARP Poisoning sends fake ARP replies to poison ARP caches. Hosts update their ARP table associating the gateway IP with the attacker's MAC, redirecting all traffic through them (MitM). Mitigated with Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI).

questions.question: Which command generates the RSA key pair required for SSH on an IOS router?
questions.explanation: crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048 generates the RSA key pair. A domain name must be configured first with 'ip domain-name'. Minimum recommended modulus is 2048 for SSH v2.

questions.question: Which command prevents users from logging in after 3 failed attempts within 60 seconds for 120 seconds?
questions.explanation: login block-for <seconds> attempts <count> within <seconds> enables the IOS login quiet mode, blocking all login attempts for the specified duration after the threshold is exceeded.

questions.question: What does the 'no cdp run' command accomplish from a security perspective?
options: Disables routing updates on external interfaces
Prevents attackers from discovering device models, IOS versions, and IP addresses via CDP
Stops LLDP from advertising on trunk ports
Disables the CDP database to save memory
questions.explanation: CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) broadcasts device details including IOS version, platform, and IP addresses. Disabling it on external/untrusted interfaces prevents information disclosure to potential attackers.

questions.question: Which banner is displayed BEFORE a login prompt and is legally important for warning unauthorized users?
questions.explanation: banner login displays before the login prompt. banner motd (message of the day) displays before the login banner. Both should contain a legal warning. banner exec displays after successful login.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'exec-timeout 5 0' configured on VTY lines?
options: Allow sessions to stay active for 5 hours
Disconnect idle VTY sessions after 5 minutes
Set the maximum login retries to 5
Allow up to 5 concurrent SSH sessions
questions.explanation: exec-timeout <minutes> <seconds> disconnects idle sessions. 'exec-timeout 5 0' = 5 minutes, 0 seconds. 'exec-timeout 0 0' = never timeout (security risk — avoid in production).

questions.question: Which IPsec protocol provides both encryption AND authentication?
questions.explanation: ESP (protocol 50) provides confidentiality (encryption), integrity, and authentication. AH (protocol 51) provides integrity and authentication ONLY — no encryption. GRE is a tunneling protocol, not a security protocol.

questions.question: What is the purpose of IKE Phase 1 in an IPsec VPN?
options: Encrypts the data tunnel between peers
Establishes a secure management channel (ISAKMP SA) for negotiating IPsec parameters
Defines the encryption algorithm for user traffic
Performs NAT traversal for the data plane
questions.explanation: IKE Phase 1 establishes the ISAKMP SA — a secure, authenticated channel used to negotiate Phase 2. It uses Diffie-Hellman for key exchange. Phase 2 (Quick Mode) establishes the actual IPsec SAs for data traffic.

questions.question: Which Diffie-Hellman group provides the strongest security for IKE key exchange?
questions.explanation: Higher DH group numbers = larger key size = stronger security. DH Group 14 (2048-bit) is the minimum recommended for modern deployments. Groups 1 and 2 are considered weak. ECDH groups (19, 20, 21) are even stronger.

questions.question: What does 'switchport port-security maximum 2' configure?
options: Allows only 2 VLANs on the port
Limits the port to a maximum of 2 MAC addresses
Sets the port to err-disable after 2 violations
Allows 2 concurrent logins to the switch
questions.explanation: switchport port-security maximum <N> sets the maximum number of MAC addresses the switch will learn and allow on that port. Default is 1.

questions.question: Which port security violation mode drops violating frames silently without sending a syslog or SNMP trap?
questions.explanation: protect silently drops frames from unknown MACs. restrict drops frames AND increments a violation counter/sends a syslog. shutdown (default) puts the port in err-disable state.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'switchport port-security mac-address sticky' command?
options: Manually configures a static MAC address for the port
Dynamically learns MAC addresses and converts them to static entries in the running config
Enables port security on a trunk port
Configures the port to only accept frames with the sticky bit set in Ethernet headers
questions.explanation: Sticky port security learns MAC addresses dynamically and saves them as secure static entries in the running-config. On next startup (after 'copy run start'), they persist and no new MACs are accepted.

questions.question: Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) uses which table to validate ARP packets on untrusted ports?
options: The ARP cache of the switch
questions.explanation: DAI uses the DHCP Snooping Binding Table (which maps IP → MAC → port → VLAN) to validate ARP replies. If the ARP sender's IP/MAC doesn't match the binding table, the packet is dropped.

questions.question: In Zone-Based Firewall, what must be configured to allow traffic between two zones?
options: An interface ACL on the source zone interface
A zone-pair with a service-policy referencing a policy-map with inspect or pass actions
An ISAKMP policy for the zone pair
questions.explanation: ZBF requires: zones → assign interfaces → class-map (what traffic) → policy-map (what action: inspect/pass/drop) → zone-pair (direction) → attach service-policy to zone-pair.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'inspect' and 'pass' actions in a ZBF policy-map?
options: inspect logs traffic; pass encrypts it
inspect performs stateful inspection (automatically permits return traffic); pass allows traffic in one direction only
pass performs stateful inspection; inspect only logs packets
questions.explanation: inspect creates a stateful session entry — return traffic is automatically permitted. pass allows traffic but does NOT create state — you would need a reverse zone-pair to permit return traffic.

questions.question: What is the CIA Triad in information security?
questions.explanation: CIA Triad: Confidentiality (only authorized access), Integrity (data not altered in transit), Availability (systems accessible when needed). The foundation of information security policy.

questions.question: Which type of firewall operates at Layer 7 and can inspect HTTP content, block specific URLs, and detect application signatures?
questions.explanation: NGFW (Next-Gen Firewalls) like Cisco Firepower operate at Layer 7 with deep packet inspection (DPI), application identification, URL filtering, and IPS capabilities — far beyond stateful packet inspection.

questions.question: Which wireless security protocol is considered most secure for enterprise networks?
questions.explanation: WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X with a RADIUS server for per-user authentication. WPA2-Personal uses a shared pre-shared key (PSK) — if compromised, all devices are vulnerable. WEP and WPA are deprecated.

questions.question: What is a rogue access point?
options: An AP configured with the wrong SSID
An unauthorized AP connected to the corporate network that may allow unauthorized access
An AP that only broadcasts on 5GHz
questions.explanation: A rogue AP is an unauthorized AP connected to the corporate wired network. It may be set up by an employee for convenience or by an attacker. Detected using wireless intrusion detection systems (WIDS) or WLC scanning.

questions.question: An attacker creates an AP with the same SSID as the corporate network to intercept traffic. This is called:
questions.explanation: An Evil Twin is a rogue AP that mimics a legitimate SSID to lure clients. Clients connect thinking it's legitimate; attacker performs MitM. Different from a passive rogue AP — this is actively adversarial.

questions.question: What is a false positive in IDS/IPS terminology?
options: A real attack that was missed by the IDS
A legitimate action incorrectly flagged as malicious
A blocked attack that was successfully stopped
An attack that bypassed the IPS signature database
questions.explanation: False positive = legitimate traffic incorrectly identified as malicious. False negative = real attack that wasn't detected. High false positives cause alert fatigue; high false negatives mean attacks slip through.

questions.question: An IPS is deployed inline in the traffic path. An IDS is typically deployed:
options: Inline as well — there is no difference
Out-of-band, connected to a SPAN/mirror port
Between the router and the ISP
questions.explanation: IDS is typically passive/out-of-band, receiving a copy of traffic via a SPAN port or network tap. It can detect but cannot block. IPS is inline — traffic must pass through it, allowing active blocking.

questions.question: Which type of encryption uses the same key for both encryption and decryption?
questions.explanation: Symmetric encryption (AES, 3DES) uses one shared key. It is fast but requires secure key distribution. Asymmetric encryption (RSA) uses a public/private key pair — slower but solves key distribution.

questions.question: What is the purpose of hashing in network security?
options: To verify data integrity — a hash changes if data is altered
questions.explanation: Hashing (MD5, SHA-256) produces a fixed-size digest from input data. The same input always produces the same hash. Any change to the data produces a completely different hash — used to verify integrity (not confidentiality).

questions.question: Which algorithm is used in the Diffie-Hellman key exchange to allow two parties to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel?
options: RSA encryption of the symmetric key
Discrete logarithm / modular exponentiation — DH establishes a shared secret without transmitting it
AES key wrapping with a certificate
MD5 hash of both parties' public keys
questions.explanation: DH uses modular exponentiation: each party generates a private value, computes a public value, exchanges public values, and independently computes the same shared secret. The secret is never transmitted — only the public values are.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) in network security?
options: A zone for military network traffic only
A network segment between the internet and the internal network hosting public-facing services
A backup network for disaster recovery
A high-security zone for executive workstations
questions.explanation: A DMZ hosts public-facing servers (web, email, DNS). It sits between the internet and the trusted internal network. Compromised DMZ servers cannot directly reach the internal network.

questions.question: What is the principle of least privilege in network security?
options: Users should have the minimum access rights needed to perform their job function
All users should have equal access to all network resources
Privileged accounts should only be used from physically secure locations
Network devices should run with the minimum IOS feature set
questions.explanation: Least privilege limits access to only what is needed. If an account is compromised, the blast radius is minimized. Applied to users (RBAC), services (run as non-root), and network devices (privilege levels).

questions.question: Which Cisco feature sends a syslog-level security alert when an SSH login attempt fails, helping identify brute-force attacks?
questions.explanation: login on-failure log generates a syslog message when login attempts fail. Combined with login block-for, this provides detection and prevention against brute-force SSH attacks.

questions.question: What is defense in depth?
options: Using the deepest firewall ruleset possible
Layering multiple security controls so that if one fails, others still protect the asset
Placing all security controls at the network perimeter
questions.explanation: Defense in depth uses multiple layers (perimeter firewall, IPS, endpoint AV, ACLs, encryption, AAA) so a single failure doesn't compromise the entire system. Also called 'layered security'.

questions.question: A vulnerability scanner actively attempts to exploit vulnerabilities, while a penetration test only identifies them without exploitation.
questions.explanation: It is the opposite: a vulnerability scanner identifies potential vulnerabilities without exploiting them. A penetration test goes further and actively attempts exploitation to confirm the vulnerability is real and assess its impact.

questions.question: What type of attack involves sending crafted packets with the source IP set to the destination IP, causing a device to send traffic to itself?
questions.explanation: A Land Attack sends a TCP SYN with source IP = destination IP (the target). The target sends SYN-ACK to itself, creating a loop that consumes resources. Modern OSes and firewalls drop such packets.

questions.question: Which access control model assigns permissions based on a user's role within an organization?
questions.explanation: RBAC assigns permissions to roles (e.g., 'network-admin', 'read-only-user'), then assigns users to roles. Simpler to manage at scale than individual user permissions. Used in IOS with 'privilege levels' and 'parser views'.

questions.question: Why should NTP authentication be configured in production networks?
options: To encrypt NTP traffic between peers
To prevent an attacker from providing false time information, which could invalidate certificates and log correlation
To compress NTP packets for efficiency
To allow NTP to traverse NAT boundaries
questions.explanation: NTP authentication (MD5 keys) ensures NTP updates come from trusted sources. Time manipulation can invalidate TLS certificates, confuse syslog correlation, and disrupt Kerberos authentication (which has a 5-minute tolerance).

questions.question: What is SNMP community string security weakness in SNMPv1/v2c?
options: Community strings are encrypted with DES but can be brute-forced
Community strings are sent in cleartext and act as passwords — anyone who can sniff the network can obtain them
SNMPv2c does not support read-only communities
Community strings expire every 24 hours automatically
questions.explanation: SNMPv1/v2c community strings are sent in cleartext UDP packets. An attacker sniffing the network can capture both read (public) and read-write (private) community strings. SNMPv3 provides authentication (HMAC) and encryption (AES).

questions.question: Which of the following are characteristics of TACACS+ compared to RADIUS? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: TACACS+ uses TCP (port 49), encrypts the entire packet payload, and separates AAA services. RADIUS uses UDP (1812/1813) and only encrypts the password. RADIUS combines authentication and authorization.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid mitigation techniques for Layer 2 attacks? (Select all that apply)
options: DHCP Snooping — prevents rogue DHCP servers
Dynamic ARP Inspection — validates ARP against DHCP bindings
Port Security — limits MAC addresses per port
OSPF MD5 authentication — prevents routing loops
questions.explanation: DHCP Snooping, DAI, Port Security, and 802.1X are all Layer 2 security features. OSPF MD5 is a routing protocol security feature (Layer 3), not a Layer 2 attack mitigation.

questions.question: Which elements are required to enable SSH on an IOS device? (Select all that apply)
options: RSA key pair generated with crypto key generate rsa
username with a password configured (for local auth)
OSPF running for management plane routing
transport input ssh on VTY lines
questions.explanation: SSH requires: (1) ip domain-name for key generation, (2) RSA key pair, (3) a local user account or AAA for authentication, (4) transport input ssh on VTY lines. OSPF has nothing to do with SSH enablement.

questions.question: Order the steps to configure a basic Zone-Based Firewall (first to last):
questions.explanation: ZBF configuration order: zones → interfaces → class-maps → policy-maps → zone-pairs. Each step depends on the previous: zone-pairs reference policy-maps, which reference class-maps, applied to zones that have interfaces assigned.

questions.question:
Match each attack to its primary Layer 2 mitigation (order mitigations to match attacks):
Attacks: 1) Rogue DHCP server, 2) ARP spoofing, 3) Unauthorized device, 4) CAM table overflow
questions.explanation: DHCP Snooping blocks rogue DHCP servers. DAI validates ARP packets using DHCP bindings. 802.1X authenticates devices before network access. Port Security limits MAC addresses to prevent CAM table flooding.

questions.question: Order the IOS commands to configure SSH access (correct sequence):
questions.explanation: Hostname must be set first (used in RSA key name). Then domain-name (also used in key name). Then generate RSA keys. Then enforce SSHv2. Finally, restrict VTY to SSH only.

questions.question: Which cloud service model requires the customer to manage the operating system, middleware, and application?
questions.explanation: IaaS provides virtualised compute, storage, and networking. The customer manages everything from the OS up. PaaS adds OS and runtime management. SaaS provides the full application.

questions.question: A developer deploys code to Heroku without managing servers or the OS. This is which cloud model?
questions.explanation: PaaS (Platform as a Service) — the developer pushes code; the provider manages the runtime, OS, scaling, and patching. Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, and Azure App Service are PaaS examples.

questions.question: Which cloud deployment model places infrastructure on-premises for exclusive organisational use, managed either internally or by a third party?
questions.explanation: Private Cloud is dedicated to a single organisation. It can be on-premises (e.g., VMware vSphere) or hosted by a provider exclusively for that organisation. Provides maximum control and compliance suitability.

questions.question: In the Shared Responsibility Model, which component is ALWAYS the cloud provider's responsibility regardless of the service model?
options: Physical data centre security and hardware
questions.explanation: Physical security of data centres and underlying hardware is ALWAYS the provider's responsibility across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. The dividing line shifts upward depending on the service model.

questions.question: A company uses AWS for web servers but keeps its financial database on-premises connected via Direct Connect. What deployment model is this?
questions.explanation: Hybrid Cloud combines public cloud (AWS web servers) with private/on-premises infrastructure (financial DB), connected via dedicated connectivity (Direct Connect). The key is workloads running in both environments.

questions.question: What is 'cloud bursting' in a hybrid cloud model?
options: Moving all workloads from on-premises to the public cloud permanently
Automatically scaling into public cloud resources when private cloud capacity is exhausted
Distributing traffic globally across multiple cloud providers
Replicating data to a public cloud for disaster recovery only
questions.explanation: Cloud bursting: when on-prem/private cloud resources hit capacity, workloads automatically overflow ('burst') into the public cloud. The public cloud handles peak demand; non-sensitive workloads scale elastically.

questions.question: What is an Internet Gateway in AWS?
options: A managed VPN endpoint for site-to-site connectivity
A horizontally scalable gateway that enables internet access for resources in a VPC
A load balancer for distributing HTTP traffic
A DNS resolver for VPC resources
questions.explanation: An Internet Gateway (IGW) is attached to a VPC and enables internet communication. Public subnets have a route to the IGW. Without an IGW, VPC resources cannot reach the internet.

questions.question: In AWS, what is the difference between a public subnet and a private subnet?
options: Public subnets use IPv6; private subnets use IPv4
Public subnets have a route to an Internet Gateway; private subnets do not
Public subnets are in the us-east-1 region; private subnets are in all other regions
Public subnets allow all traffic; private subnets deny all traffic by default
questions.explanation: The defining characteristic: a public subnet has a route 0.0.0.0/0 pointing to an Internet Gateway in its route table. A private subnet has no such route — instances cannot be directly reached from the internet.

questions.question: How does AWS Direct Connect differ from a VPN Gateway?
options: Direct Connect is wireless; VPN Gateway uses fibre
Direct Connect provides a dedicated private physical connection to AWS; VPN Gateway creates an encrypted tunnel over the public internet
Direct Connect supports IPv6; VPN Gateway supports IPv4 only
They are functionally identical — only the name differs
questions.explanation: Direct Connect provides a dedicated, private, low-latency physical circuit from on-prem to AWS — bypassing the public internet. VPN Gateway creates an IPsec tunnel over the internet — cheaper but variable latency and shared bandwidth.

questions.question: A Network ACL in AWS is applied at which level and uses what matching model?
options: Subnet level, stateless (rules evaluated in order; both inbound and outbound must be explicitly allowed)
questions.explanation: NACLs apply at the subnet level and are stateless — like a traditional ACL. Rules are evaluated in numbered order. You must explicitly allow both the inbound request AND the outbound response (ephemeral ports).

questions.question: What is a hypervisor?
options: A physical server with extra CPU cores
Software that creates and manages virtual machines by abstracting physical hardware
A network device that virtualises switch ports
questions.explanation: A hypervisor (VMM — Virtual Machine Monitor) abstracts physical hardware and allocates it to VMs. Type 1 (bare-metal) runs directly on hardware (VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM). Type 2 runs on a host OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).

questions.question: What is the key difference between a virtual machine and a container?
options: VMs run on bare metal; containers require a hypervisor
Containers share the host OS kernel; VMs each have their own full OS
VMs are faster than containers; containers are slower
Containers support Windows only; VMs support Linux only
questions.explanation: Containers (Docker, Podman) share the host OS kernel — lightweight, fast startup. VMs include a full OS per instance — more isolated but heavier. Containers trade isolation for efficiency; VMs trade efficiency for stronger isolation.

questions.question: What does NFV (Network Functions Virtualisation) accomplish?
options: Separates the network control plane from the data plane
Runs network functions (firewall, load balancer, WAN optimiser) as software VMs on commodity hardware instead of dedicated appliances
Virtualises the physical switch fabric in a data centre
questions.explanation: NFV replaces dedicated network appliances (firewalls, IDS, load balancers) with software running on standard x86 servers. Reduces hardware costs and increases flexibility. SDN separates control/data planes — NFV virtualises the functions themselves.

questions.question: SDN (Software-Defined Networking) and NFV (Network Functions Virtualisation) are the same technology.
questions.explanation: SDN separates the network control plane from the data/forwarding plane — centralising routing decisions. NFV virtualises network functions (firewall, IDS) onto commodity hardware. They are complementary but distinct concepts.

questions.question: Which SD-WAN capability automatically routes real-time applications (VoIP) over the best-performing WAN path?
options: Application-aware routing with Quality of Experience (QoE) measurement
BGP path selection based on AS path length
questions.explanation: SD-WAN continuously measures each WAN path's latency, jitter, and packet loss. Application-aware routing places real-time traffic (voice, video) on the path meeting QoE thresholds, automatically failing over if quality degrades.

questions.question: What is ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access) and how does it differ from traditional VPN?
options: ZTNA encrypts all traffic; VPN does not
ZTNA grants access based on identity and device posture to specific apps only; VPN grants full network access after authentication
ZTNA uses TLS; VPN uses IPsec — security level is identical
ZTNA is cloud-only; VPN works on-premises only
questions.explanation: Traditional VPN: authenticate once, get full network access — lateral movement risk. ZTNA: every access request is verified (identity + device posture + context), and access is granted only to the specific application requested — not the entire network.

questions.question: A Secure Web Gateway (SWG) in a SASE architecture primarily provides:
options: URL filtering, malware scanning, and content inspection for outbound web traffic
Identity federation between cloud identity providers
BGP route filtering for internet-bound traffic
questions.explanation: SWG intercepts outbound web traffic and applies URL filtering (block malicious/inappropriate sites), malware scanning, and data loss prevention (DLP). In SASE, the SWG is cloud-delivered at the nearest PoP.

questions.question: Ansible uses which language for its playbooks?
questions.explanation: Ansible playbooks are written in YAML (Yet Another Markup Language) — human-readable, indentation-based syntax. The underlying engine is Python, but the playbooks themselves are YAML.

questions.question: What does 'idempotent' mean in the context of Ansible?
options: Running the playbook multiple times always produces the same result; changes only happen when needed
Ansible runs tasks in parallel on all hosts simultaneously
Ansible rolls back changes if a task fails
The playbook is encrypted before being sent to managed nodes
questions.explanation: Idempotency: running a playbook multiple times produces the same result. If the desired state already exists, no changes are made. This makes automation safe to run repeatedly without unintended side effects.

questions.question: Which Ansible component lists the managed nodes and groups them for targeting?
questions.explanation: The Ansible Inventory (hosts file or dynamic inventory script) defines managed nodes and organises them into groups. Playbooks target inventory groups or specific hosts.

questions.question: What is an Ansible module?
options: A YAML file defining a sequence of tasks
A reusable unit of code that performs a specific task (e.g., ios_config, apt, copy)
A variable file included in a playbook
questions.explanation: Ansible modules are the actual units of work — they perform specific tasks: ios_config (configure IOS devices), apt (manage packages), copy (copy files). Playbooks orchestrate which modules run and in what order.

questions.question: Puppet uses which configuration model, and what is its key difference from Ansible's execution model?
options: Puppet uses push model; Ansible uses pull model
Puppet uses declarative pull model (agents poll a Puppet Master); Ansible uses an imperative push model via SSH
Puppet and Ansible both use push model; only the language differs
Puppet uses SSH like Ansible but requires Ruby instead of Python
questions.explanation: Puppet: declarative (define desired state), pull model (agents on managed nodes check in with Puppet Master every 30 min and apply changes). Ansible: agentless, push model (controller SSHes to nodes and runs tasks imperatively on demand).

questions.question: Which HTTP method is used to retrieve data from a REST API without modifying it?
questions.explanation: GET retrieves data (read-only, idempotent). POST creates a new resource. PUT updates/replaces a resource. DELETE removes a resource. PATCH partially updates a resource.

questions.question: REST APIs most commonly use which data format for request and response bodies?
questions.explanation: While REST APIs can technically use any format, JSON is by far the most common for modern APIs due to its readability and lightweight nature. NETCONF uses XML; RESTCONF supports both XML and JSON.

questions.question: What HTTP status code range indicates a successful API response?
questions.explanation: 2xx = success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content). 4xx = client errors (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found). 5xx = server errors (500 Internal Server Error). 3xx = redirects.

questions.question: Which protocol uses YANG data models and SSH transport to configure network devices, and is considered the successor to SNMP for network management?
questions.explanation: NETCONF (RFC 6241) uses SSH (TCP port 830), XML-encoded data, and YANG data models for configuration management. It supports transactions with commit/rollback. RESTCONF is a REST-based alternative using HTTP/HTTPS.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a YANG model in network automation?
options: A scripting language for writing Ansible playbooks
A data modelling language that defines the structure and constraints of configuration and operational data for network devices
A transport protocol replacing SSH for NETCONF
A GUI framework for visualising network topology
questions.explanation: YANG (Yet Another Next Generation) is a data modelling language (RFC 6020/7950) that defines the schema of configuration and state data. NETCONF/RESTCONF use YANG models to validate and structure the XML/JSON data they exchange.

questions.question: In a northbound/southbound API architecture (SDN), which boundary do applications use to communicate with the SDN controller?
options: Northbound API (e.g., REST API, Python SDK)
questions.explanation: Northbound APIs: between SDN controller and applications/orchestrators (REST, Python SDK). Southbound APIs: between SDN controller and network devices (OpenFlow, NETCONF, gRPC). East/Westbound: between controllers.

questions.question: What does SDN separate in traditional networking?
options: The control plane (routing decisions) from the data plane (packet forwarding)
questions.explanation: SDN decouples the control plane (decides where traffic goes — traditionally in each device's CPU) from the data plane (actually forwards packets — ASICs). A centralised SDN controller manages control plane logic for all devices.

questions.question: Cisco's DNA Center (now Catalyst Center) uses which southbound protocol to program network devices?
questions.explanation: Cisco DNA Center uses NETCONF/YANG for southbound communication with IOS-XE devices. It also supports RESTCONF and CLI via SSH. OpenFlow is more common with open-source SDN controllers like OpenDaylight.

questions.question: In a traditional distributed network, what is the control plane responsible for?
options: Switching frames at line rate using ASICs
Building and maintaining the routing/forwarding table (RIB/FIB) using routing protocols
questions.explanation: Control plane: runs routing protocols (OSPF, BGP), builds the Routing Information Base (RIB), installs the best routes into the Forwarding Information Base (FIB). Data plane: uses the FIB to forward packets in hardware at line rate.

questions.question: What does 'declarative' mean in the context of Infrastructure as Code tools like Terraform?
options: You write step-by-step instructions for how to build the infrastructure
You describe the desired end state and the tool figures out how to achieve it
Infrastructure is declared in a programming language like Python
Each change must be manually approved before applying
questions.explanation: Declarative IaC: define what you want (e.g., '3 EC2 instances, 1 VPC') and the tool determines HOW to make it happen. Terraform computes the difference between current state and desired state, then makes only the necessary changes.

questions.question: Which command in Terraform shows what changes will be made WITHOUT applying them?
questions.explanation: terraform plan shows a dry-run: what resources will be created, modified, or destroyed. terraform apply executes the changes. terraform validate checks syntax. This workflow: init → validate → plan → apply → destroy.

questions.question: Which of the following are advantages of cloud computing over on-premises infrastructure? (Select all that apply)
options: Elastic scalability — scale up/down on demand
Full physical control over all hardware
questions.explanation: Cloud advantages: elasticity, no CapEx, pay-as-you-go OpEx. Cloud latency is NOT guaranteed to be lower (often higher than local DC). Physical hardware control is NOT possible in public cloud — that's a trade-off, not an advantage.

questions.question: Which of the following are components of a SASE (Secure Access Service Edge) architecture? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: SASE combines: SD-WAN, CASB, ZTNA, SWG, and FWaaS (Firewall-as-a-Service). BGP route reflectors are a traditional WAN routing construct — not a SASE component.

questions.question: Which of the following tools are used for Infrastructure as Code (IaC)? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Terraform, Ansible, CloudFormation, and Puppet are all IaC tools. Wireshark is a network packet analyser — not an IaC tool.

questions.question: Order the cloud service models from MOST customer-managed to LEAST customer-managed:
questions.explanation: On-Prem → IaaS → PaaS → SaaS represents increasing provider management responsibility. On-Prem: manage everything. IaaS: OS and up. PaaS: applications only. SaaS: provider manages all layers.

questions.question:
Match each on-premises concept to its cloud equivalent (order the cloud terms to match):
On-prem: Physical Network, Stateful Firewall, Stateless ACL, Leased Line
questions.explanation: Physical Network → VPC; Stateful Firewall → Security Group (stateful, instance-level); Stateless ACL → Network ACL (stateless, subnet-level); Leased Line → Direct Connect (dedicated private physical connection).

questions.question: Order the Terraform workflow steps in the correct sequence:
questions.explanation: Terraform workflow: init (download providers) → validate (syntax check) → plan (dry-run, show changes) → apply (make changes, confirm state) → destroy (when decommissioning). Never skip plan before apply in production.

questions.question: Which cloud characteristic describes the ability to provision and release resources rapidly without manual provider interaction?
questions.explanation: NIST defines 5 essential cloud characteristics: (1) On-demand self-service, (2) Broad network access, (3) Resource pooling, (4) Rapid elasticity, (5) Measured service. On-demand self-service = provision without human provider interaction.

questions.question: What is 'elasticity' in cloud computing?
options: The ability to stretch network cables to reach distant locations
The ability to automatically scale resources up or down to match demand
The compression ratio of cloud storage
questions.explanation: Elasticity allows cloud resources to scale automatically in response to demand. Scale out (add instances) during peak loads, scale in (remove instances) when demand drops — paying only for what you use.

questions.question: What is 'multitenancy' in cloud computing and what security risk does it introduce?
options: Multiple administrators managing a single cloud account — risk of privilege escalation
Multiple customers sharing the same physical infrastructure — risk of data leakage between tenants if hypervisor isolation fails
Multiple cloud providers used simultaneously — risk of data fragmentation
Multiple applications running on a single VM — risk of resource exhaustion
questions.explanation: Multitenancy: many customers share the same physical servers, isolated by the hypervisor. Risk: VM escape or side-channel attacks (Spectre/Meltdown) could leak data between tenants. Mitigated by dedicated instances (bare metal) for sensitive workloads.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the benefit of network automation over manual CLI configuration?
options: Automation allows devices to operate without IOS
Automation enables consistent, repeatable configuration changes at scale with reduced human error
Automation replaces the need for network engineers
Automation only works with virtualised network devices
questions.explanation: Network automation enables consistent configuration across hundreds of devices simultaneously, eliminates typos/human error, provides audit trails, and enables change management at scale — impossible with manual CLI at enterprise scale.

questions.question: What is version control (e.g., Git) used for in network automation?
options: Tracking IOS version upgrades across all devices
Storing, versioning, and collaborating on automation code and configuration templates with rollback capability
Controlling which network engineers can access which devices
Managing software licences for automation tools
questions.explanation: Git provides version history, branching, peer review (pull requests), and rollback for automation code (Ansible playbooks, Terraform configs, Python scripts). Treating network config as code ('GitOps') enables audit trails and safe change management.

questions.question: What is a Jinja2 template used for in network automation?
options: A Python testing framework for validating network configurations
A templating engine for generating device configurations dynamically from variables
A data model language replacing YANG
A REST API client library for Cisco DNA Center
questions.explanation: Jinja2 templates allow you to create config templates with variables (e.g., {{ hostname }}, {{ interface_ip }}). Combined with a data source (YAML/CSV), you generate device-specific configs automatically — used heavily in Ansible and Nornir.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a CI/CD pipeline in network automation?
options: Continuously monitoring network device CPU and memory
Automatically testing and deploying configuration changes through stages (lint → test → stage → production)
Providing a continuous backup of device running-configurations
Continuously discovering new devices added to the network
questions.explanation: CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment) for networks: code change → automated lint/syntax check → automated testing in a lab → staged rollout → production. Catches errors before they hit production, enables rapid safe change delivery.

questions.question: Ansible requires a dedicated agent process running on each managed network device to execute playbooks.
questions.explanation: Ansible is agentless. For network devices, it connects via SSH (or HTTPS for some APIs) with no agent required on the managed device. This is a key differentiator from Puppet and Chef, which require agents.

questions.question: Which NETCONF operation retrieves the current running configuration from a network device?
questions.explanation: <get-config> retrieves configuration data from a specific datastore (running, startup, or candidate). <edit-config> modifies the configuration. <commit> applies candidate config changes. <lock> prevents other sessions from modifying.

questions.question: In RESTCONF (RFC 8040), which HTTP method is used to create a new configuration resource that must not already exist?
questions.explanation: In RESTCONF: POST creates a new subordinate resource (fails if it already exists). PUT creates or replaces a resource entirely. PATCH partially modifies a resource. GET retrieves. This mirrors standard REST semantics.

questions.question: What is the primary role of Cisco DNA Center (Catalyst Center) in an enterprise network?
options: A centralised network management, automation, and analytics platform providing intent-based networking
A BGP route reflector for large-scale deployments
questions.explanation: Cisco DNA Center is an SDN controller providing: network design (topology, addressing), policy (segmentation via SGT), provisioning (zero-touch), and assurance (AI-driven analytics). It uses NETCONF/YANG to configure IOS-XE devices.

questions.question: Which Python library is specifically designed to simplify SSH connections to network devices and handles IOS prompt detection automatically?
questions.explanation: Netmiko is built on top of Paramiko and adds network-device-specific logic like prompt detection, enable mode handling, and config mode management. Paramiko is the lower-level SSH library.

questions.question: In a Netmiko device dictionary, which key specifies the platform type (e.g., 'cisco_ios' or 'arista_eos')?
questions.explanation: The key is 'device_type'. Netmiko uses it to select the correct device handler class. Common values: cisco_ios, cisco_nxos, cisco_asa, arista_eos, juniper_junos.

questions.question: Which Netmiko method sends a show command and returns the device output as a string?
questions.explanation: send_command() is for show/operational commands. It waits for the prompt to return and gives you the output as a string. send_config_set() is for pushing configuration commands.

questions.question: What does Netmiko's send_config_set() method do automatically that send_command() does not?
options: It saves the config to NVRAM
It enters and exits configuration mode
questions.explanation: send_config_set() automatically issues 'configure terminal' before your commands and 'end' afterward, so you just pass a list of config commands without worrying about mode transitions.

questions.question: A Python script calls ConnectHandler(**device) where device is a dict. What does the ** operator do here?
options: Unpacks the dict as keyword arguments
Creates a copy of the dict
Passes the dict as a positional argument
questions.explanation: The ** (double-star) operator unpacks a dictionary into keyword arguments. ConnectHandler(**device) is equivalent to ConnectHandler(device_type='cisco_ios', host='10.0.0.1', ...) — each key becomes a parameter name.

questions.question: A network engineer writes a loop that connects to 50 routers sequentially. Each connection takes ~3 seconds. Approximately how long does the script take?
questions.explanation: Sequential processing: 50 devices × 3 seconds = 150 seconds. To speed this up, use Python's concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor or multiprocessing to connect to multiple devices in parallel.

questions.question: Which exception should be caught when a Netmiko connection times out because the device is unreachable?
questions.explanation: Netmiko raises NetmikoTimeoutException (from netmiko.exceptions) when it can't reach the device within the timeout period. NetmikoAuthenticationException is raised when credentials are wrong.

questions.question: A Netmiko script uses send_command('show ip bgp') and the output is truncated. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: BGP is not running — enable it first
The terminal length is not set to 0 — add expect_string or use send_command with read_timeout
Python can only read 4096 bytes — use a larger buffer
questions.explanation: By default, IOS paginates long output with '--More--'. Netmiko normally sets 'terminal length 0' automatically, but sometimes this fails (e.g., on NX-OS). Fix: specify expect_string=r'#' or increase read_timeout parameter.

questions.question: Which Python module is used to search for patterns in text, such as extracting IP addresses from show command output?
questions.explanation: The 're' (regular expressions) module provides functions like re.search(), re.findall(), and re.match() for pattern matching. It's essential for parsing unstructured CLI output.
